responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954: Lilli Peller (New York).  'Libidinal Phases, Ego Development and Play.'  Pp. 178–198.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Lilli Peller (New York). 'Libidinal Phases, Ego Development and Play.' Pp. 178–198.

This is an attempt to fit the wealth of observational data on children's play into the conceptual framework of psycho-analytic theory. Play is conceived as a form of ego activity attempting to deal with quantitatively low levels of anxiety arising from external pressures and intersystemic conflicts. It has a pleasure-seeking as well as a defensive function and is instrumental in the growth of sublimations.

Four developmental groupings are discriminated according to the main anxieties attached to prevailing phases of object relationship. (I) Anxieties in the early narcissistic cathexis of the body, arising from feelings of powerlessness, are denied and compensated for by body play with hallucinatory gratification and magical motivation. A notable increase in body skill and an active search for body mastery results. (II) Separation anxieties related to the preoedipal aspects of the omnipotent mother relationship lead to an attempt to master loss through play with substitute materials, initiate the ability to bear delay, and so to set up lasting object relations. (III) Anxieties about loss of love in the oedipal situation initiate defensive identificatory phantasies, which are played out plastically with other children and lead to extensive imaginative manipulation of ideas and materials. (IV) Anxieties relating to the superego and superego figures lead to organized group games in which the dissolving oedipal ties find gratifying substitutes in the framework of rules and an organized plot. The more structured play of the older child shows a decrease in overt phantasy consequent on guilt feelings. The highly formalized play of the latency period carries as well deeply unconscious group phantasies congealed into conventional and relatively affectless forms in the course of race history. A similarly defensive mitigation of affect takes place for the adult in the formalized media of the arts, notably the dramatic.

214

responseSet.title: Caricatures of 88 Pioneers in Psychoanalysis: By Olga Székely-Kovacs and Robert Bereny.  (New York:  Basic Books, 1954.  $4.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Caricatures of 88 Pioneers in Psychoanalysis

By Olga Székely-Kovacs and Robert Bereny. (New York: Basic Books, 1954. $4.50.)

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones  

This is a reprint of a collection of caricature sketches made on the occasion of the Eighth International

480

Psycho-Analytical Congress at Salzburg in 1924. Copies of it were privately circulated at the time, but it has now for the first time been made available to the public and has been furnished with a preface and the Presidential Address to the Congress.

There are sketches of eighty-eight psycho-analysts in the book. The general artistic level is high, but some of the drawings are naturally more successful than others in capturing the characteristic elements of the persons concerned. Since I knew them all it might be of some interest if I recorded my judgement of the various sketches. The most successful appear to me to be those of Bernfeld, Boehm, Eitingon, Helene Deutsch, van Emden, Federn, Levy, Pailthorpe, Sachs, James Strachey, Weiss, and Winterstein. Those of Abraham, Alexander, Ferenzci, Hollos, and Otto Rank perhaps err on the cruel side. The least successful are those of Wälder, Hoffer, Landauer, Nunberg, Mira Oberholzer, Pfister, Beate Rank, Reich, Reik, Ella Sharpe, and (in the victim's opinion) myself.

The authors and publishers are to be congratulated on this historically interesting collection.

481

Lewin.  'Dream Psychology and the Analytic Situation.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 2: Bertram D. Lewin.  'Dream Psychology and the Analytic Situation.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 2

Bertram D. Lewin. 'Dream Psychology and the Analytic Situation.'

Lewin continues his attempt to apply the metapsychology of the dream to the analytic situation (Psa. Quarterly, 1954, No. 4). He points out that unconsciously the couch is the place for sleeping, and that this basic analytic situation may need to be interpreted, as for example if sleep is equated with death. In general the narcissistic phenomena of the analytic situation are neglected in interpretation. Rank

202

approached them, but his fallacy was to take at their face value fantasies of the uterine state and rebirth which really referred to sleeping and waking.

Searching for equivalents for components of the analytic situation in The Interpretation of Dreams, he compares the analyst, as a transference-figure, with the indifferent day-residues to which unconscious ideas lend their cathexis. As an interpreter the analyst is to be equated with an external stimulus which threatens to arouse. Lewin also finds in the analyst the opposite number of certain external stimuli which did not interest Freud in connection with the dream—the stimuli which promote sleep. Such stimuli are crooning, the full meal, and the memory of the nursing situation. In so far as the analyst lulls he represents the trace of the pre-oedipal mother and of all the wish-fulfilments leading to sleep. In so far as he wakes he represents the trace of the pre-oedipal father.

Analysis is formed from the wish to free-associate with the object of interpretation as dreams are formed from the wish to sleep, and using the same psychic systems, though in different proportions. The analyst, being a stimulus 'at both ends of the diagram of the psychic apparatus, is "around" the couch as the external world is around the dream'.

203

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 2: Otto E. Sperling.  'A Psychoanalytic Study of Social-Mindedness.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 2

Otto E. Sperling. 'A Psychoanalytic Study of Social-Mindedness.'

Sperling is dissatisfied with previous explanations of social-mindedness. To ascribe it to identification in reaction-formation against sibling-rivalry obscures the question of the origin of the libido behind the identification. He attempts to show from case-material that it may be a deflection on to the siblings of the love for either parent, and therefore be homosexual or heterosexual in origin. The love of siblings serves self-preservation in the family and it must therefore be regarded as anaclitic. However, narcissistic libido may also be involved, and be converted into social-mindedness by the mechanism described as 'appersonation' (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 25, 1944). The love expressed in social-mindedness is not reactive. For Sperling it is true love which cannot come out of hate. But it is transference-love directed to the wider 'abstract' group of people not ordinarily met with face to face, the abstraction favouring tendencies to regression and projection. Many other component instincts and defences may also be involved.

203

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 2

Warren J. Barker. 'The Stereotyped Western Story.'

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

The Western formula is analysed in terms of the oedipal aspirations and guilt of the son hero and its similarities with other myths shown. The various projections of parts of the son's personality on to other characters in the story are traced.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3: E. Eduardo Krapf.  'The Choice of Language in Polyglot Psychoanalysis.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3

E. Eduardo Krapf. 'The Choice of Language in Polyglot Psychoanalysis.'

The patient chooses the language which is calculated to relieve the ego of anxiety caused by the superego. This may be either the language which reinforces repression, or that which weakens the severity of an archaic superego. Either of these purposes may be fulfilled, for instance, by avoiding the intimate language when dealing with the intimate physical relations with the mother. But this defence may also have a more positive value, as when a chosen language represents the deliberate adoption of a more reasonable ego-ideal, and thus provides the ego with a value-determined armoury against a superego determined by the id.

204

'Etiology and Treatment of Sleep Disturbances in Children.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3: Melitta Sperling.  'Etiology and Treatment of Sleep Disturbances in Children.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3

Melitta Sperling. 'Etiology and Treatment of Sleep Disturbances in Children.'

Disturbances of sleep are the first reliable signs of emotional conflict in children, preceding any other overt indication in behaviour. Each phase of development has its own pattern. If the small infant's natural ability to sleep is disturbed, external interference should be suspected. In the second and third years, mild disturbances are common. They are associated with the repression of anal-erotic and aggressive drives. Additional traumata (such as the birth of a sibling, which often induces a mother to speed toilet training) are immediately reflected in them. Particularly important are emotional over-stimulation and seduction, because they prematurely stimulate phallic impulses and thus reinforce anal conflicts and intensify the child's repressive struggles. In the oedipal phase disturbances are also ordinarily mild and temporary. They are typified by occasional nightmares and difficulty in falling asleep. When they are more severe the traumatic agent is the excessive suppression by the parents of the child's sexual interests and jealousy, frequently combined with over-stimulation. Acute suppression of object-directed destructive impulses leads to the formation of a 'sleep-phobia'.

The psychodynamics of neurotic sleep disturbances in children are similar to those of traumatic neuroses. Though chronic insomnia is always serious, treatment is not too difficult if initiated promptly.

204

'Vicissitudes of the Dream Screen and the Isakower Phenomenon.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3: Angel Garma.  'Vicissitudes of the Dream Screen and the Isakower Phenomenon.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3

Angel Garma. 'Vicissitudes of the Dream Screen and the Isakower Phenomenon.'

Garma gives numerous examples to illustrate the manner of linkage of later anxieties with early oral ones to produce the Isakower phenomenon or the visible dream screen—by means of oral regression in the face of a current anxiety, by condensation of fantasies concerning the penis and the breast, etc. Of particular note is the representation in one scene of both the flattened breast seen by the infant and the rounded breast seen by the older child. The rounded breast may be condensed with the breasts or the abdomen of pregnancy, the flattened breast with the abdominal wall. Oscillation between two such sets of fantasies may occur in the same dream. Oral regression may fail as a defence against a later anxiety if the oral frustrations act as a reinforcement of the later ones.

204

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3: Mortimer Ostow.  'A Psychoanalytic Contribution to the Study of Brain Function.  Parts II, The Temporal Lobes, and III, Synthesis.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3

Mortimer Ostow. 'A Psychoanalytic Contribution to the Study of Brain Function. Parts II, The Temporal Lobes, and III, Synthesis.'

The author examines very carefully the anatomical, experimental and clinical evidence on the functions of the temporal lobe. Within it lie structures which have the function of matching percepts with preconscious memories and thus through them with unconscious repressed memories and fantasies. The result of this is the psychic assessment of the value of current percepts in terms of prospective somatic pleasure or pain, which is expressed as an affect. If the affect is a positive one the derivative fantasy is recreated. Such fantasies with their affects are conveyed to the frontal lobe by way of the efferent tracts of the temporal lobe, especially the fornix. This gives the frontal lobe the information it requires to regulate the procession of unconscious fantasies as described in Part 1 (Psa. Quarterly, 1954, No. 3) and thus to enable the individual to pursue his goals in a flexible way with minimum of waste emotion.

204

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3

William G. Barrett. 'On the Naming of Tom Sawyer.'

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

Mark Twain felt himself to be more 'twain', more divided into a good and a bad self, than is usual. The author attempts to show that in the name Tom Sawyer Tom represented the denigrated anal part of the self and Sawyer the approbated part striving for independence.

204

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 4: Simon Rothenberg and Arthur B. Brenner.  'The Number 13 as a Castration Fantasy.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 4

Simon Rothenberg and Arthur B. Brenner. 'The Number 13 as a Castration Fantasy.'

An obsessional neurotic's fear of the number 13 masked castration anxiety caused by his death-wishes towards his wife. The authors cite numerous examples from folk-lore and religion to prove that here too the number 13 represents both the danger of castration and the triumphant phallus, and that the two meanings are closely associated.

205

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1

Intuitive Processes in the Psychotherapy of Schizophrenics

Frieda Fromm-Reichmann. 'Introduction.'

This is a brief communication consisting of an introduction to the papers abstracted below. The four papers were the outcome of a weekly two-hour medical staff discussion group which met at Chestnut Lodge Sanitorium over a period of nine months. The recorded material of patients provided the basis for discussions in which each member of the group commented on his reactions to the records. The aim of the group was to obtain a clearer idea of the factors facilitating or retarding the analyst's intuitive awareness.

206

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1: Harold F. Searles.  'Dependency Processes in the Psychotherapy of Schizophrenia.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1

Harold F. Searles. 'Dependency Processes in the Psychotherapy of Schizophrenia.'

This paper is written in four sections. First, 'Sources of the patient's anxiety about his dependency needs'. Second, 'The patient's way of dealing with dependency needs in therapy'. Thirdly, 'Manifestations of anxiety in the therapist with regard to his own and the patient's dependency needs'. Fourth, 'Therapeutic technique in dealing with the patient's dependency needs'.

In (i) six topics are subjected to detailed discussion. These include (a) the patient's lack of awareness of his dependency needs. Dependency needs alone do not engender anxiety, but these needs plus the defences against them are what is decisive. Hostility is one such defence. (b) Dependency needs are anxiety-provoking because they imply that the other person is indispensable. (c) Anxiety is caused by the patient's dread of taking in harmful things or losing his identity. (d) The object of the dependency needs is regarded as hostile and rejecting. Here the failure of the projection mechanism is paramount. (e) The repressed dependency needs are closely associated with repressed feelings of loneliness. (f) Once dependency needs are recognized, omnipotent phantasies become valueless as a defence.

In (ii) the first topic to be discussed is the patient's projection of his own dependency needs on to the therapist. Second come the patient's competitiveness with and contempt for the therapist. Awe and admiration may serve a similar defensive purpose. A further complication in therapy is that the schizophrenic patient cannot conceive of gratification of his dependency needs in terms of adult satisfactions. The desire for immediate tangible satisfactions soon leads to discontent with the therapist.

In (iii) the therapist's anxiety arising out of his own dependency needs is given attention. The schizophrenic patient tends to provoke the therapist's anxiety more readily than the psychoneurotic. Several pointers are given to the presence of anxiety in the therapist. Such manifestations fall into two groups: (a) the therapist's compulsion to be helpful, and (b) the therapist's failing to hear, or actively discouraging, the patient's expression of dependency needs.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1: Donald D. Burnham.  'Some Problems in Communication with Schizophrenic Patients.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1

Donald D. Burnham. 'Some Problems in Communication with Schizophrenic Patients.'

The speech of the patient who is quoted in this paper was often incoherent and confused. At the outset of therapy the author felt that his main task was to translate the schizophrenic speech into normal talk. His efforts to attain this end resulted in frustration and boredom. At this point the patient's material was discussed at the weekly study seminar mentioned in the Introductory Communication by Frieda Fromm-Reichmann. This resulted in an improvement in the patient-therapist situation. Gradually the patient came to realize that his conception of the therapist as an expert translator of 'schizy talk' was unrealistic.

The obscurity of the patient's verbal communications reflected both his wish and his fear of a close relationship with the therapist. The therapist's task was not to undertake full responsibility for understanding everything the patient said. To do so would prevent independent development on the patient's part. The patient has to be encouraged to participate in the discovery of the hidden meanings in his speech. At times the patient does not know the meaning of what he is saying, but this is not invariably so. The author suggests that any given piece of schizophrenic speech is highly overdetermined. This has an important practical value in that it enables the therapist to throw off the anxiety lest he may not be making the correct translations of the patient's comments.

The remainder of the paper is taken up with examples of patients' remarks illustrating the tendency to regard words as objects, the use of speech to dramatize conflicting self conceptions, and the loss of the capacity to think abstractly.

207

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1

Frieda Fromm-Reichmann. 'Clinical Significance of Intuitive Processes of the Psychoanalyst.'

This paper underlines some of the points made in the previous three papers. Intuitive awareness in the analyst is an aspect of his countertransference experiences and the free use of intuition is hampered by excessive anxiety. An extra source of difficulty is provided for the analyst when he works as a full-time member of a hospital staff. Anxiety arising out of concern for his colleagues' opinions will interfere with the expression of his intuitive powers.

207

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2: Victor H. Rosen.  'The Reconstruction of a Traumatic Childhood Event in a Case of Derealization.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2

Victor H. Rosen. 'The Reconstruction of a Traumatic Childhood Event in a Case of Derealization.'

This case report describes the reconstruction of a severe childhood trauma at about the age of three and a half years in a patient suffering from the symptoms of derealization. The traumatic experience was the witnessing of suicidal attempts on the part of the mother. The symptoms resulted from an identification with the 'hanging mother'. In the discussion the author suggests that the usual mechanism of repression failed and compelled the ego to revert to primitive identification, and that the transition from the pre-oedipal to the oedipal phase is associated not only with a more stable type of identification but also with the development of certain perceptual processes with the separation of phantasy from reality. In the case reported the event which led to the symptoms of derealization occurred during the transition period postulated above, with the result that a reality event became confused with phantasy.

208

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2

Samuel A. Guttmann. 'Bisexuality in Symbolism.'

This short paper presents material to demonstrate the bisexual nature of many symbols. The fact that symbols may have bisexual representation has important consequences for psycho-analytic practice. The analyst must guard against arbitrary judgement of symbols and must utilize the patient's associations if he is to know on what occasions the symbol has a male and when a female significance.

209

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 3: Gert Heilbrunn.  'The Basic Fear.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 3

Gert Heilbrunn. 'The Basic Fear.'

In this paper a considerable amount of evidence is gathered from zoological, anthropological, historical

210

and clinical sources to support the theory that the basic fear is that of being eaten. Castration fear, fear of loss of the breast or the mother are regarded as being defences against the anxiety about being devoured. Two dreams are reported to illustrate the tendency to ward off the basic fear by a preoccupation with castration fears. The danger of being eaten does not emanate exclusively from the mother. It can equally arise from the father but the majority of patients, male and female alike, fear destruction by the mother. The author quotes Szekely's conclusions that the infant reacts with fear to the visual gestalt of the 'two eyes and forehead' pattern (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 35, 1954; see also 36, 1955, 162–166). As it is the mother who is the infant's first object, her face with the 'enemy schema' becomes imprinted upon the baby's memory and releases anxiety. This marks the mother as the primary biological enemy.

211

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954: Paul Kramer, M.D. (Chicago).  'Early Capacity for Orgastic Discharge and Character Formation.'  Pp. 128–141.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Paul Kramer, M.D. (Chicago). 'Early Capacity for Orgastic Discharge and Character Formation.' Pp. 128–141.

A type of behaviour is described marked by restless energy, impulsive unrestraint, and counterphobic attitudes. The endurance and tirelessness of these persons who constantly seek company and physical hazards and are uneasy when alone, is nevertheless forced and artificial. Two cases are cited with the common infancy feature of having passively to observe highly stimulating adult activity, in the one case frankly sexual, and in the other unrestrainedly aggressive. Both children attempted to abreact the enforced stimulation by masturbation, which was essentially unrelieving and additionally stimulating. The search for stimulation in later life is in reality an attempt to find relief through denial of the painfulness of the overstimulated condition.

Reference is made to the stress laid by Anna Freud on the importance of phallic masturbation in the genesis of acting out in children (1949). In contrast to 'acting out characters' who continue to relate to the outside world by seeking to master stimulation counterphobically, other cases presented became submerged in unreal masochistic phantasies. It is suggested that the differentiating factor is in the nature of the traumatic experiences; the former were involved passively as observers, the latter actively in the overwhelming sadistic activities. All cases make a heavy investment of adult energy in concealing feelings of helplessness and shame.

Case material is advanced to support the contention that the greater intensity of castration fear in boys, as well as the greater frequency of the counterphobic character, may be related to a fundamental difference between the sexes to experience relieving orgastic discharge in childhood.

213

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954: Bela Mittlemann, M.D. (New York).  'Motility in Infants, Children and Adults.  Patterning and Psychodynamics.'  Pp. 142–177.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Bela Mittlemann, M.D. (New York). 'Motility in Infants, Children and Adults. Patterning and Psychodynamics.' Pp. 142–177.

Arguments are advanced in support of a 'motor-drive' in the psycho-analytic sense of that term. The dominant period is from early in the second year till the end of latency, during which time it is possible to speak of a motor phase of libido and ego development. The motor activity of the organism has significant connection with almost every emotional striving and every environmental relationship, including the inanimate. Objects impose their qualities on motility which becomes one of the main means of reality testing and ego integration.

During the first year of life, motor patterns are often generalized and rhythmic and show developmental and individual alterations. A regression to earlier generalized motor patterns may occur under stress or in pathological conditions. The rocking habits of blind and institutionalized children are instanced. Sustained restriction of motility during the motor phase may lead to anxiety reactions, and compensatory overactivity and anxiety arising from other sources may interfere with motor activity. In conflict situations motor behaviour gives evidence of compromise formation, substitution, reaction formation, and alternate doing and undoing.

213

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Dr. Augusta Bonnard (London). 'Some Discrepancies between Perception and Affect as Illustrated by Children in Wartime.' Pp. 242–251.

In an earlier article, 'War Trauma in Children, Real or Imaginary' (1951), the author set out her view that reactions of terror in children are less a response to war circumstances than to uncontrolled adult behaviour. In a child of three who fully understood the threat of bombing in a realistic sense, it nevertheless served as a condensed representation of his own and his mother's unrestrained impulses. Disturbed children showed both an understanding and a misconception of the war experiences, preconditioned by earlier infantile conflicts in which faulty object relationships and distorted superego functioning, or both, played a part.

A young girl's publication in a London paper of her wartime memories of taking shelter with her parents now affords an opportunity to study the mechanisms by which she identified in her ego-ideal with the self-mastery of the adults. Analysis of the denials, omissions, and displacements evident in her idealized story reveals an underlying clear perception of the dangerous events and of the hidden though benign motivation of the parents' simulated assurance.

The two sets of material are presented as indicative of differentiating features in the formation of superego and ego-ideal. In the more recent material, 'the adult evinces a power which does not bear upon invidious comparisons or upon threats, but upon the child's desire for empathy. … In so far as the child can emulate the adult in this, its narcissism obtains conflict-free gratification. … Another differentiating feature between these two psychic systems is that whereas in superego systems the element of imposed veto is never missing, direct gratification of the individual's own narcissism always is'.

215

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Ilse Hellman (London). 'Some Observations on Mothers of Children with Intellectual Inhibitions.' Pp. 258–273.

Three cases of pseudoimbecility and learning inhibition are presented whose aetiological similarity may be significant for the syndrome. The mother's exhibition of her naked body and of her anxieties about her own bodily states was common to all the cases. The children's sadistic phantasies and consequent guilt feelings thus became linked with looking and learning. As a condition of the continuance of the close preoedipal relation, the mothers demanded a complete repression of the children's aggression. The mothers confessed to irregular sexual relations which they denied the children knew about. Fathers were either absent or not the object of the mothers' love. These confessions served to hide more guilt-ridden details belonging to earlier phases of the mothers' lives, also unconsciously known to the children. The children identified with the mothers' compulsive lying and deliberate withholding of secrets.

215

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954: Emma N. Plank and Robert Plank (Cleveland).  'Emotional Components in Arithmetical Learning as Seen through Autobiographies.'  Pp. 274–293.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Emma N. Plank and Robert Plank (Cleveland). 'Emotional Components in Arithmetical Learning as Seen through Autobiographies.' Pp. 274–293.

Autobiographies offer material on the development of mathematical interests and abilities in boys and girls. The recollection of a positive attitude towards mathematics in children who do not necessarily become mathematicians emerges in connection with the dissolution of the strong libidinal tie to the mother. When the destructive drive is inhibited, this object relationship cannot be renounced and symbols cannot replace objects. Aggressive energy is not available for the exercise of the control, dissection, and domination required by mathematical learning.

In the childhood of mathematicians there is a relatively great incidence of early loss of parents and other severe childhood deprivations. The computational prodigies display an obsessional element indicative of a weakened mother relationship. They form a bridge to emotionally isolated children with a compulsive interest in numbers. Flight into a world of abstractions replaces satisfying object relations.

Male autobiographers with positive memories of mathematical learning have all succeeded in establishing an independent identity. This may be varyingly rooted in a life lacking relationships or in freely expressed aggression.

In the childhood of women mathematicians, the difficulty of the mother relationship is marked (here in connection with the denial of femininity), while the positive element of identification with a male figure is in the foreground. Men also learn mathematics through men. The authors have tried to explore the problem of why so many of them did not.

The article is profusely illustrated with extracts from famous autobiographies.

216

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954: Edith Buxbaum, Ph.D. (Seattle).  'Technique of Child Therapy—a Critical Evaluation.'  Pp. 297–333.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Edith Buxbaum, Ph.D. (Seattle). 'Technique of Child Therapy—a Critical Evaluation.' Pp. 297–333.

This evaluation of the diverse methods applied in the field of child psychotherapy takes account of a wide range of procedures within and beyond the strictly analytic and relates them to the types of case in which they are effective. Problems are attempted under two heads. The first concerns the type, range, and degree of contact between therapist and parent, in relation to the type of treatment, and the circumstances in which it is undertaken. The second concerns the technical procedure in the therapeutic situation with the child having regard to the nature of the child's disturbance.

In the first category, the nature of the child's and the degree of the parents' disturbance are outstanding factors. 'Children who are developmentally at the height of their oedipal feelings or already in the latency period, who suffer from their disturbance and whose parents are not significantly disturbed themselves, so that they do not interfere with treatment—these children can be treated by themselves with a minimum of contact with and information from the parents.' Conversely the dependence of the preoedipal child on the mother may demand her inclusion in the treatment even to the extent of attending sessions and corroborating interpretation in some cases.

Procedures are considered where the disturbance of child and parent are identical; where the child's attendance aims mainly at a corrective experience and the mother attends mainly for her own therapy; where parental attitudes necessitate the child's removal from home; and where the relative or complete isolation of parent and child from each other is indicated in the interests of therapy.

The article concludes with a survey of child development directed to ascertaining the nature of the functional link between technical procedures in the treatment of the child, and the elements and areas of his disturbance.

216

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954: Augusta Alpert, Ph.D. (New York).  'Observations on the Treatment of Emotionally Disturbed Children in a Therapeutic Centre.'  Pp. 334–343.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Augusta Alpert, Ph.D. (New York). 'Observations on the Treatment of Emotionally Disturbed Children in a Therapeutic Centre.' Pp. 334–343.

The author continues her account of experimental procedures in the therapeutic education of disturbed children in the Nursery of Council Child Development Center (Alpert and Krown, Psychoanal. Study of Child, 8, 1953). Procedures are discussed which aim at helping children whose acute separation anxieties appear in the context of a disturbed mother relation. New corrective identifications are established with the help of personal relationships in the nursery. Procedures are described which meet the difficulty of holding these children together for any type of group experience. A further problem concerns poverty of sublimatory capacity. Sluggishness of libido and weak ego organization predispose these children to adhere indefinitely to a regressive smearing use of paint. Materials serving regressive needs were separated from those with more progressive sublimatory possibilities and the children given more directional help for articulate pictorial presentation. In this way they became able to avoid the seductive influence of the material.

Both experiences give meaning to the children's needs for someone to hold boundaries and set limits for them, and effect a dosage of experience.

216

responseSet.title: The British Journal of Psychology 46, 1955: Frieda Goldman-Eisler.  'Speech—Breathing Activity—A Measure of Tension and Affect during Interviews.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The British Journal of Psychology 46, 1955

Frieda Goldman-Eisler. 'Speech—Breathing Activity—A Measure of Tension and Affect during Interviews.'

The aim of the investigation was to develop indicators of changes in emotional states based on the speech process itself. Previous research had suggested that respiratory behaviour in speech might be a fruitful line to study. A simple method of recording breath intake from sound recordings was discovered. Two measures were derived from speech-breathing activity: the rate of respiration during speech and the output of speech (number of syllables) per expiration. Both of them proved to be highly reliable and sensitive, but the output of speech per expiration was the more constant, as well as more sensitive, of the two. The phonetic and physiological facts of speech production and psychological evidence and theories, in respect of respiratory behaviour, suggested interpretations of the psychological significance of these measures which are in the process of being tested. (Author's summary.)

217

responseSet.title: Dr. John Sheals Partt
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dr. John Sheals Partt

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

Dr. John Pratt died of acute leukaemia on 21 March, 1956, at the age of forty-one. By this early death psycho-analysis has lost a man of unusual intellectual gifts. Educated at Wellington College and Corpus Christi College, Cambridge (where he obtained a swimming Blue), he had for a time considered making a career in the study of animal behaviour, and though he chose medicine and psycho-analysis, interest in the subject continued throughout his life. He took the Cambridge medical degree from St. Bartholomew's Hospital in 1938 and soon afterwards applied to the Institute of Psycho-Analysis for training. He maintained himself during his studies in wartime London (working with Miss Ella Sharpe) by combining them with general practice and forensic medicine. By the time he was elected to Associate Membership in 1945 he had already had two papers—of which he was later not altogether proud—published in the International Journal. He became a full Member in 1951 and participated in the training scheme from 1952. On his death, tribute was paid in the British Society to the high quality of his work on the Training Committee.

He was not, however, a very prominent member of the Society, preferring in general private discussion, in which his opinions were informed with an outstanding metapsychological grasp. When he became ill he decided to put on paper some reflections on Freudian Instinct Theory which combined his biological and metapsychological viewpoints. He wrote them with steady concentration, partly while undergoing blood transfusions, but was unfortunately not able to complete the paper. The courage, consideration for others, and control which he exercised during this illness will be unforgettable.

In spite of his wide culture and knowledge of the world John Pratt was not an easy person to know. In those who did know him he inspired love rather than affection. This was due to the childlike sweetness of his nature, which was incapable of malice or meanness. His criticisms of those who held different opinions could be sharp; what was remarkable was the complete absence of personal animosity and his refusal to exploit personal weaknesses. Such natures are very exposed, and he had to protect himself. He developed a reticence in emotional communication and even certain angularities behind which not everyone could penetrate. Such reticence did not, however, affect his ability to make contact with his patients, who highly appreciated his personality. He was a hard worker and greatly enjoyed his life as an analyst.

responseSet.title: Dr. Endre Almásy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dr. Endre Almásy

Kata Levy

Kata Levy

Kata Levy  

Dr. Endre Almásy, member of the Hungarian Psycho-Analytical Society, died in Budapest on 1 May, 1956, in his fifty-sixth year, after a short illness. He rendered extraordinary services to the Psycho-Analytical Society in days when the existence of the Society was endangered, services which should not be forgotten.

Endre Almásy was born in Lugos, a small country town, then in Hungary, now belonging to Rumania. He obtained his degree at the University of Budapest; worked for years at a State Mental Hospital as psychiatrist; and received his analytical training in the Hungarian Psycho-Analytical Society, of which he became a regular member in 1931. His first published paper appeared in the Ferenczi Memorial Volume, 1933. He soon became a member of the training committee, head of the Psycho-Analytical Clinic, and took over the Presidency of the Society when the danger of Nazism was approaching. In this so-called Horthy era the Jewish population suffered increasingly under economic and social restrictions; the Society diminished in numbers owing to emigration, and the fate of analysis in neighbouring Austria was already sealed. It was then that Dr. Almásy, as one of the few members of non-Jewish descent, tried to keep things working, as far as conditions permitted. He made use of all the resources at his command, even exposing himself to personal danger, as often as the existence of the Society was attacked. After the invasion of Hungary by the Germans his main concern was to save the Society's library, which at that time contained also a great part of Ferenczi's valuable collection.

It was due to his brave stand for the cause of psycho-analysis that the Society as such continued to exist, and could take up work again as soon as the war was over. At that time he felt his mission was ended and laid down the Presidency of the Society, which finally four years later was dissolved. When under the present régime former and even leading members of the Society denied any connection with psycho-analysis, Almásy was the only medical man who did not give up his analytical work.

responseSet.title: Insanity, Art, and Culture: By Francis Reitman.  (New York:  Philosophical Library, 1954.  Pp. 111.  $3.75.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Insanity, Art, and Culture

By Francis Reitman. (New York: Philosophical Library, 1954. Pp. 111. $3.75.)

John Pratt

John Pratt

John Pratt  

The aim of this study as stated by its author is to establish the extent to which the accepted symptomatic features of psychotic paintings can be of universal and hence of possible diagnostic significance, having regard to the fact that the material previously studied in this connexion has been predominantly derived from the psychotics of Western cultural backgrounds. With this end in view Dr. Reitman has obtained both clinical data as well as paintings from the inmates of mental hospitals in New Zealand (Maori), Mexico, East Africa, Egypt, India, Ceylon, and Japan. Problems of evaluation arising out of the varying traditional art forms of different cultures, e.g. the 'overelaboration' characteristic of Indian art that might be regarded as symptomatic, or the 'writing in' in Japanese where calligraphy is still regarded as an art, are naturally much in evidence, and are dealt with in a satisfactory manner.

487

responseSet.title: Social Science in Medicine: By Leo W. Simmons and Harold G. Wolff. (New York:  Russell Sage Foundation, 1954.  Pp. 254.  $3.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Social Science in Medicine

By Leo W. Simmons and Harold G. Wolff. (New York: Russell Sage Foundation, 1954. Pp. 254. $3.50.)

R. Markillie

R. Markillie

R. Markillie  

This is a valuable book, because its matter and its approach should form part of every medical education, an omission which is still far too common. It is the first offering from a project of medical-social collaboration in New York. It reviews the whole field of health and illness and its relation to social structure, and later volumes will take up specific subjects. There is a particularly interesting chapter on hospital practice, something often taken for granted, quite uncritically.

To the psycho-analytic reader its particular thesis of the importance of the social setting in the genesis of illness or as a determinant of the particular response of the patient will not be new, and is already part of his stock-in-trade. In fact it can be said that the authors do not go far enough, for they do not consider sufficiently the unconscious relationships at work in all human contacts and in personality development.

495

responseSet.title: Mythology of the Soul: By H. G. Baynes.  (London:  Routledge and Kegan Paul.  Second Impression, 1955; First Published, 1940.  Pp. 980.  50s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mythology of the Soul

By H. G. Baynes. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul. Second Impression, 1955; First Published, 1940. Pp. 980. 50s.)

S. Lindsay

S. Lindsay

S. Lindsay  

This is an account of the analysis of two borderline schizophrenic patients. Clinical material is largely confined to dreams and drawings, analysed by the 'introverting technique' of Jung, with the help of abundant knowledge of mythology. Erudite and fascinating as this may be, one misses an appreciation of transference dynamics, which is considered now by many to be essential if interpretations are to be mutative in accordance with the scientific laws of mental function.

The individual's myth is defined as a stream of living images enfolding the naïve mind as a river contains a trout, and already flowing like a natural fountain out of the Unconscious 'before the human mind had ever set itself to the labour of thought'. Dr. Baynes believes that the moral of a people abides in their relation to their myth; when this is destroyed by intellectual sophistication or by stagnation in still pools of custom, the virtue of a people departs. He sees such people, like the prodigal son, as prised from their original background and eager to depart from the monotony of traditional patterns, to appraise their birthright from a new and vital standpoint, and thus to progress to fresh creative ways. This is the advantage attributed to the new 'introverting technique' of Jung, whose departure from Freud, the author asserts, brought to an untimely end a collaboration which was full of promise for the development of the new science. One realizes with solemn thought that this departure differed from that of the prodigal son, who returned after seeking pastures new.

Dr. Baynes describes vividly a demonic renegade factor in the schizophrenic which threatens the stability and integrity of consciousness, eavesdropping at every session and trying to poison the work. One feels that solid transference analysis would be the only antidote to this. However, attraction to the myth as a possible return to the golden age of infant fancy is denied, and the monotonous piecemeal interpretations on those lines seem sometimes to be merely reinforcing infantile manic omnipotent tendencies.

Genetics and the Inheritance of Integrated Neurological and Psychiatric Patterns. Proceedings of the Association for Research in Nervous and Mental Disease. December, 1953. Vol. 33.

Edited by D. Hooker and C. C. Hare. (Baltimore: Williams and Wilkins; London: Bailliere, Tindall and Cox, 1954. Pp. 426, with 77 illustrations. 76 s. 6 d.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

In this series of articles, leading American geneticists report on their studies. In recent years it has been generally recognized that even in those types of mental illness in whose origin hereditary factors are known to play an important part, environmental factors are important. This is an area where co-operation between geneticists and psycho-analysts seems to be very promising. So far this has not materialized, but there are several articles in this book which might stimulate such joint research.

496

This is a collection of six talks given on the Third Programme by senior experimental psychologists. They include a chapter on 'experimental psycho-analysts' by the editor. Although brief, the talks give the reader a good idea of some of the problems with which experimental psychology in this country is preoccupied.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Time Distortion in Hypnosis

By Linn F. Cooper and Milton H. Erickson. (Baltimore: Williams and Wilkins; London: Bailliere, Tindall and Cox, 1954. Pp. 192. 31 s. 6 d.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

Time distortion in dreams is well known; a period of seconds or less may be experienced as very much longer and may be crowded with events. The same phenomenon was produced experimentally in the hypnotic state by the first-named of the authors. He distinguished 'experiential' from 'world time', and 'seeming duration' from 'clock reading'. Subjects were trained in producing trance states with time distortions. Then they were assigned definite tasks which they had to hallucinate. The experiments suggested that it was possible, by means of time distortion, for a subject to experience a stipulated number of events in a clock time interval of the experimenter's choosing.

Milto H. Erickson reports on the clinical and therapeutic application of time distortion, which he found to offer a method by which access could be gained to the experiential life of the patient. He succeeded in making the patient re-experience events in very short periods. One of his patients 'had in 20 seconds achieved a sufficiently comprehensive recollection of her life history to be able to see it in meaningful perspective'. When accompanied by catharsis this process appeared of therapeutic value in several cases. The author is cautious in the evaluation of his observations, but he believes 'time therapy' to be worthy of extensive study. It is to be hoped that the remarkable experiments reported in this book will be repeated in many places. Even if their therapeutic use should prove disappointing they deserve most careful study.

497

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Outline of a Comparative Pathology of the Neuroses

By Ludwig Eidelberg. (New York: International Universities Press, 1954. Pp. 263. $4.50.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

The author of this book has long been known as an excellent teacher of psycho-analytic psychiatry. This introduction to the psychopathology of the neuroses confirms and enhances this reputation. Some of his colleagues may perhaps object to Dr. Eidelberg's tendency to schematization, and possibly disagree with some of his schemata, but this kind of didactic presentation is helpful to the student who is easily discouraged by the obscurities of psycho-pathology. Apart from its virtues as an introduction, the book contains a great deal that is of interest to the psycho-analyst and psychiatrist.

497

responseSet.title: The Technique of Psychotherapy: By Lewis R. Wolberg, M. D. (New York:  Grune and Stratton, 1954.  Pp. 869.  $14.75.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Technique of Psychotherapy

By Lewis R. Wolberg, M. D. (New York: Grune and Stratton, 1954. Pp. 869. $14.75.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

The author, who is Associate Professor of Psychiatry at New York Medical College, is Director of the 'Postgraduate Center for Psychotherapy', which is 'a psychotherapeutic agency devoted to helping people in low and moderate income groups'. The Center also serves the teaching of psychotherapy to doctors and apparently, within limits, to clinical psychologists. The author states the arguments for and against the psychotherapeutic rôle of the latter without taking sides, and the book is obviously written for their benefit also. Professor Wolberg wields a fluent pen and makes ample use, for the purpose of illustration, of a large collection of tape recordings of psychotherapeutic sessions. All the methods of psychotherapy used in the United States are described and discussed. Psycho-analysis comes under the heading of Insight Therapy with Reconstructive Goals. Other types of therapy are: Supportive Therapy, Insight Therapy with Reeducative Goals, and Re-educative Therapy. The three various phases of every psychotherapy (beginning, middle, and terminal) are thoroughly discussed and illustrated with representative recordings. The book closes with a case history, i.e. the transcription of a complete recording of the treatment of a patient with pseudo-organic symptoms, consisting of nine sessions, covering nearly one hundred pages. To many psycho-analysts the book will appear superficial and at times irritating.

497

responseSet.title: The Conception of Disease: By Walter Riese.  (New York:  Philosophical Library, 1953.  Pp. 99 + 21.  $3.75.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Conception of Disease

By Walter Riese. (New York: Philosophical Library, 1953. Pp. 99 + 21. $3.75.)

J. Rowley

J. Rowley

J. Rowley  

This book consists of a most learned and scholarly account of the various conceptions of disease throughout the ages, with particular reference to the various factors contributing to the different concepts in each culture. The width and vision of the problem is perhaps high-lighted for the psycho-analyst in Dr. Riese's discussion of disease as an essential concomitant of human development, in the sense of disease being so often a complex expression of our real limitations. It is only when disease is thus accepted that further true development is possible. What is most to be avoided, as the author demonstrates, is any prolonged attempt to deny that we are only what we are.

498

responseSet.title: La Tentative De Suicide (The Suicidal Attempt): By Pierre-B. Schneider.  (Neuchâtel and Paris:  Delachaux and Niestlé S.A., 1954.  Pp. 291.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La Tentative De Suicide (The Suicidal Attempt)

By Pierre-B. Schneider. (Neuchâtel and Paris: Delachaux and Niestlé S.A., 1954. Pp. 291.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

The author calls his monograph a statistical, clinical, psychological and catamnestic study. The last-mentioned aspect is the most important, because it has been sadly neglected by most writers on attempted suicide hitherto. The author has found that only a small minority of those who have attempted suicide finally kill themselves. He expresses the view that suicide research has been much too statistical in the past. Nevertheless, he tends to draw far-reaching conclusions from the available figures concerning suicidal acts. He believes the total of suicides plus attempts among both sexes to be about the same, and the greater incidence of suicide among men to be the expression of a lower vitality of the male. He found the mortality from natural causes to be greater among persons who attempted suicide than in a comparable group of the general population. Although some of Schneider's views are open to doubt this book is a valuable contribution to the literature on attempted suicide.

500

responseSet.title: From Custodial to Therapeutic Patient Care in Mental Hospitals Explorations in Social Treatments: By Milton Greenblatt, Richard G. York and Esther Lucile Brown, in collaboration with Robert W. Hyde.  (New York:  Russell Sage Foundation.  Pp. 497.  Price $5.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Custodial to Therapeutic Patient Care in Mental Hospitals Explorations in Social Treatments

By Milton Greenblatt, Richard G. York and Esther Lucile Brown, in collaboration with Robert W. Hyde. (New York: Russell Sage Foundation. Pp. 497. Price $5.00.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

We are witnessing in Britain and in America a dramatic change within the mental hospital. Many features of the behaviour disorders of psychotic patients have been recognized as institutional artefacts and amenable to environmental influence. It has also been acknowledged that the recent trend in the mental hospitals is only a reversal to the doctrines of the psychiatric pioneers of the first half of the nineteenth century. Psycho-analysis has had a large share in initiating this process, mainly by breaking down the barriers which tend to separate the psychotic from the so-called normal population. This book gives an account of the changes in the treatment of patients which have taken place at the Boston Psychopathic Hospital within the last ten years. It also includes reports on allied experiments in two mental hospitals.

The orientation of the authors is chiefly clinical and sociological. Their observations confirm those made by Stanton and Schwartz in the more circumscribed set-up of a psycho-analytically orientated small private hospital. The authors view the mental hospital as a community every part of which has to participate in the therapeutic process of resocialization. This implies the careful study of interpersonal relations and of group processes. The Boston Psychopathic Hospital has established a 'patients' government' into which both the constructive and destructive energies of the patient-members of the community are channelled. The rôle of the nurses in the treatment process is receiving careful attention and the therapeutic potential of the personnel is fully utilized. Psychodrama has been found highly effective in social treatment. The reports from the other two mental hospitals illustrate some of the difficulties which the new approach is bound to encounter in non-teaching hospitals where the spirit of experiment is less alive and where too few doctors have to look after far too many patients. This book deserves to be studied in detail by psychiatrists and nurses working in mental hospitals. It will not only teach them a great deal, but also heighten their morale and thus contribute to their patients' progress.

[[RunningHead]]

A Handbook of Medical Hypnosis an Introduction for Practitioners and Students

By Gordon Ambrose and George Newbold. (London: Bailliere, Tindall and Cox. Pp. xiv + 256. Price 21 s.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

Hypnosis in Asthma. By A. Philip Magonet, M.D. (London: Heinemann Medical Books, 1955. Pp. 95. Price 7 s. 6 d.)

The first is a very unsophisticated book with a tendency to overstating the therapeutic usefulness of hypnosis which is recommended mainly for facilitating abreaction. Direct hypnotic suggestion is used only for relief of tension and rarely for removal of symptoms. The book contains nothing new and could have been written at the time when Freud used hypnosis and found it wanting. Some general practitioners will find useful hints here, while others might feel that it fails to do justice to the complexity of the method and to the capriciousness of its results.

The little book on asthma seems to be addressed to the general reader, probably also to patients undergoing hypnotherapy. Dr. Tredgold, who writes a foreword, sounds a word of caution. One can only hope that the numerous patients about whom the author reports maintained their improvement or recovery beyond the periods during which they were followed up, and that other workers will confirm 'the tremendous value of hypnotherapy in the treatment of bronchial asthma'. The author is convinced that he cures his patients through giving them intellectual understanding by a short course of hypno-analysis.

501

responseSet.title: The Nature of Hypnosis: By Paul Schilder.  Translated by Gerda Corvin.  (New York:  International Universities Press, 1956.  Pp. 204.  Price $4.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Nature of Hypnosis

By Paul Schilder. Translated by Gerda Corvin. (New York: International Universities Press, 1956. Pp. 204. Price $4.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

The present experimental trend in psychiatry is no doubt responsible for the revival of the interest in hypnosis. This makes the reappearance of Schilder's writings on this subject all the more welcome. This volume contains a short monograph on the nature of hypnosis first published in 1921, and the Textbook of Hypnosis, written jointly with Kauders. It is striking how much of Schilder's writings has preserved its freshness and topicality. It is fair to say that no new ideas of importance concerning hypnosis have been advanced since the appearance of these studies. It would be highly desirable that some at least of his interesting experiments should be repeated to-day, if possible with greater sophistication. There is another reason for welcoming the appearance of these papers in an English translation: they will save the interesting concepts of Landauer, Ferenczi, and others concerning hypnosis from oblivion. The translation is excellent.

501

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Destiny and Motivation in Language. Studies in Psycholinguistic and Glossodynamics

By A. A. Roback. (Cambridge, Mass.: Sci-Art Publishers, 1954. Pp. 474. Price $8.50.

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This book is a plea for the study of psychological factors in the evolution of language, an approach which seems badly neglected by linguists. Language is fashioned by the needs and aspirations of men. Words and phrases keep up a constant struggle for existence. The author is particularly interested in glossodynamics, i.e. the psychology of language from a motivational angle, either on the level of sound or of meaning or of grammar, and believes that there is room for a 'metaglottology'. Some of Freud's observations concerning language are used as illustrations. In a chapter entitled 'Destiny in Names' he deals with what psycho-analysts have called 'the determining power of the name', and demonstrates the ways in which repression obscures the original sexual origin of words and phrases. Certain linguistic formations owe their existence to oral regression where the underlying libidinal tendencies had been most repressed by spiritual injunction. The author believes that language affords a deep insight into the minds of its creators and he attempts to demonstrate the ways in which national mentality is reflected in language. An interesting chapter deals with the development of slang and its emphasis on sexual factors. The author is critical of attempts made by some psycho-analysts to use linguistics to bolster up psycho-analytic propositions, but he believes that psycho-analysis may throw light on taboo words, on the rôle of repression in the disappearance of words, on euphemisms and certain metaphors. The struggle between words reflects conflicts in the unconscious. This book is of considerable interest to the psycho-analyst, who is frequently faced with linguistic problems. To the student of language it opens up a new and fundamental approach.

502

<artinfo arttype="ART" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ART" id="IJP.037.0034A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1956</artyear><artvol actual="37">37</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">34-35</artpgrg><arttitle>The Inception of 'Totem and Taboo'<ftnx r="F001" type="PG">1</ftnx></arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Jones, Ernest" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Ernest</nfirst><nlast>Jones</nlast></aut>
</artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Inception of 'Totem and Taboo'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Inception of 'Totem and Taboo'11

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones  

Freud was very seldom satisfied with his literary productions. Over and over again he made the most disparaging remarks about them, even about those of which we think so highly, such as The Ego and The Id or Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety, etc. The only two he really enjoyed writing were the little books on Gradiva and Leonardo: of the latter he said once: 'It is the only pretty thing I have ever written.' But there were three things of which he thought highly, and by which he evidently would have wished to be remembered. They were the last chapter of the Interpretation of Dreams, the last chapter of Totem and Taboo, and his essay on the Unconscious written during the war. These are the only ones with which I have ever known Freud express any satisfaction. They would altogether make a slim volume of 183 pages, a very precious selection from the massive collection of his works. To make the story complete I should add that there were two other little books of which Freud thought well, one that falls before and one after his strictly psycho-analytical period. The former was that on Aphasia, the latter Beyond the Pleasure Principle.

The three writings which, following Freud, we might label as 'good', were all written at breakneck speed, sometimes in an almost dreamlike trance. About the validity of two of them, the first and third, Freud never had any doubts, either at the time or later. This was otherwise with the second one, the one with which we are here concerned.

Freud had found the writing of the first three sections of the Totem book extremely boring, and he had to force himself to compose them. But they were necessary as leading up to the main theme, which he had already in his mind. Of the first section he said: 'It is the most lifeless thing I have ever written and only excusable by my being a tyro and for the sake of the taboo essay that is to follow.' He had been wading through the literature on the subject for eighteen months, and the only part of it he liked was Robertson Smith's book The Religion of the Semites: he told me that reading it was like gliding in a gondola. Mostly, however, the material was dull enough. After three months of reading he wrote to Ferenczi: 'The totem work is a beastly business. I am reading thick books without being really interested in them since I already know the results: my instinct tells me that … I feel as if I had intended only to start a little liaison and then discovered that at my time of life I have to marry a new wife.' When he was writing the section on Magic he wrote: 'Wundt makes me furious. To have to read such balderdash after eleven hours at analysis is a hard punishment.

[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1

Mark Kanzer. 'Gogol: A Study on Wit and Paranoia.'

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman  

In a previous paper the author investigated the process of diminution of social tension and unrest as a consequence of spontaneous and organized forms of comedy. In this work he draws attention to the fact that revolutionary movements have been foreshadowed in three different countries by the appearance of a great humorist on the national scene—Cervantes in Spain, Voltaire in France, and Gogol in Russia. This phenomenon may be due to an interplay between paranoid traits in the individual and elements of discontent in the population. This interplay between Gogol's wit and his paranoia set against a background of social tension is the subject of the paper.

The three-person situation which Freud formulated in his work on Wit can be seen as a repetition of the Oedipus situation. The son, as the narrator of the story, tries to outmanoeuvre the father by either impersonating the father himself or by pretending to be impotent and rejected by the mother. Two of Gogol's works are quoted to illustrate this process at work and its rôle in Gogol's humour. By identification Gogol's audience could permit the liberation of anti-authoritarian (anti-father) feelings. The encouragement which Gogol received from the public facilitated the appearance of his megalomanic tendencies. This resulted in a wave of hostility against Gogol with his resulting decline into psychosis. The final part of the paper is concerned with the author's views on wit and paranoia and the rôle of humour in permitting the release of repressed impulses.

207

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 3: L. Bryce Boyer.  'Christmas "Neurosis".'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 3

L. Bryce Boyer. 'Christmas "Neurosis".'

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman  

This paper is in three parts—first, a review of psycho-analytic literature dealing with Christmas; second, a number of case studies and third, a discussion. The author presents the theory that the Christmas festival stimulates sibling rivalries with a predominantly oral colouring. For such patients the birth of Christ symbolizes the newly born sibling with whom they were unable to compete successfully. Such emotional problems result in depressive reactions. Unconscious wishes to obtain the penis by means of which they could obtain the mother's love were uniformly present in the cases described. The author suggests that all patients, whatever the clinical diagnosis, who suffer from Christmas depression will be found to present a similar psycho-pathology.

211

[[RunningHead]]

Problems of Consciousness Two Volumes: Transactions of the Fourth (1953) and Fifth (1954) Conferences

Edited by Harold A. Abramson. (New York: Josiah Macy, Jr., Foundation, 1954, pp. 177, $3.25; 1955, pp. 180, $3.50.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

These volumes report the last two multiprofessional conferences sponsored by the Josiah Macy, Jr., Foundation and held at yearly intervals at Princeton, N.J., under the Chairmanship of Dr. Roy R. Grinker, of the Institute of Psychosomatic and Psychiatric Research and Training, Michael Reese Hospital, Chicago, Ill. They represent 'an attempt to stimulate research and to promote effective communication across the departmental walls which tend to isolate the professions and specialities from one another.' The twenty-two Members include representatives of anatomy, experimental biology, physiology, medicine and psychiatry, psychology, sociology and psycho-analysis (Grete L. Bibring, David Rapaport, Gregory Zilboorg).

Volume IV opens with a review by the Chairman of the three previous Conferences. Bearing in mind the difficulties of definition and the diversity of terminology and frames of reference, he says, 'In my opinion if this conference can accomplish anything, it can establish the knowable frames of reference from which consciousness may be observed, described and interfered with' (p. 18). Not unnaturally, the Conference was continually beset by 'difficulties in communication'. However, as the 'Closing Remarks' at the end of Volume V indicate, the participants did not feel that their time had been wasted in spite of the fact that no very positive results emerged. One Member's judgement of the Conferences is probably very accurate: '… I think we have got a tremendous amount from them. I think we have gained more from them than anyone will get from reading the transactions. In my opinion those volumes are simply not representative of the interchange of ideas that we have experienced. I know they are our words. I know they report what we said. However, what we, as a group, have got from these conferences does not, I think, emerge for an outside reader. It is all between the lines' (Mary A. B. Brazier, p. 158).

499

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Annual Survey of Psychoanalysis. A Comprehensive Survey of Current Psychoanalytic Theory and Practice Volume II, 1951

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer  

Edited by John Frosch, M.D., and Nathaniel Ross, M.D., Sidney Tarachow, M.D., Jacob A. Arlow, M.D. (New York, N.Y.: International Universities Press, Inc., 1954. Pp. 724. $10.000. London: Allen and Unwin.)

The Annual Survey of Psychoanalysis. A Comprehensive Survey of current Psychoanalytic Theory and Practice. Volume III, 1952. Edited by John Frosch, M.D., and Nathaniel Ross, M.D., Sidney Tarachow, M.D., Jacob A. Arlow, M.D. (New York, N.Y.: International Universities Press, Inc., 1956. Pp. 682. $10.00.)

The undertaking of Dr. John Frosch and his co-editors to produce an Annual Survey of Psycho-analysis, of which within four years three volumes have been made available, must be called gigantic. The interval of a few years only between the publication of the original articles or books—some of them in French or Spanish—and their final publication in the form of a survey is reasonably brief. The vast space demanded by editors and publishers for the immense work of assessing, selecting, abstracting, grouping under appropriate headings, for the writing of an introduction to each chapter and a summary as well, the time necessary for the final checking, editing, and consultation among the editorial committee—now enlarged to fifteen members; all these factors do not seem to have affected adversely the final result. Those workers in our field who may have hoped to find in the Survey a substitute for our periodicals which will dispense them from the task and at times the pleasure of reading the current literature of psycho-analysis will be disappointed; for them an interval of between two and three years will still be too long to make them feel up to date in reading our publications. The Survey is not meant to be a 'digest' for early and hurried consumption.

Both volumes under review have turned out richer in content than the first, reviewed in vol. 34 (1953), pp. 156–158, of this Journal. The number of articles abstracted amounts to well over 250, a figure in itself suggesting that there can hardly be a reader who can claim to combine a full analytic practice with the reading of all the originals. By offering itself as an aid to our memory and a signpost to the relevant literature, the Survey will always have its place in our study.

responseSet.title: The Meaning and Genesis of Fetishism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Meaning and Genesis of Fetishism11

Angel Garma

Angel Garma

Angel Garma
BUENOS AIRES

Angel Garma  

Fetishism has been psycho-analytically studied by R. Bak, M. Balint, P. Bergman, M. Boss, O. Fenichel, S. Freud, P. Greenacre, W. H. Gillespie, J. Glover, D. Hunter, E. Kronold, S. Lorand, S. Payne, M. Romm, R. Sterba, H. von Hug-Hellmuth and M. Wulff with results of great value. The exposition of the present case will therefore be limited to an attempt at determining the importance of pregenital positions in the genesis of fetishism and the meaning of the fantasy of the feminine genital organ with a penis in relation to castration-anxiety.

The case is that of a man whose libido oscillated between fetishistic practices and others previous to his fetishism, a fact which made it relatively easy to discover psycho-analytically the reason for his perversion.

Owing to professional discretion the exposition of the patient's psychic mechanism is possible only in a very general way.

His fetishism, which may be traced back to the age of 5, consisted in practices of caressing the feet, and at times the legs as well, of various people, usually women, and of smelling their stockings and shoes, with great genital excitement, which he often satisfied by masturbating.

This man's fetishism may be understood in the light of his instinctive development. He developed a very intense castration-anxiety as regards the feminine genital organ. He considered it as a wound, through which a woman could empty herself out. Through his submission to women, whom he supposed phallic, he used to perform coitus always in doubt as to who really possessed the penis. He also thought the feminine genital organ was toothed and liable to bite.

This man's genitality underwent a regression to anal and oral-digestive instinctivity. The onset of his interest in excrementitious matters dates from his early infancy, and is clearly connected with the weaning trauma. In this interest in excrementitious matters this man masochistically satisfied instinctive wishes of various kinds. Above all it was in some way a substitute for his genital interest. He regarded stool as the woman's 'organ', i.e. as her vulva or her anus as well as the female penis, for in coitus a tergo he thought his own penis was the woman's stool. The 'jet of urine', which he remarked was stronger in women than in men, also stood for the female penis. In his interest in excrementitious matter he also sought to satisfy forbidden oral-digestive wishes.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Richard Sterba</cs></ln><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Editha Sterba</cs></ln><ln>DETROIT, MICH.</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Anxieties of Michelangelo Buonarroti
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Anxieties of Michelangelo Buonarroti11

Richard Sterba and Editha Sterba

Editha Sterba

Richard Sterba
Editha Sterba
DETROIT, MICH.

Editha Sterba  

In the triad of Florentines in which the High Renaissance culminated, Michelangelo stands out as not only the greatest and most revolutionary artist but also the most unruly, enigmatic, and contradictory character. His personality contrasts so greatly with the charm of Raphael and the noble reserve of Leonardo da Vinci. Even his most idealizing biographers have to admit that he was violent, ill-tempered, uncontrolled, stubborn, and irascible; a misanthrope, suspicious even of his best friends, and full of contempt for his rivals and adversaries, ever ready to hurl at them invectives, mockery, and insults. His employers, three powerful Popes among them, had to handle him with utter care and caution. Pepe Leo X summed it up when he said: 'Michelagniolo è impossible e non si può praticar con lui' (1) (Michelangelo is impossible and one cannot deal with him). When his contemporaries called his outstanding feature 'terribilità' (2) they referred not only to the spirit-quelling force of his works of art, but also to his terror-inspiring personality. From this description we can safely conclude that one of the outstanding dynámic factors in that personality was aggression.

In our imagination, based on the effect of his creations, Michelangelo looms like a powerful giant who deserves the title 'The Titan'. In reality he was under average size, frail and sickly, though with muscular arms and shoulders and the hands of a hard-working labourer; he had an almost ugly, mutilated face with suspicious eyes and a mixed expression of meanness and misery. His garments were worn and dirty. We can well say that his appearance betrayed the tempestuous conflicts in his character. What we know of his behaviour reveals that he had the greatest difficulties in dealing with his aggression. He had to use all means of defence against being overwhelmed by it, and his efforts were not always successful. To achieve control of aggression was an ideal which he was able to reach only in the realm of artistic creation.
