responseSet.title: The Rebirth Motif in Homosexuality and its Teleological Significance
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Rebirth Motif in Homosexuality and its Teleological Significance11

I. Glauber M.D.

I. Glauber

I. Peter Glauber, M.D.
NEW YORK

I. Glauber  

This paper represents a study of an additional syndrome in a series of several previously reported. All derive from the incomplete dissolution of, or fixation upon, the primary identification of the mother-child symbiosis; and may coexist in the same patient, as might be expected in view of their common roots. Several factors are common to all the syndromes. There are distortions in ego functioning, especially in identification. Prominent are multiple part-object identifications with breast and phallus of the phallic mother and with her castrated body image. These part-self images are cathected predominantly by destructive aggression and have passive incorporative aims in relation to the narcissistic ideal image of a total-self—the phallic mother. Noteworthy is failure in accomplishing the final phase of identification—assimilation. Attempted transitions from the self-image of an organ to that of a total organism are not felt as a gradual maturational process in terms of accretion and differentiation but rather are felt (and feared) as sudden, cataclysmic emergencies into a new and definitive state, strongly suggestive of the process of birth, in effect—rebirth.

Keeping these common factors in mind, I deem it useful to include here a very brief recapitulation of the theme of rebirth as it appears in the previous studies, in each of which it has a different form and significance. Incidentally, the term motif in the title was chosen because it is more generic than fantasy, inasmuch as the latter, though clear in some cases, is in other cases more obscure and less recognizable by the patient as such.

In working through aspects of passivity with patients who stuttered (7), (8) several dwelt on the difficulties and futility of changing to a more active role. They indulged in wishful thinking akin to the proverbial jumping out of one's skin and becoming someone completely different. It recalled to mind the remark of another observer (13) about 'patients who wish for a trade-in operation instead of a repair job'. Inevitably there was much concern about the fate of the discarded self. One patient acted this out by recording his stuttering speech on a tape recorder. Then while listening to the feedback he was able to speak quite fluently 'correcting' the stutterer's speech. Thus externalization of his old self, while yet keeping it within earshot, was essential before he could be 'reborn' in identification with his therapist. The same patient, in a similar context, stated that what he really needed was an earthquake, implying a rebirth wish as well as a wish for an induction with a higher degree of active energy.

responseSet.title: The Psychiatrist and the Dying Patient: By K. R. Eissler.  (New York:  International Universities Press, 1955.  Pp. xiii + 338.  $5.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychiatrist and the Dying Patient

By K. R. Eissler. (New York: International Universities Press, 1955. Pp. xiii + 338. $5.00.)

H. Abraham

H. Abraham

H. Abraham  

In the Introduction to his book, the author suggests that it is not necessary to read the essays contained in the first part in order to understand the case histories and the conclusions drawn from them. In the reviewer's opinion, however, these essays are well worth the effort needed to follow the author's detailed and searching enquiries into his theme. They discuss, among other topics, the philosophical definitions of death; Freud's thanatology, which the author fully accepts, and which to many of us appears more credible than the Kleinian postulate of violent self-destructive drives operative almost from birth; the moment of death; and the question whether curiosity about death precedes the oedipal phase. Unfortunately, for reasons of space, it is not possible in this review to give each essay the attention it deserves, but two of them (Nos. 9 and 17) have been selected as examples of the way in which Dr. Eissler treats his subject—that of a true scientist enquiring into all aspects of his material.

The ninth essay starts with the statement that death is the only absolutely predictable fact, and because it is certain it need not be imagined. In teleological thinking, one might say that the ability to imagine one's death might destroy the ego's chief function, to serve as a barrier against the death instinct. Sleep, too, is unimaginable, but it is experienced before the ego has developed, and it may undergo difficulties during this development. 'Only the certainty that the ego will find the world of objects again makes sleep a pleasurable process.' Uncertainty in this respect causes severe sleeping disturbances. In this connexion the reviewer would like to draw attention to the book The Measure of My Days by the South African author, Sarah Gertrude Millin. This is partly an autobiographical study, in which a bitter complaint about her terrible and intractable insomnia is constantly reiterated by the writer. This insomnia had been present since childhood, and she had consulted many doctors about it, including a psycho-analyst. It is, however, quite apparent that she never had psycho-analytical treatment. The reason for her suffering, which she finds incomprehensible, becomes quite clear in her remark that to die during sleep—a mode of death so desirable to most people—holds for her a particular horror which she can neither face nor overcome.

<autaff><addr><ln>F. Pasche</ln><ln>M. Renard</ln><ln>PARIS</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Reality of the Object and Economic Point of View
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Reality of the Object and Economic Point of View11

F. Pasche and M. Renard

M. Renard

F. Pasche
M. Renard
PARIS

M. Renard  

Freud's work may seem baffling inasmuch as certain of its elements appear to have been inspired from almost mystical sources—particularly when he postulates that we retain the memory of primeval events and images, such as the primal scene, parricide, images of castrating and devouring parents, and of animals predatory upon man. (We think it equally necessary to postulate, as in animal psychology, the existence in our memory of patterns of satisfying objects, i.e. parents as protectors, givers of food, sexual partners, etc.) On the other hand those postulates which deal with the economic distribution of instinctual drives, or the quantity of instinctual energy, seem to reveal basically mechanistic convictions. In this short paper our aim is to justify the coexistence of these two concepts in psycho-analytic theory, and to demonstrate that, widely different as they are, they are structurally interdependent. It seems to us that many of Freud's successors, by not retaining the two concepts at the same time, have limited and distorted their theoretical outlook, even if the value of their clinical contributions has not been thereby diminished.

The psycho-analyst must faithfully record all aspects of psychic reality. For example, to reduce this simply to the subject himself without any reference to the real external object is to distort reality. Is not this exactly what happens, however, if the object is presented, more or less implicitly, as an emanation, a secretion or 'projection' of the subject? For this reason we feel it is important to demonstrate that the concept of atavistic memory answers to the necessity of placing the subject, as it were, simultaneously face to face with the object, right from the beginning.

If instincts are hereditary, so also is the memory of certain 'impressions from the outside' (Freud: Moses and Monotheism), and perhaps even certain events which in the distant past placed us in crucial contact with our relatives or with enemies of our species. The persistence of these impressions in a potential form for which we have no mental image, but which is certainly not that of an actual psychic presence, is the very basis of our relations with others. It is like the imprint or seal of the external world upon us, the form which moulds all our subsequent ideas. If we reject this hypothesis, and in doing so (as far as it is concerned) we are being more rigorous than the least imaginative of the writers on animal psychology, it seems to us that we lock the subject up forever within his own narcissistic world. No matter how vigorously and frequently he may make projections, he is condemned to create nothing other than object-phantoms. At the same time relationships with others, cathected for their own sake, are not accounted for, nor are those relationships with the persons who play a primary role in the subject's bodily and psychic development during the first months of life, and which come into existence prior to the establishment of a differentiated ego. Thus we find ourselves led to impose upon the personality from birth a rigid and sealed mould within which many things may happen, but to which nothing can come from the outside.

Loewald.  'Hypnoid State, Repression, Abreaction, and Recollection.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2: Hans W. Loewald.  'Hypnoid State, Repression, Abreaction, and Recollection.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2

Hans W. Loewald. 'Hypnoid State, Repression, Abreaction, and Recollection.'

This paper consists of a re-examination of Freud's early ideas concerning the mechanisms of hysterical phenomena with particular emphasis on the relationship between the concept of the hypnoid state and repression. Although Freud gave up the theory of the hypnoid state in favour of the concept of defence, the former nevertheless reappeared in later formulations concerned with the importance of sexuality in the neuroses. It is suggested that an intimate association exists between the psychological character of the hypnoid state and the 'infantile condition of the psychic functions'. Loewald points out that in neither instance will a traumatic experience evoke a psychological reaction. If sexual stimulation occurs during the period of psychosexual immaturity no associative absorption of the event is possible, but a memory trace remains which can be reactivated later when maturity is reached. The memory trace will exert its influence on the way the new stimulus is experienced. The recognition of infantile sexuality does not contradict this hypothesis which sees a similarity between psycho-sexual immaturity and the hypnoid state.

The author believes that the hypnoid state is comparable with the psychological state of early childhood. Once the hypnoid state is freed from its connotation of an abnormal mental condition it can be seen as equivalent to the ego states appropriate to the period of infantile sexuality. The two states have in common the inability to absorb instinctual experiences associatively and the tendency to register traumatic incidents as unconscious memory traces. Repression and hypnoid state are thought of as being the basic elements in hysterical mechanisms. The phenomenon of repression cannot be adequately understood without recognizing that it represents a highly developed version of a primitive type of psychical functioning. In early childhood the undeveloped condition of the mental apparatus makes it impossible for traumatic or other vivid experiences to achieve conscious or preconscious integration. Their revival by means of abreaction, through recollection, affective discharge, and verbalization is beyond and deeper than the resolution of repression. There is a lifting up of unconscious material (the memory traces of the traumata) and a transformation of the primary process into the secondary process. The tendency for the unconscious to relinquish its hold upon memory traces constantly occurs in psycho-analyses during periods of working through.

responseSet.title: Dr. Henri Flournoy Geneva, 1886–1955
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dr. Henri Flournoy Geneva, 1886–195511

The Swiss psycho-analytic movement has recently suffered heavy losses; the death of Charles Odier in 1954 was followed last spring by that of Dr. Henri Flournoy, who was among the first psycho-analysts in French Switzerland and one of those who were most attentively heard at out meetings.

Henri Flournoy was the son of Théodore Flournoy, one of the first Swiss psychologists to take an interest in Freud's discoveries. He prepared himself for psychiatric practice by an exceedingly complete and varied range of studies, not limited to medical psychiatry, but extending from the outset to a wide field of knowledge, including psychology, philosophy, anthropology, and biology. Apart from the periods of instruction passed at the usual psychiatric institutions, he spent a year at the Johns Hopkins Hospital at Baltimore, where he broadened and enriched his experience of psychological psychiatry in contact with Adolphe Meyer, who made a deep impact on him and to whom he used frequently to pay tribute. Moreover, he prepared himself for psycho-analytic practice with Ophuijsen in Holland, subsequently with Freud, and finally with Nunberg in Vienna.

Apart from this academic preparation, Flournoy broadened his horizon by foreign travel, notably in China, Japan, and India, and by enlisting in a Swiss Red Cross unit during the Balkan war of 1912–13.

From 1915 onwards he practised psychiatry, and more particularly psycho-analysis, in Geneva, his native town. His constant aim, for which his training as neurologist, psychiatrist, and psycho-analyst was a perfect preparation, was the synthesis of the psychological and the organic; his whole career was distinguished by this search for harmony between the different methods which aim at knowledge of the human mind as an indissociable whole.

Without enlarging on the very human psychotherapeutic gifts which Flournoy put at his patients' disposal, we would draw attention to the extent to which he enabled others to profit from the work done in his consulting-room. The list of his publications is impressive; it includes 143 articles published in the medical or 'lay' press or collected in volume form. All his writings are distinguished by great objectivity and, when an element of controversy is present, by great serenity and courtesy. In recent years the controversy on the subject of medical abortion which he did not think it his duty to avoid was from this point of view quite remarkable; it made us very much alive to the extent of the development of Flournoy's social sense, as well as his sense of the scientist's responsibility in relation to human prejudices and injustices.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 1: Jacob A. Arlow.  'Notes on Oral Symbolism.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 1

Jacob A. Arlow. 'Notes on Oral Symbolism.'

Arlow points to the oral roots (as opposed to the roots in other levels) of the symbolism of fire, of tools, and of the fantasy of being crushed. Fire is intimately associated with active and passive fantasies of devouring. Tools are the extension of the body, and when they are employed to express similar fantasies they represent the teeth. The fantasy of being crushed may be derived from the wish to be eaten, swallowed, and thus reunited with the mother.

202

'On Weeping.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 2: Gert Heilbrunn.  'On Weeping.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 2

Gert Heilbrunn. 'On Weeping.'

The power of tears to wash away irritants in the eye is symbolically extended to all parts of the physical and psychic structure. Weeping is thus a defence against narcissistic injury, and as a defence its aim is regressive. Since it appears in the fourth post-natal week its goal must be a retreat to the amnion, the fluid of which closely resembles tears in composition. Heilbrunn speculates that tears are not shed during the first four weeks of life because sleep affords sufficient possibilities for regression before cerebration is stimulated by the exigencies of life (and also before the lachrymal glands have further developed). Weeping may become pathological when a decisive infantile trauma renders the patient hypersensitive to later narcissistic injuries.

203

Kolb and Adelaide M. Johnson.  Aetiology and Therapy of Overt Homosexuality.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 4: Lawrence C. Kolb and Adelaide M. Johnson.  Aetiology and Therapy of Overt Homosexuality.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 4

Lawrence C. Kolb and Adelaide M. Johnson. Aetiology and Therapy of Overt Homosexuality.'

This is a further study by Johnson and her coworkers of the rôle of unconscious instigation by the parents in the acting out of their children. Four cases of adult male homosexuality are described. The patients' mothers had behaved to them in an over-seductive way, at the same time frustrating their heterosexual drives and sanctioning homosexual ones. The fathers had failed to protect their sons against their mother, often partly owing to absence from home. The main aetiological agent was a hostile identification with the mother.

In treatment the therapist's passivity may be interpreted by such patients as a repetition of the mother's permission for homosexuality. When it is clear that the parent's seductiveness encouraged the patient, and a strong positive transference has been obtained, the therapist should forbid homosexual activity on pain of termination of treatment, explaining that the mother's acquiescence in this relief of tension cannot be repeated. This causes the patient to face the anxiety aroused by his hostility to the hated mother and allows the analyst to resolve it by interpretation in the transference.

205

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1: Edward D. Hoedemaker.  'The Therapeutic Process in the Treatment of Schizophrenia.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1

Edward D. Hoedemaker. 'The Therapeutic Process in the Treatment of Schizophrenia.'

This is the report of a very long analysis of a woman patient suffering from paranoid schizophrenia. The essential point which the author makes is that the analytic process results in attitudes and opinions of the analyst being incorporated into a new superego structure within the patient. The work of Rosen and Wexler is referred to with respect to this concept of the development of the superego in chronic schizophrenic patients. Several examples from the analytic material to illustrate the process at work are given.

207

Rubinfine.  'On Obsessive Thoughts.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2: George Gero and David L. Rubinfine.  'On Obsessive Thoughts.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2

George Gero and David L. Rubinfine. 'On Obsessive Thoughts.'

The fear of hurting or killing a child is the symptom selected for examination here. This obsessional thought is the result of an unconscious masturbatory phantasy. In general the masturbatory phantasy and the temptation underlying the obsessive fear are unconscious, but this does not imply that patients suffering from such obsessive fears never experience masturbatory excitement or never become aware of certain aspects of the sexual phantasy. The essential point is that the connection between the phantasy and the sexual sensations has been disrupted. The history of a female patient is presented in which the content of the unconscious masturbatory phantasy is reconstructed. In it the patient identified herself with both parents. In this way she could play both male and female rôles with the aim of achieving the wish to hurt and to be hurt at the same time. The hurting or killing represented a sado-masochistic phantasy in a displaced and condensed form. The obsessive thoughts of the patient could be divided into two groups: (i) obsessive fears of harming herself, and (ii) the fear of harming others. These fears arose from the unconscious masturbatory phantasy, noted above, in the following way. The highly condensed phantasy in which the patient played the parts of both parents simultaneously was split into two components. The sadistic drives activated the fears of hurting women and children, while the masochistic drives determined the fears of injuring herself.

In male patients suffering from obsessional ideas unconscious feminine masochistic phantasies can be revealed during psycho-analytic treatment. The unconscious phantasy which lies at the root of such obsessive thoughts as the fear that the penis has been injured has a similar structure to that of the fear of killing a child seen in the female patient.

The authors devote the last part of the paper to some general considerations of obsessive symptoms and further, to the relationship between obsessive thoughts and compulsive acts.

'On the Psychoanalytic Theory of Anxiety:  A Statement of a Unitary Theory.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 3: Leo Rangell.  'On the Psychoanalytic Theory of Anxiety:  A Statement of a Unitary Theory.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 3

Leo Rangell. 'On the Psychoanalytic Theory of Anxiety: A Statement of a Unitary Theory.'

This paper has a threefold task—first, an evaluation of the present theoretical status of anxiety and its place in psycho-analytic metapsychology, the divergences which exist and a synthesis into a unitary theory; second, the integration of the concept of anxiety into the psycho-analytic theory of affects and into the recent developments in ego psychology; third, some remarks on anxiety in the therapeutic process.

The first section opens with a description of the two types of anxiety reaction which Freud distinguished: (a) anxiety as a signal, and (b) anxiety resulting from a traumatic factor. Recent psycho-analytic publications dealing with anxiety centre around two main themes—the nature of the 'actual neurosis' and the problem of anxiety in the earliest period of life. Having reviewed this literature the author postulates a unitary theory of anxiety which while consistent with the 'signal' hypothesis is applicable to all demonstrable instances of anxiety reactions. In states of mild or severe damming up of tension (traumatic situation) it is not anxiety which is automatically produced but the affect of unpleasure. Just as diffuse unpleasure is an automatic accompaniment of the tension and discharge of the traumatic state so is the anxiety, set off when the ego is confronted with danger, an automatic and inevitable psychobiological accompaniment. The anxiety in the latter case is used and responded to as a signal but it is not produced by the ego in the first instance.

The second section of the paper describes how anxiety is transformed from an 'all or nothing' response to a more modulated, controlled and effective reaction. This is accomplished by the development and structualization of affect discharge thresholds with the gradual attainment of ego control. If this ego control weakens a reversal of this process can occur and signal anxiety is converted into the traumatic state. Two further problems are raised in this part of the paper—first, the source of the energy necessary for the anxiety reaction and second, the relation between anxiety and the instinctual drives.

'The First Treasured Possession, a Study of the Part Played by Specially Loved Objects and Toys in the Lives of Certain Children.'  Pp. 199–217.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Olive Stevenson (Exeter), with a Preface by D. W. Winnicott, F.R.C.P. (London). 'The First Treasured Possession, a Study of the Part Played by Specially Loved Objects and Toys in the Lives of Certain Children.' Pp. 199–217.

Dr. Winnicott's paper 'Transitional Objects and Transitional Phenomena' (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 34, 1953) drew attention to the first 'other-than-me' object—blanket, woolly or soft toy, and suggested that further study might confirm its importance for clinical work and for the theory of early object relations. His preface to the present study notes that the phenomenon is related to the affectionate, secondary activities attendant on thumb sucking and fixes its normal genetic position between the infant's thumb and his first toy. 'The transitional object comes from the environment as we know, but it is essential to understand that from the infant's point of view it was created by the infant.'

Miss Stevenson quotes from some fifty examples which illustrate the empathy with which mothers regard this aspect of the child's growth. Some objects played an actual part in the earliest mother-child contacts, e.g. feeding bottles. Smell, texture, and visual appearance were varyingly esteemed from child to child. To some the 'treasure' represented a true transitional object, with others it was cathected in response to a traceable need or a disturbance of established object relationship, at a later level. Some examples suggest the child's own awareness of this distinction; the transitional object is given up because the child decides he is now too old for it, but retains similar toy objects, differently invested. In some cases the need for the object takes priority over the need for the mother in time of stress. Failure to cathect a transitional object may indicate that 'the basic relationships from which the child can move into transitional satisfaction are not being established'.

The special toy may persist or be readopted as a regressive defence. In ambivalent adults, the continued cathexis of a childhood toy is shown to have the quality of a defensive bridge where direct personal contact is feared.

214

The Interpretation of Dreams

By Sigmund Freud. An Entirely New Translation by James Strachey. (London: George Allen and Unwin Ltd., 1955. Pp. xxxii + 692. 21 s. net.)

M. Brierley

M. Brierley

M. Brierley  

Except for a single page of additional notes, this volume is a reprint of Vols. IV and V of the Standard Edition of the Works of Sigmund Freud (Hogarth Press, 1953), already reviewed in this Journal (1954, 35, 68). It thus enables readers who for one reason or another are not subscribers to the Standard Edition to benefit by James Strachey's new and first-class translation of the book that Freud himself regarded as his most important work, and by all the special features that make that edition so valuable a source of information relevant to the text itself. The Editor makes full acknowledgement to Anna Freud 'for her unfailing help and criticism at every stage of the work'.

responseSet.title: The Clinical Interview Vol. I, Diagnosis. Vol. II, Therapy: By Felix Deutsch and William F. Murphy.  (New York:  International Universities Press, 1955.  Pp. 613.  $10.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Clinical Interview Vol. I, Diagnosis. Vol. II, Therapy

By Felix Deutsch and William F. Murphy. (New York: International Universities Press, 1955. Pp. 613. $10.00.)

S. Yates

S. Yates

S. Yates  

These two volumes are, in my opinion, the only books that have made any real contribution to teaching the student how to develop a definite method in his approach to the psycho-neurotic patient, in both diagnostic explorative interviews and in psychotherapy.

Verbatim reports are given of clinical interviews with patients suffering from many types of psycho-neuroses, but psychosomatic syndromes are dealt with most fully. Psychiatrists resident in the Neurological hospital were present at these interviews, which are based on psycho-analytic principles, i.e. associative anamnesis, but guided by the doctor. Very useful, too, is the inclusion of the running commentary and interpretations going on in the doctor's mind but not given to the patient.

The volume on Therapy again gives verbatim recorded interviews showing the goal-limited psycho-therapy, in which one sector is chosen by the therapist—one symptom, or set of symptoms is taken and its relationship to figures in the past explored and linked up with figures in the present.

If these teachings are used as a guide by the beginner in psychotherapy, while he is developing his own technique, I would say that they are invaluable; if they are used in a stereotyped way the whole thing could become mechanistic and dead. The therapist would cease to practise an art and the patient could well use this technique as a resistance; this is seen clearly in the section 'weak feet'. The authors consider therapy 'as an intellectual process on the part of the therapist'—if it is only this the creative side, which to my mind is an integral part of psychotherapy, would vanish.

These books are so good because the interviews are far from mechanical, intellectual exercises. With these reservations in mind, this work can be very highly recommended. It should be in the library of every teaching hospital.

481

responseSet.documentRef: Thompson, A. G. (1956) Homosexuality: By D. J. West.  (London:  Duckworth, 1955.  Pp. xiii + 145.  15 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis  37:489
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Thompson, A. G. (1956) Homosexuality: By D. J. West. (London: Duckworth, 1955. Pp. xiii + 145. 15 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 37:489
responseSet.documentRefXML: Thompson, A. G. (1956) Homosexuality: By D. J. West. (London: Duckworth, 1955. Pp. xiii + 145. 15 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 37:489

responseSet.title: Homosexuality: By D. J. West.  (London:  Duckworth, 1955.  Pp. xiii + 145.  15 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Homosexuality

By D. J. West. (London: Duckworth, 1955. Pp. xiii + 145. 15 s.)

A. Thompson

A. Thompson

A. Thompson  

This book is evidently intended for the general reader. Dr. West's approach is sober, sensible, and well-informed, and his book may be recommended to anyone in search of a short and comprehensive account of the problems of male homosexuality in our society today. It is pleasantly written, and the tone throughout is that of the clinician who is discussing problems of which he clearly has extensive first-hand experience in his day-to-day professional work.

The book is divided into two parts. The first consists of a brief and readable account of the basic facts of male homosexuality considered from the anthropological, clinical, sociological, and legal points of view. In the second part the etiology, treatment, and prevention of homosexuality are discussed. After dealing with the question of the organic and hereditary factors, real or supposed, in the etiology of homosexuality, the author devotes a chapter to an account of psycho-analytical findings and hypotheses. Bearing in mind that the book is intended for the general reader and not for the psycho-analyst, this chapter, though over-simplifying many of the complex issues involved, gives a reasonable account of the relevant basic psycho-analytical formulations.

Dr. West's book should contribute greatly to dispelling the thick cloud of ignorance, fear, and prejudice which envelops the whole question of homosexuality in the public mind at the present time. It should be read by all those who are accustomed to express themselves on the subject with a vehemence unmatched by their knowledge or understanding, such as judges, magistrates, legislators, and writers of articles in the Press.

489

responseSet.title: Clinical Versus Statistical Prediction. A Theoretical Analysis and a Review of the Evidence: By Paul E. Meehl.  (Minneapolis:  University of Minneapolis Press, 1955.  Pp. 149.  $3.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Versus Statistical Prediction. A Theoretical Analysis and a Review of the Evidence

By Paul E. Meehl. (Minneapolis: University of Minneapolis Press, 1955. Pp. 149. $3.00.)

F. Taylor

F. Taylor

F. Taylor  

This book is a serious and thoughtful attempt by Prof. Meehl to consider impartially the pros and cons of an argument which has often been put in a garbled and barbed form through ignorance, intolerance, and partisanship. The argument concerns clinical predictions, and may be stated thus: Who is likely to make better predictions—the clinician who knows his patients through close and personal contact, or the statistician who has only actuarial data at his disposal, but knows how to arrange them in a mathematical formula that will provide a predictive answer?

Few people are qualified to consider both sides of this problem. Prof. Meehl, who describes himself as a 'hybrid working clinician and a rat psychologist', has the necessary qualifications, knowledge, and experience. Unfortunately, he seems to have lacked the time to tidy up the presentation of his thesis in this book; he has merely expanded a course of lectures. As a result, his book is discursive, repetitive, and often difficult to read. It is likely to scare away many would-be readers who would profit by a knowledge of his thesis.

I shall therefore try to give a short account of the essence of that thesis.

To begin with, we can postulate that the statistician and the clinician are to start with the same set of data, derived from case histories, interviews, test scores, and so on. They are, of course, free to disregard some data (i.e. to give them zero statistical weighting).

How then does the statistician manipulate his data to obtain a predictive answer? The data can be classified in various ways. For each classification actuarial tables can be constructed which will contain the frequencies with which different predictive answers occur. Finally, a statistical formula can be evolved which will reflect the contents of the actuarial tables. When all this has been done, the most likely prediction can be mechanically computed, with ease and speed, by any trained clerk. To give a concrete example, let us consider prognostic predictions in schizophrenia. Actuarial tables exist that contain the frequencies with which improvement or other changes in the clinical picture occur, and show how these are influenced by such factors as sex, age, type of onset, and so on. The predictive formula will give due weight to the various factors considered.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Mental Hospital. A Study of Institutional Participation in Psychiatric Illness and Treatment

By Alfred H. Stanton and Morris S. Schwartz. (New York: Basic Books, 1954. Pp. 492. $7.50.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

The two authors, a psychiatrist and a sociologist, both trained in psycho-analysis, present in this book the results of their joint studies at Chestnut Lodge, the well-known private psychiatric hospital where the treatment is mainly psycho-analytical. The numerous aspects of life and work in a mental hospital seen from the viewpoint of all those making up the institutional community have been analysed in great detail. Administrative psychiatry receives due attention, as well as everything which happens to the patient in the 'other twenty-three hours of the day'. The way in which the interpersonal and group relations among the staff affect the patients was the special study. The book contains a wealth of material which is of the greatest importance for everybody concerned with mental illness. For those working in psychiatric hospitals, it is indispensable. This work will no doubt serve as a model for future studies in other closed communities. The authors are to be congratulated on having produced a book which is an outstanding contribution to the psychiatric literature and promises to become a classic.

496

[[RunningHead]]

A Handbook of Hospital Psychiatry. A Practical Guide to Therapy

By Louis Linn. (New York: Universities Press, 1955. Pp. 560. $10.00.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

For everyone who works in a mental hospital, psychiatrists, nurses, psychologists, administrators, and auxiliaries, this book contains a wealth of information on the manifold aspects of life in these large communities of whose importance society is only just beginning to become aware. A book like this augurs well for the results of the reforms of psychiatric hospitals so urgently required. Dr. Linn's approach is catholic, and he attempts to deal with all types of treatment and problems arising in psychiatric hospital practice. It is this very comprehensiveness rather than preoccupation with details which makes this book so useful and stimulating.

496

responseSet.title: Disorders of Character: Persistent Enuresis, Juvenile Delinquency and Psychopathic Personality: By Joseph J. Michaels, M.D.  (Springfield, Illinois:  Charles C. Thomas Publ., 1955.  Pp. 148.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disorders of Character: Persistent Enuresis, Juvenile Delinquency and Psychopathic Personality

By Joseph J. Michaels, M.D. (Springfield, Illinois: Charles C. Thomas Publ., 1955. Pp. 148.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This book represents a synthesis of the author's studies on persistent enuresis, juvenile delinquency, and psychopathic personality published over eighteen years. He was impressed by the high incidence of enuresis among juvenile delinquents persisting beyond the age of twelve. The book reflects the changes in his assessment of the problem, in which he became interested in 1932. He regards persistent enuresis as a psychosomatic symptom of a biopsycho-social nature. He has found a strong indication for a constitutional factor apart from the triad of maleness, persistent enuresis, and psychopathic personality. He delimits a specific impulsive type of juvenile delinquents; their personality structure appears to lack maturity, the main characteristics being impulsiveness and selective lack of control. There are disturbances of object relations resulting from early unstable identifications. The ego is weak and has a low tolerance for anxiety. Superego formation is defective. Persistent enuresis in the juvenile delinquent indicates an unchanged perpetuation of an original urethral impulse influencing character formation. It reflects 'sphincter immorality' and foreshadows social immorality. The author stresses the need for closer studies of the significance of the urethral zone for character formation. Tentatively he expresses the view that impulsiveness as related to the urethral zone might form a counterpart to compulsiveness associated with the anal zone.

In elaborating the concept of persistent enuresis as a disorder of character Dr. Michaels suggests enlarging larging Freud's 'libidinal types' to include an 'impulsive character'. The inclusion of such a type would pay attention to the urethral zone and allow for the 'psychopathic personality' of clinical psychiatry being psycho-analytically characterized as the impulsive, urethrally rooted character type.

This is a most important comprehensive study of conditions very much in need of exploration. Everybody concerned with their treatment will profit by this book.

responseSet.title: The Origins and History of Consciousness: By Erich Neumann.  With a Foreword by C. G. Jung.  Translated from the German by R. F. C. Hull.  (London:  Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1954.  Pp. xxiv + 493.  30 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Origins and History of Consciousness

By Erich Neumann. With a Foreword by C. G. Jung. Translated from the German by R. F. C. Hull. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1954. Pp. xxiv + 493. 30 s.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

The German original of this volume first appeared in 1949, and the present translation embodies corrections and revisions made by the author. In his Introduction the author writes: 'It is the task of this book to show that a series of archetypes is a main constituent of mythology, that they stand in an organic relation to one another, and that their stadial succession determines the growth of consciousness. In the course of its ontogenetic development, the individual ego consciousness has to pass through the same archetypal stages which determined the evolution of consciousness in the life of humanity' (p. xvi). 'The individualized conscious man of our era is a late man, whose structure is built on early, pre-individual human stages from which individual consciousness has only detached itself step by step' (p. xx). Hence, Part I deals with 'The Mythological Stages in the Evolution of Consciousness' in three sections: A, 'The Creation Myth'; B, 'The Hero Myth'; C, 'The Transformation Myth'; and Part II with 'The Psychological Stages in the Development of Personality' in four sections: A, 'The Original Unity'; B, 'The Separation of the Systems'; C, 'The Balance and Crisis of Consciousness'; D, 'Centroversion and the Stages of Life'. There are two appendices, a bibliography, an index, and thirty-one illustrations. Since it is essentially a book for what one might term 'advanced Jungians', Jung's own opinion of it may be of general interest. In an appreciative Foreword he says: '… the present work opens at the very place where I unwittingly made landfall on the new continent long ago, namely the realm of matriarchal symbolism; and, as a conceptual framework for his discoveries the author uses a symbol whose significance first dawned on me in my recent writings on the psychology of alchemy: the uroboros. Upon this foundation he has succeeded in constructing a unique history of the evolution of consciousness, and at the same time in representing the body of myths as the phenomenology of this same evolution. In this way he arrives at conclusions and insights which are among the most important ever to be reached in this field' (pp. xiii-xiv).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Guide to Psychiatric Books. With Some Suggested Reading Lists

By Karl A. Menninger. (New York and London: Grune and Stratton, 1956. Pp. 157. Price $4.75.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This is the second edition of a book which has proved of great value for practising psychiatrists and research workers. Nowadays, when it has become quite impossible to keep up to date even in a limited field of psychiatry, a guide such as this is indispensable. The basic disciplines, including neurophysiology, neurology, psychology, and sociology, receive full consideration, more than one-third of the book being devoted to them. Psycho-analytic literature is well represented. The author has dropped the starring of the titles of works of seemingly outstanding significance, but he has remained both selective and catholic. He has taken care to include the classics, thus countering the widespread tendency to ignore them. Only books written in English have been included. While in the first edition there was only one basic reading list, the new edition has one for psychiatrists in training, one for medical practitioners, and one for non-medical people who encounter psychiatric problems, such as clergymen and others. The reading lists are well balanced and not too exacting. They will be welcomed with gratitude by all concerned, including the teachers of psychiatry. This Guide adds to the great debt of gratitude which modern psychiatry owes to the Menninger School and to Karl Menninger in particular.

501

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">S. Nacht</cs></ln><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">R. Diatkine</cs></ln><ln>J. Favreau</ln><ln>PARIS</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Ego in Perverse Relationships
responseSet.authorMast: S. Nacht,  R. Diatkine and  J. Favreau
responseSet.authorCitation: Nacht, S., Diatkine, R. & Favreau, J.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ego in Perverse Relationships11

S. Nacht , R. Diatkine and J. Favreau

J. Favreau

S. Nacht
R. Diatkine
J. Favreau
PARIS

J. Favreau  

In most of the work dealing with sexual perversion an attempt has been made to differentiate between the perverse structures of the neuroses and the psychoses. Early writings concentrated upon instinctual development, but subsequently attention was shifted to the functions of the ego. It is, however, difficult to describe a perverse structure common to all the patients suffering from sexual anomalies which we find in clinical work. Classically, every individual who cannot have an orgasm without an anomaly in his choice of object, or without distorting the sexual act, is considered sexually perverse, sexual aberration being the necessary or sufficient condition of gratification. Observations show that there are many cases which do not come within this strict definition, some because, although they are able to have normal sexual intercourse, they prefer an abnormal sexual act, others because they experience their perversions more in imagination than in reality, which brings them within the category of neurotic patients. Despite the variety of structures which must be borne in mind, we believe we have been able to discover a constant feature in the object relationships of these patients.

i. In all the categories we have in mind, the patients keep a certain distance between themselves and the object which is unconsciously sc cathected that they escape a constantly underlying fear but nevertheless maintain a certain possibility of erotic gratification.

ii. They succeed in eroticizing just those mechanisms of the ego which permit them to maintain this particular object relationship.

Eroticization of defence mechanisms or of anxiety with the consequent possibility of orgasm is the specific characteristic of sexual perverts, whereas in neurotics this eroticization, even when it exists, is never recognized as such by the ego and in no case leads to orgasm.

A few preliminary remarks appear to us to be necessary at this point:

i. Let us remember that perversion is never a liberation of instinct in its raw state but always a complex elaboration in which various aspects of the personality participate according to the past experiences of the individual.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 3: Joachim Flescher.  'A Dualistic Viewpoint on Anxiety.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 3

Joachim Flescher. 'A Dualistic Viewpoint on Anxiety.'

Flescher advances a theory of anxiety which is causally related to the fate of the aggressive drives. In his opinion the aggressive tendencies are always activated in situations of danger. Anxiety appears as a substitute for aggressive action provoked by dangers which may have either an internal or external origin. The intensity of the anxiety reaction will be in proportion to the degree of damming up of the aggressive drives. Even when the danger situation is caused by the sexual drives aggression is manifested. Flescher was led towards this new formulation by the difficulties which Freud's second theory of anxiety presents. These problems are listed at the beginning of the paper. The author's theory is called dualistic because instinctual and emotional manifestations usually appear as fusions of erotic and aggressive drives.

In the anxiety reaction itself traces of its aggressive origins can be detected. The content of clinical anxiety consistently points to repressed aggressive impulses. Animals subjected to frightening situations express behaviour which has either an offensive or defensive aim. The concepts of birth trauma, separation anxiety, castration anxiety and super-ego anxiety are individually examined in the light of the author's theory—for example, castration anxiety results from castration (aggressive) wishes which are projected on to the father by the boy and super-ego anxiety is seen as the outcome of the projection of death wishes against the parents and the fear of retaliation. Further confirmation of the theory is believed to be found in the writings of other analysts whose work is mentioned.

In the last pages of the paper Flescher discusses the possibility of whether or not his theory is wholly erroneous or is in fact a new solution to the problem of anxiety. He quotes from Civilization and Its Discontents to show that Freud later came to regard fear of authority (super-ego anxiety) as due not to the fear of loss of love but to a fear of punishment. Flescher suggests that his theory is nothing other than this formulation applied to the genetically earlier anxiety-arousing situations.

responseSet.title: Delinquent Boys. The Culture of the Gang: By Albert K. Cohen.  (Glencoe, Ill.:  The Free Press, 1955.  Pp. 202.  $3.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Delinquent Boys. The Culture of the Gang

By Albert K. Cohen. (Glencoe, Ill.: The Free Press, 1955. Pp. 202. $3.50.)

J. Jacobs

J. Jacobs

J. Jacobs  

This is a sociological study of delinquent youths and the gangs they form in the big American cities. The gang is seen as a working-class subculture, brought into being by the dominant middle-class culture and its pressures.

A general theory of subcultures is developed which seeks to integrate the study of the delinquent individual and his personality with enquiries into the origin and structure of the delinquent gang as a byproduct of American society.

Dr. Cohen contends that middle-class and working-class children in the United States grow up in markedly different homes, whilst their schooling is similar, if not identical. While the schools, on the whole, tend to reinforce the influence and standards existing in middle-class homes, they are often in conflict with those found in homes of the working-class. Many young delinquents are boys of unstable make-up with a working-class back-ground, and it is largely from them that recruits are drawn to the gangs in the big cities. The delinquent behaviour of gang members can be seen as an attempt by disaffected youths to achieve status and acceptance by their peers, as well as expressing their protest against authority.

The author concludes by stressing the importance of extensive further research, as an essential step towards the solution of a serious social problem.

491

Szasz.  'The Ego, the Body, and Pain.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2: Thomas S. Szasz.  'The Ego, the Body, and Pain.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2

Thomas S. Szasz. 'The Ego, the Body, and Pain.'

Szasz suggests that the scarcity of psycho-analytic studies on pain reflects the possibility that the theoretical framework of psycho-analysis does not allow of a simple integration of the phenomenon of pain into its structure. He proposes that the ego

207

must be considered in its relation not only to external objects but also to the body as an object. Pain must be regarded in the same way as are other affects. Just as anxiety is a danger signal warning of the possible loss of an object, so pain acts as a signal warning of the danger of loss of part or the whole of the body. To understand fully the experience of pain the concept of over-stimulation must be added to the danger of loss of bodily parts. Considered from the developmental point of view pain has characteristic features dependent upon the integrative level of the ego. During the earliest phase of the ego the affect experienced is a type of undifferentiated pain-anxiety. When the infant has achieved some degree of distinction between self and objects which facilitates the recognition of the body as an object, an awareness of the sensation of pain as distinct from the earlier pain-anxiety appears. Finally there is the experience of pain appropriate to the adult ego. The ego defences against pain have not been given attention commensurate with that given to the defences against anxiety. The principal defence against pain can be said to be that embodied in medicine, surgery, and anaesthesia. Medicine may be regarded as a socially structured defence against pain in much the same way as religion is a socially structured defence against anxiety.

Szasz continues with a discussion of varieties of pain—'organic', 'psychological', and 'experimental'. If pain is considered in terms of the relationship of the ego to the body the distinction between 'organic' and 'psychogenic' pain disappears. The terms 'organic' and 'psychogenic' do not refer to the pain experience, but describe the judgement of what the observer considers to be the source or the location of the pain. This analysis of the differences between 'organic' and 'psychogenic' pain parallels the distinction between objective and neurotic anxiety. The paper concludes with some comments on the available psychoanalytic literature on pain.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954: Anna Freud, LL.D. (London).  'Psychoanalysis and Education.'  Pp. 9–15.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Anna Freud, LL.D. (London). 'Psychoanalysis and Education.' Pp. 9–15.

This paper is itself an abstract of the Freud Memorial Lecture delivered at the New York Academy of Medicine in May 1954. At an all-day meeting of the New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute which followed, the Memorial Lecture was considered as a contribution to the Symposium on Problems of Infantile Neurosis in conjunction with papers read by Drs. Greenacre and Hartmann. It precedes the report of the Symposium and was discussed simultaneously.

In a detailed discussion of the conscious and unconscious motivations of the so-called 'rejecting mother', Miss Freud emphasized that in the period of need satisfaction, where the libidinal cathexis is attached not to the image of the object but to the experience of satisfaction and relief, frustration is inevitable. The infant suffers from delays in satisfaction and from quantitative differences between insatiable wishes and rationed satisfaction, as well as from the uncoupling under conditions of cultural nurture of innately coupled needs, such as the need for sleep and skin contact. These events were profoundly influential for the nature of the mother-child relationship, for ego structure, and for symptom formation.

By accepting the principles of delay and rationing involved in infant care, the ego adopts a cautious attitude to the id drives, makes an initial step towards the reality principle, and introduces into the personality the first break between principles governing ego and id. Somatic compliance may conceivably arise when the mental representative of a need or drive is weighed down by the intense unpleasure connected with it in the period of need satisfaction.

211

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis. Practical and Research Aspects

The Abraham Flexner Lectures. Series Number Twelve. By Willi Hoffer. (Published for Vanderbilt University. Baltimore: Williams and Wilkins; London: Bailliere, Tindall and Cox, 1955. Pp. 102. $3.50 or 27 s. 6 d.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

The Abraham Flexner Lectures were established at the Vanderbilt School of Medicine in honour of a man who rendered outstanding service to American medical research and education. The list of the lecturers chosen contains the names of some of the most illustrious physicians and scientists of our time. To be invited to deliver these lectures is one of the greatest honours American medicine can bestow. This is the first series of lectures devoted to psychiatry. The fact that a psycho-analyst was chosen, illustrates the high esteem in which psycho-analysis is held in American medicine. The editor of this journal is to be congratulated upon having been invited to act as the Abraham Flexner Lecturer for 1953. The lectures are addressed to medical students.

486

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference. Its Meaning and Function in Psychoanalytic Therapy

By Benjamin Wolstein. (New York: Grune and Stratton, 1954. Pp. 206. $5.00.)

Paula Heimann

Paula Heimann

Paula Heimann  

Scientific communication traditionally proceeds from the familiar to the new. This method is helpful to the reader because it is adapted to the principle of learning. In new perceptive acts the memory of old perceptions and conceptions is utilized. When comparison is invoked so that similarities and differences can be detected, the latter will be the more easily understood. A re-evaluation of established theories follows naturally, and this will be accepted or rejected on the basis of the reader's own experience and mode of thinking.

491

responseSet.title: The Voice of Neurosis: By Paul J. Moses.  (New York:  Grune & Stratton, 1954.  Pp. 131.  $4.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Voice of Neurosis

By Paul J. Moses. (New York: Grune & Stratton, 1954. Pp. 131. $4.00.)

J. Rowley

J. Rowley

J. Rowley  

This book will appeal immediately to anyone who is interested in personality make-up. Dr. Moses' attempt to analyse the component features of expressed speech is most praiseworthy, and his work in correlating varying changes in speech with underlying psychogenic conflicts is one which the psycho-analyst, who depends so much on speech communication, will find most absorbing. The author rightly stresses that it is time now to go beyond intuitive understanding of voice changes, and that there should be fuller knowledge of the meanings of speech variation in actual quantitative terms. Many examples of such increased knowledge are given.

With the part of the book that deals with therapy, however, the psycho-analyst will not be able to agree. The author's therapeutic approach is one in which a lack of real understanding of the unconscious is strangely, at first glance, at variance with his zeal for full analysis of the 'voice of neurosis'.

498

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Papers and Essays on Psycho-Analysis

By Karl Abraham. Edited by Hilda C. Abraham. Translated by H. C. Abraham and D. R. Ellison, with the assistance of Hilda Maas and Anna Hackel. Preface by Ernest Jones. The International Psycho-Analytical Library, No. 49. (London: Hogarth Press. 1955. Pp. 336. 25 s.; New York: Basic Books. 1956. $6.)

Miriam Williams

Miriam Williams

Miriam Williams  

In editing and publishing this second volume of Karl Abraham's papers, the author's daughter, Hilda Abraham, M.D., herself a psycho-analyst, has added a most valuable and long-awaited contribution to psycho-analytic literature.

The first volume of Abraham's selected papers was published in book form in English in this Library Series as far back as 1927—that is, two years after the author's death.

In his introductory memoir to the first volume, as well as in the Preface to the second, Ernest Jones refers to Karl Abraham's premature death as 'by far the most severe blow that has befallen the young science of psycho-analysis in the first thirty years of its development.' Abraham's work and what we know of him as a person and as a leader bears out this judgement, for he emerges as a man of the highest scientific and personal integrity. Karl Abraham has created for himself a unique place in the history and development of psycho-analysis, to which Freud makes frequent reference.

It is not only the subject matter which shows Abraham's originality, but the particular gift of presenting his investigations with great lucidity. In some of his writings he takes the reader through difficult territory like a careful and circumspect mountain-climber, which in fact he was. In his thorough way Abraham moves along, step by step, examining the terrain in order to gain stronger footholds. If the problem seems obscure, he directs his searchlight closer to it or, as the case may be, into the distance, relating the question under discussion to more remote ones. After a steep ascent of thoughts he may suddenly disclose a wide view of the inter-relationship of the problems involved. At all times the clarity of his thinking is an outstanding feature of his presentations.

The first volume contains Abraham's more important clinical work; the second gives us a vivid picture of the many-sidedness of his interests. These encompass fascinating analyses of the structure of personalities in the world of art and ancient history, as well as problems of religion and mythology.

responseSet.title: Beethoven and his Nephew: By Editha and Richard Sterba.  Translated by Willard R. Trask.  (New York:  Pantheon Books, 1954.  Pp. 351.  $5.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Beethoven and his Nephew

By Editha and Richard Sterba. Translated by Willard R. Trask. (New York: Pantheon Books, 1954. Pp. 351. $5.00.)

Hedwig Hoffer

Hedwig Hoffer

Hedwig Hoffer  

Beethoven's artistic life was a success from the very start and remained so until the end. In contrast,

507

his emotional life was exceedingly unhappy from earliest childhood until the last days of his life. Sterba's pathography does not attempt to explain the two sides or to correlate the work and the emotional life; it only collects and scrutinizes the data from the composer's last ten years and his day-to-day life with his nephew. The reason for the choice of this period and of the story of this relationship is evident. The existing documents would not allow of a full understanding of the relevant emotional facts of Beethoven's whole life. No psycho-analytically justifiable biography could be written on the basis of the existing documentation. Owing to Beethoven's deafness the last years appear to be best documented; the conversation-books are a rich source of knowledge, and in connexion with the numerous letters, descriptions and other documents preserved, a reconstruction is possible. The conversation-books contain not only the answers of Beethoven's companions to his remarks, but also many reflections and monologues in the composer's own handwriting.

The authors' skill in presenting their material demands respect. Their care and objectivity unfolds the whole tragedy of the relationship between Beethoven and his nephew step by step. Most likely the reader at large will be fascinated and horrified. Sterba's psycho-analytical comments guide him to a more or less complete understanding. The authors' honesty and sincerity does not permit them to venture on a more complete picture. It would be easy for the experienced clinician to attempt a complete psycho-analytical post-mortem, but the authors refrain from doing so. This was an unavoidable outcome for the psycho-analyst who respects the limitations of his science; but it provides the clinician only with an object lesson in such limitations. Relationships such as Beethoven's to his nephew are not too common, but many experienced analysts have seen something similar. The nephew's unsuccessful attempt at suicide is also no rarity; only the happy ending—that the young man developed so well later on—is unfortunately not very common. But our interest centres round the composer. His illness shows well-known features, and could be much better described as a clinical case from an analysed patient. The relation between gift and illness remains unexplained. The authors are well aware of this, and hope that further investigations of psycho-analytical ego-psychology will allow of more results. The reviewer does not share this optimism. Only a person whose gifts allowed him to undertake an extraordinarily subtle and extensive introspection might help us to answer the question of sublimation.

responseSet.title: Swift and Carroll. A Psychoanalytic Study of Two Lives: By Phyllis Greenacre, M.D.  (New York:  International Universities Press, 1955.  Pp. 306.  $5.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Swift and Carroll. A Psychoanalytic Study of Two Lives

By Phyllis Greenacre, M.D. (New York: International Universities Press, 1955. Pp. 306. $5.00.)

Charles Rycroft

Charles Rycroft

Charles Rycroft  

In her Introduction Dr. Greenacre tells us that she was originally led to undertake this study of Swift and Carroll by certain observations she made while analysing fetishists. 'I came to the conclusion, ' she writes, 'that fetishism, and perhaps other perversions of emotional development, occurred in people who were constitutionally active and strong, but subject at certain critical periods in early life to external stresses of a nature which upset the integrity of the self-perception and the assimilation of the sensations of their own bodies. In other words, the body image becomes impaired; and the fetishistic perversion is an (unconsciously determined) attempt to stabilize this in such a way as to increase the capacity to utilize inherent aggressive and sexual drives rather than to be prematurely destroyed by them. One way in which this impairment of secure self-awareness of the body appeared was in disturbed subjective sensations of changing size of the total body or of certain body parts. … When it occurred to me that the two great classics, Gulliver's Travels and Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, presented through Gulliver and Alice exactly those sensations of which my patients complained, I determined to study the life stories of the two authors to see the relation of their lives and characters to the production of these remarkable fantasies.' However, the significance of Swift and Carroll does not only depend on the fact that they elaborated into works of art phantasies which are based on sensations of changing body size; they were also satirists, parodists and writers of nonsense, while Swift was an active politician and Carroll a pioneer photographer. As a result Dr. Greenacre's initial impetus to study and exemplify the pathology of phantasies of changing body size has led her to produce a detailed characterological study of two highly complex psycho-pathological in nature and is intended and extraordinary individuals. This study is primarily psycho-pathological in nature and is intended for psycho-analysts and others well versed in analytical concepts. It makes no claim to be the sort of literary biography imbued with analytical insight which might have a popular appeal, and Dr. Greenacre has indeed very pertinent things to say about the hazards of psycho-analytical biography—and also about the evasive and futile nature of so much non-analytical biography.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1: Alberta B. Szalita-Pemow.  'The "Intuitive Process" and its Relation to Work with Schizophrenics.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 1

Alberta B. Szalita-Pemow. 'The "Intuitive Process" and its Relation to Work with Schizophrenics.'

The word 'intuition' is derived from the Latin verb intueor, which means to look closely at something, to look upon (mentally), to observe, to keep before the eyes, etc. In everyday speech the words 'see' and 'look' are more frequently used to denote what is meant by the word think than is the word 'think' itself. On this and other evidence, much of which is clinical, the author proposes the following hypotheses. (I) All thought stems from sensory perceptions, amongst which the visual predominate. (II) Sensory perceptions constantly combine into new forms. Study of the dream may provide the answer to the problem of how the mind has the capacity to form new combinations of old and new sensory perceptions. The stimulus for these new productions is always an affective one.

The intuitive experience has an element of suddenness, it erupts into consciousness in a 'ready for use' form. It differs from other thinking processes only with regard to the rapidity of the connections and in the availability of channels of communication. An ability to utilize what the author terms 'inner tools' endows the individual therapist with greater intuitive awareness.

The schizophrenic patient provides material which taxes the analyst to the uttermost. As the patient is in a sense dreaming though awake, his thinking being dominated by primary process mechanisms, this places a great strain upon the stability of the analyst's ego defences. The tendency to regress with the patient provokes great anxiety. The analyst must be able to tolerate this bizarre experience and try to translate the dream into terms of the patient's present reality. Only with the aid of intuitive under-standing can this be accomplished.

Excerpts from the treatment of a severely disturbed woman are given to support the theoretical views presented.

206

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Edith Jacobson, M.D. (New York). 'The Self and the Object World, Vicissitudes of their Infantile Cathexes and their Influence on Ideational and Affective Development.' Pp. 75–127.

In reviewing the processes of normal development during which cathexes of self and object with libidinous, aggressive, and neutralized energy are established in the course of structural differentiation, the author proposes to dispense with the concepts of primary narcissism and masochism in favour of new psycho-economical concepts which she summarizes as follows: 'We would visualize an initial psycho-economical state characterized by a low level of tension and by a general diffuse dispersion of as yet undifferentiated physiological energy, within the structurally also undifferentiated self. After birth part of the energy would be vested as a basic energetic stock in the nuclei of the future autonomous ego. Under the influence of external stimuli, the undifferentiated forces would then begin to develop into the libidinous and aggressive drives with which the id is endowed. During the embryonic and still predominantly during the earliest infantile stage most of this undifferentiated energy of the self is diffusely discharged in small amounts on the inside through merely physiological centripetal channels. But after birth the pregenital zones and in an increasing degree the whole sensory and motor system become periodically hypercathected and processes of drive discharge towards the outside begin to develop, which become observable as pregenital sexual activity, and in biologically pre-patterned, primitive affectomotor and instinctive reflex reactions, easily recognizable as the forerunners of feeling, thinking and functional motor responses.

212

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Rudolph Ekstein, Ph.D., and Judith Wallerstein, M.S.W. (Topeka). 'Observations on the Psychology of Borderline and Psychotic Children. Report from a Current Psychotherapy Research Project at Southard School.' Pp. 344–369.

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas  

This type of child is subject to marked fluctuations of ego states. Dramatic shifts can occur in treatment in which neurotic defences crumble and psychotic defences erupt into view without perceptible cause. What causes these shifts and what purpose do the fluctuations fulfil in the psychic economy? Three determinants are suggested: (i) they occur autistically and bear no relation to external stimuli; (ii) they reflect changes in the transference; (iii) they occur in response to inner feelings which may be related to changing introjects as yet unexpressed in the therapeutic context.

Fluctuations of ego state in response to transference reactions appear to serve the purpose of maintaining contact as well as indicating an inability to do so. 'Both the regressed mode of relationship and the communication content from the regressive ego state can be conceived as aimed at revealing to the therapist the ways in which the conflict is experienced in the depths of the child's personality by its most primitive and most cathected organization.' The child's underlying conception of the conflict as absolute and insoluble remains constant.

These fluctuations occur in the main outside conscious control. What is the nature of the mechanism of control which in contrast is available to the neurotic? 'The degree of conscious control which the ego exerts over it shifting states may be a function of ego structure and the relationship between the stages of (ego) development achieved. Thus where the different developmental levels are closely connected, as in the neurotic patient, we may think of the ego as having relatively full control over its fluctuations; whereas in our patients the lines of communication between the different ego states are tenuous and easily disrupted, and the ability to go back and forth at will is seriously impaired.'

Is there a relationship between the content of the conflict and the ego state in evidence? In this case it would be theoretically possible to chart the time of the occurrence of the original conflict by plotting the content of the conflict with the ego state.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sigmund Freud. Life and Work. Vol. II Years of Maturity, 1901–1919

By Ernest Jones. (London: Hogarth Press, 1955. Pp. 534. 30 s.)

M. Brierley

M. Brierley

M. Brierley  

This eagerly awaited second volume of Ernest Jones' biography of Freud covers the period from his forty-fifth to his sixty-third year. The Author's Preface opens with an apt summary-comment: 'The years here under discussion may fairly be called Freud's years of maturity. He had overcome any

478

personal inhibitions and corrected early mistakes. He had perfected the instruments of research he had devised and was now free to exploit them by exploring in detail the new world of knowledge they had opened to him—in a word, the Unconscious. The perplexities of youth were past and were succeeded by a greater serenity and a more critical judgement.'

This book is as encyclopaedic as the first volume and is an equally remarkable achievement. The author's task may have been lightened to some extent by the far more numerous data available and by his own friendship with Freud and intimate association with the development of psycho-analysis from 1908 onwards. But this wealth of material and first-hand knowledge must have furnished its own problems of selection, condensation, and mode of presentation. The 'skill, honesty, and candour' for which Ernest Jones has been justly praised are only equalled by the tact and respect for other people's feelings he displays in his account of the key personalities, critical events, and discussions of this period. His modest account of his own vigorous activities is a feat in itself. Thus, although he mentions his usefulness to the Vienna group as a window on the outer world, he in no way stresses the value of this service and soft-pedals his own pioneering work in English-speaking countries as an accompaniment to the central theme of Freud's life and work.

The book is organized on lines similar to the first volume, but is divided into three distinct Parts, which makes a clearer demarcation between life, work, and character and personality. The main text is preceded by a 'Chronology', a list of the main events, publications, and developments in order of occurrence, that brings together the elements considered more or less separately in the Parts.

responseSet.title: The Contribution of Psycho-Analysis to Forensic Psychiatry
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Contribution of Psycho-Analysis to Forensic Psychiatry11

Gregory Zilboorg

Gregory Zilboorg

Gregory Zilboorg
NEW YORK

Gregory Zilboorg  

It was quite inevitable that psycho-analysis should have sooner or later exerted its influence on the field of criminology as a whole, and on the courts of law in particular, and this for two reasons: (i) The problem of the psychological factors involved in a given crime is an old one. Medico-psychological experts have struggled with formalistic courts since the dark days of witch-hunting in the early sixteenth century, and psycho-analytic psychology could not help but influence the psychiatric expert sooner or later. (ii) Psycho-analysis very early showed its effective influence on the social sciences, and the social sciences became concomitantly more and more interested in criminology and penology.

But despite this influence the law itself—and by this I mean the penal codes and the court procedures in criminal cases—have remained relatively immune to psycho-analytic psychology. Here and there we can find exceptions, like the introduction of therapeutic instead of punitive measures in certain cases in Mexico, or the recent so-called Dewey Law governing the legal disposition of sex offenders in the State of New York. These exceptions are rare; moreover, in many cases they represent little psychological enlightenment.

The Dewey Law, for instance, recognizes the need for the therapy of sex offenders rather than the need for punishment—apparently a sign of true progress. According to the Dewey Law a so-called sex offender is given an indeterminate sentence; from one day to life imprisonment. Once the sentence has begun, the prisoner thus convicted is presumably to be treated, 'given psychotherapy' as the newest vernacular puts it. Depending upon the outcome of this psychotherapy, the prisoner might be cured of his criminal sex affliction (anything from exhibitionism or homosexuality to sexual assault would fall under this rubric) and set free, or if proved incurable he would be left in prison for the rest of his life.

It is quite interesting to note that neither prosecutors nor judges liked this law, because both were loath to take part in sentencing a man to a term which might prove life imprisonment. It seemed psychologically easier for a prosecutor to demand, and for the judge to grant, a definitive sentence of two, three, five, or ten years in prison, rather than to leave the matter in the hands of psychiatrists or fate, so to speak. It is not difficult to see that the prosecutors and the judges could better handle their own unconscious guilt, if the law set a definite limit on what it demanded. It does seem that prosecutors and judges, as far as their psycho-sociological role is concerned, would prefer to remain at least formally free from that sense of guilt which the primitive law as an expression of social revenge must generate in officers of the law.
