responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psycho-Logische Beitrage Vol. II, 1955, P. 56

Von Carl Muller-Braunschweig. 'Zur Menschlichen Grundhaltung, Psychologie und Technik der Psychoanalytischen Therapie.'

J. Aufreiter

J. Aufreiter

J. Aufreiter  

The first part of this paper deals with the basic attitudes necessary for the psycho-analyst's work: conviction of dynamic forces as existential data; respect for the individuality of the patient; recognition of the essential fact that man has the ability to direct his life and has an internal need to create future reality; conviction of the central power of Eros expressed in love and pleasure; and love of the analyst for reality, combined with an ability to keep this love at a distance.

The second part deals with transference and counter transference. The patient-analyst relationship is not only a projection from the past, but also a genuine experience of the patient. Counter-transference is compared to acting out. The basic rule demands first acceptance of every phenomenon, and secondly observation and communication of these phenomena and associations. This acceptance is based on ability to keep the phenomena at a distance.

The author concludes that the analyst's work is based on love for his patient and for the analytic activity and situation, and on the distance of recognition for this love combined with an ability to keep this love at a distance.

512

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Way to the Soul of the Mentally Ill

By Gertrud Schwing. Translated by R. Eckstein and B. H. Hall. (New York: International Universities Press. Pp. 158. Price $3.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This monograph, published in German in 1940, is an unpretentious record of Mrs. Schwing's experiences and observations in the psycho-analytically orientated nursing and treatment of a few chronic schizophrenics in the Vienna University Klinik. She was at the time receiving psycho-analytical training from the late Paul Federn, who also supervised her work at the Klinik. This book has since become a classic whose translation into English was long overdue. It preceded several other books on the psychoanalytical therapy of schizophrenics. Its orientation concerning the patient-therapist relationship, with its emphasis on motherliness, is similar to that of Mme. Sechehaye (1947), another Swiss worker in this field. Mrs. Schwing was one of the first to see the importance of the ward community for the psycho-therapeutic process. She is modest in assessing her results, which were very impressive, though not lasting. She deserves recognition and gratitude for an important piece of pioneer work.

The translation is excellent, but it would have been more correct and in keeping with the spirit of the book, which is free from mysticism, if Seele had been translated by mind.

501

On the Oral Basis of a Case of Male Homosexuality11

Alfred Winterstein

Alfred Winterstein

Alfred Winterstein, Dr.
VIENNA

Alfred Winterstein  

The patient who is to be the subject of my report—let us call him L—came under my treatment three years ago after having been analysed for two years by a colleague of mine who had to give him up on account of his emigration to the U.S.A. The patient, a chemical worker at the beginning of his thirties, was born in one of the countries neighbouring on Austria. He came in the course of the war to Vienna, where he studied chemistry against the will of his father, who was a farmer. The only member of L's family still living, apart from himself, is his much older unmarried sister who, as we shall see, played a most decisive part in the patient's life.

L is physically healthy, complains of inhibitions, feelings of inferiority and guilt, has not yet got out of the habit of masturbating, and suffers now and again from premature ejaculation. He can recall many memories of his childhood days and exhibits an unusually rich dream-life.

One of his earliest recollections is of his mother crying in the bedroom at night, which he most probably misunderstood. At the age of six he was in any case a witness of the primal scene. He made attempts to disturb his parents, complaining of an acute itching in his penis, at which his father said to his mother: 'We will cut off his penis for him.' At this period of his life the child entertained thoughts hostile to his father, whom the patient describes as being a brutal man who used to beat his wife in just the same way as he did his horses, gulped his food down and imposed upon himself not the slightest constraint with regard to anal functions. Once the father even threatened to shoot the patient's mother. L always sought protection from his father in his sister, who incited the boy against him. The patient revenged himself upon his father by ill-treating dogs which, in his eyes, represented the father.

L's original attitude towards his father, however, was different; this is shown by very early memories in which he remembered himself as sitting proudly on his father's knees or swinging excitedly upon his leg. L believes that, as a small child, he felt a great fondness towards him.

responseSet.title: Memorandum on the Electrical Treatment of War Neurotics (1920)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Memorandum on the Electrical Treatment of War Neurotics (1920)

Sigmund Freud

Sigmund Freud

Sigmund Freud  

Editorial Introduction

At the end of the first World War, after the break-up of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, many reports became current in Vienna that men suffering from war neuroses had been brutally treated by the army doctors. An enquiry into the matter was therefore instituted by the Austrian War Ministry, in the course of which Freud was called upon to give an expert opinion. He accordingly submitted a memorandum to the commission responsible for the enquiry, and subsequently appeared before them for oral examination. His memorandum was traced in the Archives of the War Ministry by Professor Josef Gicklhorn, of the University of Vienna, who has most generously put a photostat at our disposal. We are further indebted to Dr. K. R. Eissler, of New York (Secretary of the Sigmund Freud Archives), for having first drawn our attention to the document. The original MS occupies five and a half of the large foolscap sheets used habitually by Freud. The document is headed, in his handwriting: 'Gutachten über die elektrische Behandlung der Kriegsneurotiker von Prof. Dr. Sigm. Freud', and is dated by him: 'Wien, 23.2.20'. An official stamp at the top of the first page records that the memorandum was received by the 'Kommission zur Erhebung militärischer Pflichtverletzungen' (Commission for Enquiry into Violations of Military Duty) on 25 February, 1920. Each page also bears the official stamp of the State Archives.—The original German has not yet (1955) been published.

There were plenty of patients even in peacetime who, after traumas (that is, after frightening and dangerous experiences such as railway accidents, etc.) exhibited severe disturbances in their mental life and in their nervous activity, without physicians having reached an agreed judgement on these states. Some supposed that with such patients it was a question of severe injuries to the nervous system, similar to the haemorrhages and inflammations occurring in non-traumatic illnesses. And when anatomical examination failed to establish such processes, they nevertheless maintained their belief that finer changes in the tissues were the cause of the symptoms observed. They therefore classed these traumatic cases among the organic diseases. Other physicians maintained from the first that these states could only be regarded as functional disturbances, and that the nervous system remained anatomically intact. But medical opinion had long found difficulty in explaining how such severe disturbances of function could occur without any gross injury to the organ.

responseSet.title: A Contribution to the Problems of Female Sexuality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Contribution to the Problems of Female Sexuality11

Hilda Abraham

Hilda Abraham

Hilda C. Abraham
LONDON

Hilda Abraham  

Some four or five years ago a case was referred to me by the Department of Gynaecology of the hospital where I was then psychotherapist. It had been impossible to overcome the patient's fear of a vaginal examination, which had been attempted by several doctors at different times. That was the reason for her referral.

She had been married for ten years, yet her marriage had never been consummated because of her anxiety, which she had tried in vain to conquer. I shall later give some more details about her, as I found her story especially illuminating. Here I should like to report, first of all, that her case unexpectedly proved to be the first of a long series of similar ones, though the time which had elapsed in each case since marriage varied from two months to many years. I cannot go here into the method of treatment I had to evolve when faced with a constant demand to treat cases which obviously could not be put on a psychiatric waiting list. It is sufficient to say that psycho-analytical understanding helped me to deal successfully with the great majority of these patients. It should be added that, although the number of interviews had to be limited, I was in no case content to see the patient merely until consummation had been achieved. In most cases it was possible to help her to a satisfactory, or at least partly satisfactory, marital relationship. Where patients wished for and achieved pregnancy, they were seen throughout that time and during the puerperium, as it was assumed that women who had denied their femininity so strongly would need support in their adjustment to their maternal rôle. At the same time, this was regarded as a preventive measure which might protect the new-born from undergoing a similar fate.

The patient mentioned at the outset was a pleasant, tall and well-built woman of 39 who had, after much hesitation, decided to seek help since she wanted a child. Her own mother had died in giving birth to her and she had heard about her mother's tragic death in great detail from her maternal grandmother. Her early childhood was nevertheless a happy one. She lived with her father and was looked after by her mother's sister. Her father remarried when she was 3 ½, and from that time her life changed. Her stepmother was at first indifferent and, after she had given birth to several daughters, she became actively unkind to her stepdaughter, who slowly came to hate her. The patient left her home at the age of 18 to live with her grandmother and at 29 married a man of whom she was very fond. She was, however, frightened of intercourse, and after a time he became less pressing and finally lost interest. She came to ask for help on her own initiative. My first conclusion, which helped me to handle other cases of this kind, was that where several months had elapsed without the husband urging his wife to take action, the husband might be assumed to be a latent homosexual. In the case under discussion this was undoubtedly so, and my patient was deeply disappointed and hurt that, when she was ready and willing to have intercourse, her husband showed very little interest and tended to give up altogether. I have come to regard the frequent statement of such women: 'My husband is very good, he doesn't bother me' with a certain dismay.

responseSet.title: On the Teaching of Psycho-Analysis in Universities (1918)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Teaching of Psycho-Analysis in Universities (1918)

Sigmund Freud

Sigmund Freud

Sigmund Freud  

Editorial Introduction

This paper was first published in a Hungarian translation (probably made by Ferenczi) in the Budapest medical periodical Gyógyászat, 59 (13), 192, on March 30, 1919. No trace can be found of the original German text. The Hungarian title, 'Kell-e az egyetemen a psychoanalysist tanitani?', means literally: 'Should psycho-analysis be taught at the University?'. The paper seems to have been one of a series, by different writers, dealing with reforms in medical education. It is likely that Freud wrote it in the autumn of 1918, at about the time of the Fifth International Psycho-Analytical Congress in Budapest. There was then a considerable agitation among the medical students at Budapest for the inclusion of psycho-analysis in their curriculum. In March, 1919, when a Bolshevik government took temporary control in Hungary, Ferenczi was in fact installed as Professor of Psycho-Analysis at the University.—The rediscovery of the paper is due to the energy of Dr. Ludovico Rosenthal of Buenos Aires, and we are indebted to him for putting a photostat of the original publication at our disposal. The present translation from the Hungarian is based on one made by J. F. O'Donovan and Ludovico Rosenthal. It has been revised with the assistance of Dr. Michael Balint. Readers will understand that this is at best only a third-hand version of Freud's actual words.

The question of the advisability of teaching psycho-analysis in Universities may be considered from two points of view: that of psycho-analysis and that of the University.

1. The inclusion of psycho-analysis in the University curriculum would no doubt be regarded with satisfaction by every psycho-analyst. At the same time it is clear that the psycho-analyst can dispense entirely with the University without any loss to himself. For what he needs in the matter of theory can be obtained from the literature of the subject and, going more deeply, at the scientific meetings of the psycho-analytic societies as well as by personal contact with their more experienced members. As regards practical experience, apart from what he gains from his own personal analysis, he can acquire it by carrying out treatments, provided that he can get supervision and guidance from recognized psycho-analysts.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2: Frederic G. Worden.  'A Problem in Psycho-analytic Technique.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2

Frederic G. Worden. 'A Problem in Psycho-analytic Technique.'

The purpose of the case report contained in this paper is to draw attention to the pitfalls attendant upon alterations of the standard psycho-analytic technique. While changes from standard technique may appear justified at one stage of analysis with certain types of 'borderline' patient, further understanding of the case may reveal that the difficulties could have been resolved by correct interpretation without the utilization of non-interpretative measures. The patient reported on was able to make considerable progress only after certain interpretations were given when earlier non-interpretative intervention had failed.

209

responseSet.title: Truants from Life. The Rehabilitation of Emotionally Disturbed Children: By Bruno Bettelheim.  (Glencoe, Ill.:  The Free Press, 1955.  Pp. xvi + 511.  $6.00.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Truants from Life. The Rehabilitation of Emotionally Disturbed Children

By Bruno Bettelheim. (Glencoe, Ill.: The Free Press, 1955. Pp. xvi + 511. $6.00.)

Augusta Bonnard

Augusta Bonnard

Augusta Bonnard  

The reader of these detailed clinical studies of the

493

life history and excellent therapeutic handling of four gravely disturbed children cannot but feel full of admiration for Dr. Bettelheim and his devoted staff. Whether he be a child analyst, psychiatrist, paediatrician, or lay humanitarian, these studies provide the kind of professional knowledge and poignancy which should inspire concerted action. The total number in the world of residential institutions for gravely disturbed children in which therapy is available, other than that of humane and safe guardianship, is very small. Among these few, there are such rarities as the Orthogenic School, Chicago, directed by Dr. Bruno Bettelheim, a psycho-analyst. In describing the four cases, he not only provides instruction in psycho-pathology and psycho-analytic therapy, but also in the dynamics of running such a complex residential establishment. These range from matters such as architectural layout to the intricate psycho-dynamics of teamwork in regard to an individual child patient.

It cannot be too strongly emphasized how scarce is this kind of available information. For its nearest counterpart, we have to go back to Aichhorn's masterpiece Wayward Youth, and thence to the voicing of regret in the matter of the disservice caused by the incorrect use of titles, both in the case of Aichhorn's book and of Dr. Bettelheim's clinical material. The title Wayward Youth is not Aichhorn's but his translator's. It is a free, romanticized rendering of Verwahrloste Jugend, of which the nearest translation is 'Derelict Youth'. To be rendered derelict does not connote waywardness even when it should engender it. So it is that an unfortunate if compelling title misleads the English language reader into supposing that psychopathological conditions as varied as psychosis and traumatic neurosis, which may bring about social maladjustment, including delinquent acts, have been lumped together, instead of differentiated, by Aichhorn. By changing his title so beguilingly into an apparent euphemism for delinquency, we have lost understanding of the specific explanatory stamp or element characterizing a variety of cases and of psychopathologies. Despite the fact that only some of his examples are instances of dissociality, with or without delinquency, the book has come to be regarded and misapplied as if Aichhorn thought he was explaining a phenomenon, namely that of delinquency.

responseSet.title: Julia Mannheim 1895–1955
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Julia Mannheim 1895–1955

Barbara Lantos

Barbara Lantos

Barbara Lantos  

On 16 December 1955, Julia Mannheim, a member of the British Psycho-Analytical Society since 1944, died at the age of 60, working, in spite of a painful and malignant illness, almost until her last day.

Her death leaves us with the tragic note of a broken melody—a note which ran throughout her life. so often, on the verge of fulfilment, fate would step in with frustration and she would have to start afresh. This last time, when she was just ready to devote herself to creative work, after a rich experience in therapy and teaching, death intervened to prevent the full fruition of her professional capabilities.

Julia Mannheim's initial studies were languages, aesthetics, philosophy, and psychology. Since she showed a flair for teaching and an exceptional gift for expression, she became at an early age an Assistant Lecturer at the University of Budapest. This position she relinquished to join her future husband, Karl Mannheim, after he had had to leave Hungary. They married in 1921 at Heidelberg, where they soon became part of the cultural élite, in close friendship with such personalities as Friedrich Gundolf, the art historian, Emil Lederer, the economist, and Alfred Weber, the sociologist, all eminent representatives of European New Humanism. It was in keeping with this ideology that both Karl and Julia Mannheim turned gradually from aesthetics and philosophy towards sociology and education.

Karl Mannheim soon acquired fame as a sociologist, and in 1929 was called to the Chair of Sociology at Frankfurt University. Julia Mannheim joined in the work of the well-known paediatrician, H. Homburger, who, inspired by Freud, undertook research into child development with special regard to infantile sexuality. It was in his Child Guidance Clinic that Julia Mannheim took the step from philosophical and psychological interest in psycho-analysis to its practical application, and decided to concentrate on psycho-analysis as her life work.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1995, No. 1: Poul M. Faergeman.  'Fantasies of Menstruation in Men.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1995, No. 1

Poul M. Faergeman. 'Fantasies of Menstruation in Men.'

Psycho-analytic experience has shown that when a man bleeds he is unconsciously suffering castration. Faergeman maintains that he is not only castrated but also turned into a woman; if he bleeds he probably always does so in one or more of the ways in which a woman bleeds genitally. Manifest fantasies of menstruation, however, are probably associated with severe states of psychopathology.

202

responseSet.documentRef: (1956) The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 1: Phyllis Greenacre.  'The Mutual Adventures of Jonathan Swift and Lemuel Gulliver.'. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  37:202

'The Mutual Adventures of Jonathan Swift and Lemuel Gulliver.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 1: Phyllis Greenacre.  'The Mutual Adventures of Jonathan Swift and Lemuel Gulliver.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 1

Phyllis Greenacre. 'The Mutual Adventures of Jonathan Swift and Lemuel Gulliver.'

Greenacre is interested in distortions of the body image which she believes have a particular significance in fetishism. This paper is extracted from a book in preparation on Swift and Lewis Carroll. Here she reconstructs Swift's biography and early fantasies by comparing his own life with that of Gulliver. Though Swift was not an overt fetishist there was much in his personality which he shared with the fetishist. He fulfilled a fetishistic need by converting girls into boys in his fantasy, but he did not need an actual fetish because he resigned from physical genitality.

The Travels are largely the projection into activity of Gulliver's masturbation fantasies, which, like the character of Swift, are closly interwoven with anal problems and ambitions. The problems of changes in body size (based on phallic functioning) are characteristically reflected on to the total body, and much reinforced by the theme of the reversal of generations. There is much less substitution of different parts of the body for the phallus than is to be found in Alice's Adventures in Wonderland.

202

'A Unitary Hypothesis of Emotion.  1. Anxiety, Emotions of Displeasure, and Affective Disorders.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 1: Abram Blau.  'A Unitary Hypothesis of Emotion.  1. Anxiety, Emotions of Displeasure, and Affective Disorders.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 1

Abram Blau. 'A Unitary Hypothesis of Emotion. 1. Anxiety, Emotions of Displeasure, and Affective Disorders.'

The psychology of emotion has two facets: affect, which is the inner kinetic reaction, and emotional expression. The faculty of emotional expression is acquired environmentally.

Anxiety is the primary emotion of displeasure. Originally its function is defensive in the visceral sphere, the maintenance of homeostasis. Later it acquires a signalling function and calls forth the more highly organized secondary and tertiary emotions of displeasure.

The secondary emotions of displeasure are rage, fear, and depression, associated respectively with reactions of aggression, evasion, and submission. These reactions are acquired by learning patterns of behaviour to cope with various life situations. The tertiary emotions include guilt, shame, and disgust, and evolve with more complex integration of the personality, including development of the superego.

Affect as here defined is never unconscious. The author believes the emotional component of the affective disorders is to be related to that of actual anxiety neurosis.

202

Israel Zwerling. 'The Favourite Joke in Diagnostic and Therapeutic Interviewing.'

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

A favourite joke focuses, by denial, a 'favourite' anxiety. By asking a patient for his favourite joke an approach to a troubled area may be achieved with a light touch. Various social and other factors may reduce the diagnostic precision of this technique.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 2: Phyllis Greenacre.  '"It's My Own Invention":  A Special Screen Memory of Mr. Lewis Carroll, Its Form and History.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 2

Phyllis Greenacre. '"It's My Own Invention": A Special Screen Memory of Mr. Lewis Carroll, Its Form and History.'

The screen memory concerned is the White Knight's—described in his poem

'I'll tell thee everything I can:

There's little to relate.

I saw an aged, aged man

A-sitting on a gate.'

This is a screen memory within a screen memory (Alice's memory of the White Knight) within a dream, since the whole of Through the Looking Glass is a dream. According to Freud the inclusion of an incident in a dream within a dream is the most complete affirmation of the reality of that incident. Greenacre argues the same of a screen memory within a screen memory. The repetitive serial enclosure so characteristic of Carroll's work is the index of the force of the denied experiences. These are indicated by the form itself, which symbolizes the successive pregnancies undergone by his mother. Similar anxieties are also revealed by his preoccupation with changes in body size—they concern both pregnancy and the tumescence and detumescence of an excited father-figure in the primal scene with its influence upon him. The fixation on the girl child at the age of eight—the least sexual form of childhood, being a fused and castrated version of both sexes—served as the most efficient defence against the anxieties of the changing body forms, whether of the phallic boy or of the phallic (pregnant) mother.

203

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3: May E. Romm.  'The Unconscious Need to be an Only Child.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 3

May E. Romm. 'The Unconscious Need to be an Only Child.'

Certain children do not outgrow their wish to be an only child but use all sorts of techniques to perpetuate the fantasy of being one. These include demanding all the parents' attention, becoming the only child of an older sibling, adopting a younger

203

sibling in the same way and identifying with it, family romances, etc. Patients who make intensive use of such methods are strongly orally fixated. Their inability to outgrow a parasitic relationship with the mother suggests that their illness has taken root before or during the weaning period. Their omnipotent and magical thinking gives them unrealistic goals. They experience a helpless rage against their environment, which through guilt they turn against themselves, suffering severely emotionally and somatically.

204

'Homosexuality and the Paranoid Psychoses as Related to the Concept of Narcissism.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 4: David Grauer.  'Homosexuality and the Paranoid Psychoses as Related to the Concept of Narcissism.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 4

David Grauer. 'Homosexuality and the Paranoid Psychoses as Related to the Concept of Narcissism.'

The fact that homosexual features are not overt in the majority of paranoid and paranoid-schizophrenic states leads Grauer to question Freud's theory of the aetiology of paranoia, since he considers that such impulses might be expected to break through in view of the weakening of the ego. Freud's idea that the homosexual formations are restitution phenomena depends on his theory of a regression having taken place to a state of primary narcissism. Grauer finds primary narcissism a confused concept. Instead he equates 'narcissism' with Federn's concept of 'reflexive ego-libido', while he considers that Federn's 'medial ego-libido' contributes to the formation of the ego and to feelings of self-esteem. Like Federn and Weiss he regards the primary pathological process in paranoid psychoses as the loss of the cathexis of the ego. This, with the consequent loss of ego and object differentiation, explains sufficiently the prevalence of projection. When homosexual formations appear it is because the disintegration of the ego has disturbed the sexual identifications, so that the minor one is no longer integrated into the personality. Further, it would be in accord with the dream-like quality of psychosis if homosexuality were sometimes only the disguise and dramatization, for example, in a man of his basic need for dependence.

205

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 4: William V. Silverberg.  'Acting Out versus Insight.  A Problem in Psychoanalytic Technique.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 4

William V. Silverberg. 'Acting Out versus Insight. A Problem in Psychoanalytic Technique.'

The author discusses those patients who continue to act out in spite of an apparent insight into its meaning. It appears that they regard the insight as only half their own; it is also half the analyst's. They react to the interpretations as though they were a disciplinary measure on his part, which they equate with the sort of reasoning of an adult which aids the child's maturity but none the less deprives it of its toy. To understand how insight is unconsciously regarded is important in the therapy not only of 'acting out' cases but probably of 'unanalysable' cases in general. The author notes some similarities and differences between his views and Bergler's in the latter's discussion of the 'cynical phase' which Bergler maintains occurs towards the end of 'progressed cases' (Psa. Rev., 32, 1945).

205

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly 24, 1955, No. 4

Jacob Levine and Frederick C. Redlich. 'Failure to Understand Humour.'

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

205

Observation of patients who failed to see the joke in certain cartoons revealed that this occurred where the theme of the cartoon approximated to an important conflict of the patient. Often the failure operated by means of the denial of a perception. This is the mark of a scoptophilic conflict over the exposure of the impulse. Those people who are chronically humourless have too punitive a superego to allow themselves the pleasure of the temporary and controlled regression which humour involves.

206

Greenson.  'Forepleasure:  its Use for Defensive Purposes.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2: Ralph R. Greenson.  'Forepleasure:  its Use for Defensive Purposes.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 2

Ralph R. Greenson. 'Forepleasure: its Use for Defensive Purposes.'

The study of a patient's forepleasure behaviour reveals the predominant patterns of pre-genitality and their relation to end-pleasure, genitality and orgasm. Forepleasure may express not only instinctual needs but also defensive manoeuvres which are themselves directed against the instinctual drives. Greenson believes that forepleasure offers one of the richest opportunities for observing the interaction between instinctual drives and defences. After giving an account of what may be considered adequate criteria of normal forepleasure activities, certain typical patterns of forepleasure are described as pathological. First, lingering at forepleasure to the point that orgasm occurs prior to coitus; second, elimination of forepleasure activities; third, rigid and unchanging patterns of forepleasure.

Greenson subdivided his material into (a) aberrations in the relationship between forepleasure and endpleasure. There are patients who demonstrate an overemphasis on forepleasure. They are avoiding some specific anxiety about coitus. There are also patients who avoid forepleasure, for to them it represents pre-genitality or masturbation. (b) Aberrations in regard to the rôle of the sexual partner; there are many persons who use foreplay to obtain reassurance from the sexual partner. There are others who are unable to establish any sympathy with the sexual object. (c) Forepleasure can be a means of enacting unconscious phantasies.

209

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 3: Elizabeth R. Zetzel.  'The Concept of Anxiety in Relation to the Development of Psychoanalysis.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 3

Elizabeth R. Zetzel. 'The Concept of Anxiety in Relation to the Development of Psychoanalysis.'

The concept of anxiety is defined and is followed by the presentation of a number of problems amongst which are questions such as—to what extent is anxiety the result of heightened instinctual tension due to frustration and whether or not anxiety is primarily a tension phenomenon or a mode of discharge. The author then proceeds to review the development of Freud's views on the subject of anxiety. This is a comprehensive account of Freud's transition from his earlier physiological theory of anxiety to the later psychological one.

209

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954: 'Problems of Infantile Neurosis.  A Discussion.'  Pp. 16–71.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

'Problems of Infantile Neurosis. A Discussion.' Pp. 16–71.

This paper reports in full the Symposium referred to above. The meeting adhered in the main to the definition of 'Infantile Neurosis' used by Dr. Greenacre in her opening paper, viz. 'The inner structure of infantile development with or without manifest symptoms which forms the basis of a later neurosis.' From observations and reconstructions made in the analyses of borderline patients, Dr. Greenacre attributes specific importance to the interrelation between two types of observable rhythmic discharge, the even lulling (typified by sucking and breathing), and the orgastic type (typified by the hollow organs). Autoerotic activities pertain to both types, with a developmental shift towards combined rhythms (finger sucking with hair pulling) and an increase and sharpening of orgastic discharge probably influenced by neuromuscular development. At birth there is a discharge potential at all instinctual levels, though the levels have specific and divergent maturational speeds. Towards the end of the first year, all zones have achieved the capacity to respond in a special way and with varying forms and degrees of interrelationship. 'It is the maturational peak and its relative prominence in the total activity of the individual organism which marks the phase and the succession of the maturational peaks which creates the appearance of a succession of phases'. Infantile response will be influenced by type of stimulus and phase dominance. 'The infant probably tends towards a specific response if at all possible. … The proportion of mixture between dominant phase response and behaviour appropriate to the specific stimulus outside the phase must vary.

211

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954: Otto E. Sperling, M.D. (Brooklyn).  'An Imaginary Companion Representing a Pre-stage of the Superego.'  Pp. 252–258.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Otto E. Sperling, M.D. (Brooklyn). 'An Imaginary Companion Representing a Pre-stage of the Superego.' Pp. 252–258.

The function of an imaginary companion in a thirty-three-month-old boy is discussed. Early history and current observation showed a narcissistic clinging to illusions of omnipotence. The parents were united in their educational endeavours and have shown jealousy when the boy appealed elsewhere. The companion represented a point of appeal against parental omnipotence, without arousing jealousy. By giving magical powers to the illusory figure, the child retained an illusion of freedom, and the personified support without which he was still too young to operate.

The child's mode of operation with the companion sheds light on the process of superego unification. There was a widespread tendency from early days to deal with narcissistic threats by diverse primitive identifications with idealized objects, e.g. a dog and a train. Like the father, the companion reproached the child with these ideals. 'The father's jealousy is taken over into the introjected image and insists on a repression of other ego-ideals. … In the phallic phase or shortly thereafter there occurs that decisive introjection of the father (in other cases of the mother), which takes over the organization and unification of the superego, digesting previous acquisitions and assimilating later acquisitions.'

The child's tormented reaction to the tyranny of the companion, itself the outcome of projection, is comparable with the splitting off either of the pre-stages of the superego, or of id drives in paranoia, and offers further evidence that the anal stage which is significant for the production of the imaginary companion is a significant fixation point in paranoia. In the normal child the imaginary companion operates as a training in controlled illusions.

While the case confirms Freud's findings that the passing of the Oedipus complex is decisive for the formation of the superego, nevertheless identification here was not identification after the loss of an object (which may be less important for the establishment of the superego) but primary identification of a narcissistic nature.

Psychotherapeutic Intervention in Schizophrenia

By Lewis B. Hill. (London: Cambridge University Press; Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1955. Pp. vii + 216. 37 s. 6 d.)

H. Klein

H. Klein

H. Klein  

This book is addressed primarily to psycho-therapists working in mental hospitals. One difficulty inherent in the presentation is that, while the author advises would-be therapists to be analysed and hence implies that they have little or no emotional insight into unconscious processes, the book is written on the whole in psycho-analytic terminology and is derived largely from psycho-analytical concepts.

By Prof. J. H. Schultz. (Stuttgart: Georg Thieme, 1955. Pp. 215. DM. 24.)

Kritik der Psychosomatik (Criticism of Psycho-somatic Medicine). By H. H. Weitbrecht. (Stuttgart: Georg Thieme, 1955. Pp. 114. DM. 12.)

Zur Psychologie und Psychopathologie der Erinnerungen (Psychological and Psychopathological Studies of Remembering). By H.J. Wieck. (Stuttgart: Georg Thieme, 1955. Pp. 118. DM. 9.60.)

Professor Schultz, who is an eclectic of great erudition, attempts to arrive at a synthesis of the various psychological and physiological approaches to the study and treatment of the neuroses. For those not well versed in German psychology, psychiatry, and philosophy, the book will make heavy reading, all the more so as the author introduces a number of new terms, but it will be worth the effort. This is an interesting and stimulating work by a scholar who has devoted a lifetime to medical psychology.

Weitbrecht's penetrating critical appraisal of the present state of psycho-somatic medicine contains little that has not been said before, also by psycho-analysts. Admittedly the literature on psycho-somatic medicine must be very bewildering for the outsider, especially for the German reader who is used to finding in scientific journals only articles carefully sifted by the appropriate academical authorities. There is, in fact, much less dogmatism in psycho-analysis and psycho-somatic medicine than there appears to be to the author of this book. It is somewhat pathetic to read that 'clinical psychopathology' which dismisses the contribution of psycho-analysis is experiencing a new lease of life in criticizing the analytical schools.

Wieck's monograph is an essay on conventional phenomenological lines. The contributions of other schools to this vast subject are not considered, and the bibliography contains German names alone.

496

responseSet.title: The Practice of Dynamic Psychiatry.: By Jules H. Masserman.  (Philadelphia:  W. B. Saunders, 1955.  Pp. 790.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Practice of Dynamic Psychiatry.

By Jules H. Masserman. (Philadelphia: W. B. Saunders, 1955. Pp. 790.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This is a highly personal presentation which psychiatrists will find stimulating and thought-provoking. They will tend to overlook occasional inaccuracies, historical and other, and enjoy reading a book of unusual breadth written by a scholar to whom dynamic psychiatry owes a great deal. The author has in common with the late Paul Schilder catholicity of interests and the quest for philosophical foundations of psychiatry. One wishes only that he did not give the impression that the psychiatric millennium has all but arrived.

497

responseSet.documentRef: Lindsay, S. F. (1956) Heaven and Hell: By Aldous Huxley.  (London:  Chatto and Windus, 1956.  Pp. 88.  7 s. 6 d.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis  37:502-503
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Lindsay, S. F. (1956) Heaven and Hell: By Aldous Huxley. (London: Chatto and Windus, 1956. Pp. 88. 7 s. 6 d.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 37:502-503
responseSet.documentRefXML: Lindsay, S. F. (1956) Heaven and Hell: By Aldous Huxley. (London: Chatto and Windus, 1956. Pp. 88. 7 s. 6 d.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 37:502-503
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 195637502-503CHeaven and HellBy Aldous Huxley. (London: Chatto and Windus, 1956. Pp. 88. 7 s. 6 d.)S.F.Lindsay
responseSet.title: Heaven and Hell: By Aldous Huxley.  (London:  Chatto and Windus, 1956.  Pp. 88.  7 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Heaven and Hell

By Aldous Huxley. (London: Chatto and Windus, 1956. Pp. 88. 7 s. 6 d.)

S. Lindsay

S. Lindsay

S. Lindsay  

Huxley combines here specialized knowledge, intellectual wisdom, and literary skill. He describes

502

the use of mescalin, lysergic acid, the stroboscopic lamp, and hypnosis for artificially transporting the self to a region of the mind which he calls the antipodes, lying beyond the personal unconscious and the deeper collective unconscious. Here one sees wonderful geometrical forms, vast Gothic buildings set in lovely landscapes, heroic figures, fabulous animals, and precious stones—all in a galaxy of brilliant colour. These visionary experiences are reckoned to be on a par with the spontaneous visionary experiences of individuals under various conditions.

Such thrilling insight when acquired by taking mescalin may enrich one with gems and artistic beauties of which our earthly gems and works of art are but a poor reflection, but the method of acquisition reminds one of a smash-and-grab raid. The experience is seized rather than earned, by means of by-passing the more law-abiding subconscious and unconscious—surely an omnipotent short cut bearing little relation to science or the truly divine.

Huxley's speculations about the Hereafter are fascinating, but so are the results of inordinate curiosity in many people who unconsciously pry instead of waiting to know something in good time. He states honestly that if mescalin is taken on a tide of negative emotions such as fear, hatred, malice or lack of confidence and faith, the visionary experience may not be heavenly bliss, but very hell. Every psycho-analyst knows that these negative emotions may be quite unconscious, and so it seems that visitors to the antipodes must take pot luck.

The book has educational value so long as the reader is forewarned that it constitutes a temptation to taste something akin to a forbidden fruit, and that to follow the practice outlined therein is to gamble for a prize which may be hell, or at best a heaven similar to that experienced by a schizophrenic in his ecstatic phases.

503

responseSet.title: Psycho-Analysis and Criminology a Political Survey
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psycho-Analysis and Criminology a Political Survey11

Edward Glover

Edward Glover

Edward Glover
LONDON

Edward Glover  

In many branches of the International Psycho-Analytical Association, and certainly in the British Society up to the end of the second World War, a policy of rigid seclusion and isolationism vis-à-vis other branches of psychology and sociology was pursued with not a little esoteric zest. To be sure, psycho-analysts had always been ready to invade other territories with or without sanction, but they had never been disposed to encourage reciprocity in this respect. In any case, these were mainly armchair invasions. With the exception of some scattered essays in psycho-analytical field-anthropology, they did not involve any special knowledge of the data and technical methods of the cognate sciences concerned. They were in fact mainly forays in search of evidence confirmatory of psycho-analytic conclusions.

This isolation has been dictated in the main by the fear that too close intercommunication with other more superficial and less dynamic disciplines would lead to a watering down of psycho-analytical principles and a bowdlerization of psycho-analytical conclusions—a fear which, though no doubt defensive in origin, was not altogether unjustified. For after twenty-five years' experience of multi-disciplined research it is still my opinion that when psycho-analysis enters into co-operation with other sciences for purposes of research it ends by scaling down its standards to meet the necessities of the most superficial discipline represented in the team.

This self-protective attitude does not however account for the marked abstention of psycho-analysts from work in the criminological field. It is after all only in the past few years that criminology has had any claim to be called a science; even so it has as yet no coherent, integrated theory and such technical methods as it has pursued have never constituted a threat to psycho-analysis, although they have thrown into relief some of the limitations of pure psycho-analysis as a psycho-therapeutic system. I need hardly quote here the thoroughly deserved strictures passed by the ortho-psychiatrist and criminologist Ben Karpman (1) on the absence of organized work on criminology on the part of psycho-analysts, a criticism which has been very temperately expressed by Carroll (2) when he describes many psycho-analytical case studies of delinquency as 'partial reports of an intriguing case encountered in the course of a preoccupying practice'. From my own editorial experience I would have said that in Britain for one clinical paper offered on delinquency there are at least twenty written by social, educational and general psychologists; and, at that, the clinical paper is usually the work of a non-analytical psychiatrist, using frequently psycho-analytical concepts of the most dilute variety. The truth is that psycho-analysis has acquired much more prestige in criminology than is justified by the amount of actual work it has done in the field. Apart from a few pioneering studies and some fragmentary records mostly of non-criminal cases its influence is largely indirect, through the percolation to the field of delinquency of some metapsychological generalizations on infantile development, unconscious mechanisms and institutions, and unconsciously motivated behaviour.

responseSet.title: John Rodker 1894–1955
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

John Rodker 1894–1955

Marie Bonaparte

Marie Bonaparte

Marie Bonaparte  

I met John Rodker for the first time in 1939, in London, in the house of Freud, who had received so warm a welcome in England after being driven from Vienna by the entry of Hitler.

From the first I was attracted by his character, his charm, and his gentleness, which did not

199

exclude strength, and was so well expressed by his long, pale face. One felt in his clear, deep eyes the ardour with which he could devote himself to causes which seemed to him to deserve it.

John Rodker was born in Manchester on 18 September, 1894, and at the age of six was brought to London, where he went to school and at the same time helped his father, who kept a corset shop in the East End. He left school at the age of fourteen, but his thirst for knowledge led him to attend evening classes, at which he learned French and German, as well as the rudiments of science; he also became interested in the art of printing. On entering the Civil Service, his literary bent began to assert itself; he spent his spare time writing poems. During the first World War his pacifist convictions caused him to be a conscientious objector, a result of which was one of the works he has left us, Memoris of Other Fronts, a book full of vivid observation.

In 1914 appeared his Poems, and in 1920 his Hymns; in 1923 his novel Montagnes Russes appeared in French, with a eulogistic preface by Edmond Jaloux; this was followed in 1926 by Dartmoor and in 1929 by Trains and Adolphe 1920. This last was his favourite among his works, all of which, however, reflected a character which was both reserved and ardent, intelligent and gentle.

All his friends and admirers, both in England and in France, where he had so many, regretted that in the second part of his life he allowed this creative vein to run dry. He turned away from personal creativeness and devoted himself to publishing and making known the works of others. His breadth of view, his appreciation of others' talents, soon led him to publish works by James Joyce and T. S. Eliot; and Rodker, who loved the literature, the spirit, the air, and the wines of France, was an accurate and elegant translator. Thus he made known to the British public, among other works, the Chants de Maldoror of Lautréaumont and Henri Barbusse's Le Feu.

<artinfo arttype="ART" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ART" id="IJP.037.0265A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1956</artyear><artvol actual="37">37</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">265-269</artpgrg><arttitle>Some Psycho-Analytic Aspects of Biography<ftnx r="F001" type="PG">1</ftnx></arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Hitschmann, Edward" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Edward</nfirst><nlast>Hitschmann</nlast></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs style="AuthorName">Edward Hitschmann</cs></ln><ln>Dr. CAMBRIDGE, MASS.</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Some Psycho-Analytic Aspects of Biography
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Psycho-Analytic Aspects of Biography11

Edward Hitschmann

Edward Hitschmann

Edward Hitschmann
Dr. CAMBRIDGE, MASS.

Edward Hitschmann  

The developments of psycho-analysis and biography in the course of recent years have been such that, in a sense, the two have become identical. Psycho-analysis, together with anthropology, biology, and sociology, plays an essential part in the science of man. The unconscious processes of an individual are indispensable for the understanding of his character and personality. A therapeutic or training analysis attempts to bring to light all material from earliest infancy. The analysand is compelled to deliver his autobiography, so to speak; the analyst—I would coin a new word—is the biolistener. A complete analysis should not leave any gaps. After a few years, this biography of a patient or a candidate comes to an end, although speculations about the predictability of the future course of his life suggest themselves. While a scientific analysis has to go back to the earliest years of infancy and even to prenatal influences, this seemed impossible where biographies of great men are concerned. The study of the lives and works of such men does not carry with it the obligation to change them. But therapeutic and training analysis have this obligation. To paraphrase a statement by Karl Marx, who said: 'Philosophers merely give different interpretations of the world, but the real task is to change it, ' I would say: 'Psycho-analytic psychology can give different interpretations of a neurotic personality, but the real task is to change it.'

Creative imagination has always been a subject of the liveliest interest, and will be so in the future. Despite many contributions to the subject, the pessimism Freud expressed in his Leonardo da Vinci still appears justified. The main impression gathered so far is that an inner conflict of some kind is essential for this process. But it has not yet been proved that such a typical prerequisite actually exists. On the occasion of receiving the Goethe Prize, Freud admitted that 'We have not been very successful in the case of Goethe. (Wir haben es nicht weit gebracht.) This is attributable to the fact that as poet Goethe indulged in much self-revelation, but was also carefully reticent despite the fulness of autobiographical details.

responseSet.documentRef: Gombrich, E. H. (1956) Art and Visual Perception. A Psychology of the Creative Eye: By Rudolf Arnheim.  (London:  Faber and Faber, 1956.  Pp. x + 408.  63 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis  37:502

responseSet.documentRefXML: Gombrich, E. H. (1956) Art and Visual Perception. A Psychology of the Creative Eye: By Rudolf Arnheim. (London: Faber and Faber, 1956. Pp. x + 408. 63 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 37:502
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 195637502BArt and Visual Perception. A Psychology of the Creative EyeBy Rudolf Arnheim. (London: Faber and Faber, 1956. Pp. x + 408. 63 s.)E.H.Gombrich
responseSet.title: Art and Visual Perception. A Psychology of the Creative Eye: By Rudolf Arnheim.  (London:  Faber and Faber, 1956.  Pp. x + 408.  63 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Art and Visual Perception. A Psychology of the Creative Eye

By Rudolf Arnheim. (London: Faber and Faber, 1956. Pp. x + 408. 63 s.)

E. Gombrich

E. Gombrich

E. Gombrich  

A list of the chapter headings of this book may serve as the best indication of its scope and bias. They are: Balance, Shape, Form, Growth, Space, Light, Colour, Movement, Tension, and Expression. Professor Arnheim approaches the subject of art from the angle of the Gestalt theory, and the chapters concerned deal lucidly and persuasively with the principal tenets of this school. Whatever the ultimate fate of the Gestalt hypothesis may be, it has certainly drawn attention to phenomena which interest the artist: the organization of visual patterns, and the predisposition of the human mind towards simple configurations. A book such as this, which sets out, with a wealth of detail, the application of these laboratory observations to problems of art and art appreciation, is therefore most welcome. Perhaps its value would have been even greater if the author's ambitions had reached no further. Not that he overrates his own contribution; he is most anxious in the Preface not to give the impression of finality. But he seems less clear about the reasons why a 'psychology of the creative eye' can never take us very far. After all, it is not the eye (nor the visual cortex) which is creative in the real meaning of the term. Those who postulate a neuro-physiological basis for our bias towards simple and balanced shapes must recognize that there are other tendencies in us which delight in complexity, tension, and, most of all, the domination of chaos and its taming into harmonies. The author is aware of these conflicting tendencies. He is eloquent in his denunciation of the idea that any balanced configuration is ipso facto a work of art. Indeed he is critical of the theories of abstract painting which are oblivious of the need for 'content'. Unfortunately the author's theoretical equipment makes it hard for him to discuss what he means by 'content'. His remarks on Symbolism in general and on what he calls the Psycho-analytic Way in particular cannot be taken seriously: 'If after penetrating the work of a master we are left with nothing but references to organs and functions of the human body or to some close relative, we wonder what makes art such a universal and supposedly important creation of the human mind.' Does the author really think that psycho-analysts read sexual symbols and father-images bodily into all paintings, because Groddeck made some such remark about Rembrandt's 'Anatomy' and—even more unfortunately—about the 'Laocoön', the restorations of which his interpretation disregards? For a scholar who was on the staff of the New School of Central Research together with E. Kris such ignorance seems hardly possible. Yet that name, no less than those of most other serious writers on the subject, is not to be found in the Bibliography. No book on art needs to or indeed can discuss all aspects or theories—but in such matters discretion is really the better part of valour.

<artinfo arttype="ART" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ART" id="IJP.037.0036A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1956</artyear><artvol actual="37">37</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">36-45</artpgrg><arttitle>Character and Neurosis<ftnx r="F001" type="PG">1</ftnx></arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Nunberg, Herman" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Herman</nfirst><nlast>Nunberg</nlast><ndeg dgr="OTHER" other="M.D."/></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs style="AuthorName">Herman Nunberg, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>NEW YORK</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Character and Neurosis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Character and Neurosis11

Herman Nunberg M.D.

Herman Nunberg

Herman Nunberg, M.D.
NEW YORK

Herman Nunberg  

We see quite a number of patients who are free from neurotic symptoms but who behave pathologically. Such patients suffer from so-called character neuroses. They discharge their impulses, wishes, and fantasies in the external world, while symptom neurotics usually satisfy their drives through their own person. Character neurotics perform strange actions for which they sometimes find justifications, sometimes not. However, it is usually not possible to distinguish the rational from the irrational in their behaviour. Often it takes years before the irrational motivation is recognized; meanwhile they may have ruined themselves and their families. Whereas the symptom neurotic suffers from his illness and usually endures it passively, the character neurotic is hardly aware of being ill, and through his acts obtains gratification for his unconscious strivings.

Before we go further in our discussion, we must try to understand the nature of character. This is a difficult task, for character is an elusive phenomenon, not easily defined.

In evaluating a character one may perhaps rely on one's personal impressions and say: this man or that woman has a good or a bad character, a weak or a strong one. Such an opinion, however, will be wrong, at least as often as not. Or one may try to use external data as criteria of character, for instance, physical features only. Again one will soon be disappointed, for a masculine-looking woman may prove to be very feminine, a feminine-looking man masculine, and the like.

It is therefore necessary to look for more reliable criteria. Spread throughout Freud's writings are many remarks about the nature of character. Once, in a small circle, he offered a concise review of the problem, but, to my knowledge, never published it.

Character is a combination, or rather a synthesis, of many traits, habits, and attitudes of the ego. Sometimes one trait prevails, sometimes another. One may be tempted by this fact to evaluate a man's character on the basis of a single trait. Of course, this would not yield a true picture. In order to obtain a more accurate idea of an individual's character, we must consider it from several points of view—from the descriptive, genetic, structural, dynamic, economic, and libidinal angles. Consideration from each of these angles will lead to different results. The ideal picture of a character would be gained if it were possible to view all these aspects simultaneously. Then we should have a metapsychological conception of character.

responseSet.title: The Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud: Translated from the German under the General Editorship of James Strachey, in collaboration with Anna Freud, assisted by Alix Strachey and Alan Tyson.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud

Translated from the German under the General Editorship of James Strachey, in collaboration with Anna Freud, assisted by Alix Strachey and Alan Tyson.

M. Brierley

M. Brierley

M. Brierley  

Vol. X (1909). Two Case Histories ('Little Hans' and the 'Rat Man').

Vol. XIII (1913–1914). Totem and Taboo and Other Works.

Vol. XVIII (1920–1922). Beyond the Pleasure Principle, Group Psychology, and Other Works.

(London: Hogarth Press, 1955. Pp. vi + 342; xv + 264; vi + 295. £36 the set of 24 vols.; sold only in sets.)

James Strachey and his fellow-workers are greatly to be congratulated on the publication of three more volumes of the Standard Edition of Freud's works. All the special features of this edition which provide a wealth of information relevant to the texts are present in these volumes, e.g., the Editor's Notes, annotations and cross-references, Bibliography and Author Index, and General Index.

Whatever the headaches caused to librarians by the irregular numerical order of their appearance, the sequence of volumes to date appears to have some solid advantages for candidates in training and other new readers. One could hardly do better than begin with the 'corner stone of psycho-analysis', The Interpretation of Dreams (Vols. IV and V) and follow this with A Case of Hysteria and the Three Essays on Sexuality (VII). To read then the two crucial case-histories of 'Little Hans' and the 'Rat Man' (X) and the equally crucial developments in theory in Totem and Taboo (XIII) and Beyond the Pleasure Principle and Group Psychology (XVIII) is to provide oneself with a very sound foundation for the study of the remainder of Freud's earlier and all his later work.

Vol. X (1909), Two Case Histories ('Little Hans' and the 'Rat Man') contains an item of particular interest, namely a translation by Alix and James Strachey of Freud's original, hitherto unpublished, record of the first four months of his treatment of the 'Rat Man', presumably the 'notes' referred to by Freud in the text (n. 159). As James Strachey says, 'The record is remarkable in that it provides the only picture we have of the kind of raw material on which the whole of Freud's work rested and of the piecemeal manner in which that material came to light' (256). There is also a facsimile of a page of these notes in Freud's handwriting (facing 259). The Case History texts are not new versions but reprints of 1925 translations, considerably altered and amended. The Chronological Tables supplied for each history should help in keeping clearer the order of life-events. An entirely new map has been drawn to illustrate the complicated geography of the Rat Man 'Manoeuvres' (212). The Frontispiece is a portrait of Freud in 1909.

responseSet.title: Psycho-Analysis and Child Psychiatry: By Edward Glover, M.D.  (London:  Imago Publishing Co., 1953.  Pp. 42.  6 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psycho-Analysis and Child Psychiatry

By Edward Glover, M.D. (London: Imago Publishing Co., 1953. Pp. 42. 6 s.)

E. Anthony

E. Anthony

E. Anthony  

Dr. Glover's critical mind and lucid, persuasive style is once again put to good service in the cause of psycho-analytical education; here, it is the 'higher education' of the child psychiatrist. The pamphlet derives from the expansion of an article that first appeared in the Indian journal, Samiksa, and is clearly intended for the psycho-analytically-minded reader, since it breathes the confident, affirmative air that is inevitably coupled with preaching to the converted. Nevertheless, most child psychiatrists will find something of value between its covers, as the author is a 'constitutional didact' of a high order, and, in his account here of the mental apparatus and its functioning, he shows that he is still the best guide for beginners through the dark mazes of the metapsychological labyrinth. Much of the context is already familiar, but its containment within forty-two pages is a minor miracle of condensation.

Dr. Glover remains as passionate in his regard for scientific values, and is not very kind to the theorists in his field, who allow their intuitions to run away with them. In this respect he is not averse to the use of such epithets as 'pseudo-scientific' and 'psycho-biologically improbable' in describing the less earth-bound speculations of his colleagues. He has little difficulty, apparently, in steering a course between the 'absurdity' (sic) on one side of him, and the 'rigidity' (sic) on the other. As an 'independent member', he is possibly entitled to less cautious public statements on theory and therapy. His insistence that observational, and even interpretative, data should be submitted to rigorous statistical control would have been more welcome to scientific workers, had he devoted a section to a discussion of techniques for the quantification of the highly qualitative material that flows hourly from so many thousands of analytic couches. I am sure that we would all like to use these data in a manner more convincing to our exacting colleagues 'without the law'.

Edited by Warner Muensterberger and Sidney Axelrad. (New York: International Universities Press, 1955. Pp. 292. $6.00.)

E. Gostynski

E. Gostynski

E. Gostynski  

This annual is now published by the co-workers of Géza Róheim. It continues to be a forum for the discussion of psychological principles relevant to both sciences. The theoretical and sociological papers, by representative authors in these fields, offer formulations of dynamic processes underlying or governing the formation of groups, the interrelationships between group members and between group members and the leader. Particular attention is paid in the second part to the effects of success and of failure of leadership, and to the reciprocal manner in which leader and masses influence each other.

responseSet.title: Freud's Writings on Art
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Writings on Art

Louis Fraiberg

Louis Fraiberg

Louis Fraiberg
DETROIT

Louis Fraiberg  

I

Freud saw in art another manifestation—and a particularly attractive one—of the human spirit which he was engaged in studying scientifically. By making the same objective and painstaking approach to both he was able to enhance his knowledge of art and to use it for the benefit of psycho-analysis. he refused to speculate on the nature of the Beautiful, leaving this to the philosophers and confining himself, as a psycho-analyst, to what man perceives, imagines, and feels. To this day there is no fully developed psycho-analytic theory of aesthetics, although some analysts, notably Hanns Sachs, have put forth suggestions concerning the experiencing of a feeling of beauty which might conceivably form part of such a theory. What Freud did was to establish the principle that evaluations of art fall within the purview of psycho-analysis only as they reveal the psychic needs out of which art arises, the psychic materials which it uses, and the psychic purposes it serves.

Artistic productions, like novels or paintings, when looked at in this way, disclose a great deal about those experiences of the artist which have achieved aesthetic expression and about their communication through the artistic medium to others. Freud studied art as a record of the gifted; it translates events and the artist's reactions to them into forms by means of which they can be communicated symbolically. But he realized that it was more than simply a message in code which, when deciphered, would 'explain' art. He recognized that it actually has the property of preserving (in modified form) and creating repeatedly on demand an experience, or rather its artistic equivalent, which has afforded the author certain satisfactions and is capable of doing the same for an audience. Literature especially, since its medium, like that of psycho-analysis, is language, lends itself readily to scientific investigation without the necessity of constructing a new symbolic foundation. It was natural, therefore, that while Freud was also interested in other arts he freely and familiarly used literature and its auxiliaries, biography and criticism, to support and illustrate his new psychology.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. An Annual 9, 1954

Selma Fraiberg (Detroit). 'Tales of the Discovery of the Secret Treasure.' Pp. 218–241.

In the analysis of two boys, the search for buried treasure provided clues to the nature of their masturbatory phantasies. The hidden treasure had the meaning in one case of the baby and the father's penis 'left behind' in the mother, and in the other, with somewhat altered emphasis, the search for hidden objects became an anatomical search for the hidden phallus of the mother, The fairy tales of the Tinder Box, Ali Baba, and Aladdin represent symbolically the momentous discovery through accidental touching and observation of the 'magical secret' of the genitals and the resulting enlightenment about the hidden powers of the magician father. There is the childhood mystery of 'the place' where the treasure is hidden (access to the woman), and the child's belief that given the magician's lamp, the pirate's map, the knowledge of 'the place', he could outwit his opponent and win it for himself.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shame and Guilt. A Psycho-Analytic and Cultural Study

By Gerhart Piers and Milton B. Singer. Edited and with a foreword by Roy R. Grinker. (Springfield, Ill.: Charles C. Thomas; Oxford: Blackwell Scientific Publications, 1953. Pp. 86. $3.25 or 23 s. 6 d.)

L. Rubinstein

L. Rubinstein

L. Rubinstein  

This is the first psychiatric monograph in the American Lecture Series. It begins with Dr. Piers' study 'Shame and Guilt', which is of considerable theoretical and clinical interest. Developing Alexander's formulations of the phenomenological and dynamic differences between inferiority feelings and guilt feelings, Piers postulates a structural distinction as well. For this purpose he puts forward one of the recurrent attempts at stricter differentiation between the ego-ideal aspect of the superego and the superego proper. He uses the term superego 'exclusively as stemming from internalization of the punishing, restrictive aspects of the parental images', whereas the ego-ideal represents 'the sum of the positive identifications with the images of both the loving-reassuring and the narcissistically expecting parents'. In addition the ego-ideal, based on a core of narcissistic omnipotence, contains the later, more superficial, identifications as well as the conscious and unconscious awareness of the ego's potentialities. The main propositions are: '(1) Shame arises out of a tension between the ego and the ego-ideal, not between ego and superego as in guilt. (2) Whereas guilt is generated whenever a boundary (set by the superego) is touched or transgressed, shame occurs when a goal (presented by the ego-ideal) is not being reached. (3) The unconscious irrational threat implied in shame anxiety is abandonment, and not mutilation (castration) as in guilt.'

It is interesting to compare this thesis, which claims better potentialities as to maturation and progress for the 'shame-driven' as against the 'guilt-ridden' individual, with relevant formulations by other authors; e.g. Flugel's on the conflicts between 'the more progressive, enlightened aims of the more conscious levels of the ego-ideal and the more conservative taboo-like standards of the primitive levels of the superego', and Money-Kyrle's on the 'bad' (hated) and the 'good' (loved) aspects of the superego, and the progress from persecutory to more depressive guilt, from 'an authoritarian superego morality to an ego morality of predominantly humanistic kind'.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 4: Ives Hendrick.  'Professional Standards of the American Psychoanalytic Association.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 3, 1955, No. 4

Ives Hendrick. 'Professional Standards of the American Psychoanalytic Association.'

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman  

In this Presidential address, Dr. Hendrick describes the present situation of the American Psychoanalytic Association and reviews its past history. His description of the development of the Association, its training policy, and the present administrative machinery is detailed and comprehensive. There are at present eight standing committees and twenty-two ad hoc committees dealing with various aspects of the Association's commitments and responsibilities. The growth of the Association has been particularly rapid in the past decade. In 1930 the membership was 65; now in 1955 the membership is over 600, with 925 candidates at fourteen Institutes and three training centres.

Panel on Termination of Training Analysis

Rudolf Ekstein. 'Termination of the Training Analysis within the Framework of Present-day Institutes.'

Training analysis is particularly complicated by the fact that the training analyst participates in decisions regarding the candidate's progress. While various solutions have been offered with regard to this problem Ekstein believes that the only way is for the training analyst to analyse carefully the reality aspects of the situation. This reality function of the training analyst influences the transference situation—the candidate's feelings about his analyst are derived not only from genuine instinctual object wishes but also from the latter's potential influence on his future career. As some degree of identification with the training analyst is necessary for the candidate, especially in his early analytic work, it is impossible in the training analysis to resolve these identifications completely as would be the case with a patient. The view is expressed that a candidate's first psycho-analytic experience should be a thoroughgoing classical analysis. The principle of flexibility should be applied to the interpretative process only, and not to those constant factors which make flexible analysis possible.

Ekstein shows by means of a review of the literature that there is no simple solution to the problem of termination of the training analysis. A considerable responsibility lies on the shoulders of the training analyst. He must provide the candidate with a stable situation which allows for the maximum of analytic work. This will be easier if the training analyst is able to function smoothly within the training group. This will allow him to end the analysis taking into full consideration the various authoritarian pressures which are brought to bear upon the candidate during his training.

[[RunningHead]]

Texas Reports on Biology and Medicine XII, 1954, Pp. 692–737

Lawrence S. Kubie. 'The Pros and Cons of a New Profession: A Doctorate in Medical Psychology.'

The author makes critical use of official statistics on the rapidly growing school-age and adolescent population in America, on the increasing population in the older decades, on the projected future needs of the country for physicians and surgeons, and on the predictable future supply of medical students. From these data he calculates the discrepancy between the most conservative estimates of America's future need for practitioners of psychodiagnosis and psychotherapy and the most optimistic estimates of the future supply of medically trained psychiatrists. The author assumes further that for many decades the early use of psychodiagnosis and psychotherapy will continue to be our major preventive device. The conclusion is inescapable that the existing discrepancy is destined to become even larger. To fill this gap the author proposes that a new profession of doctors in medical psychology should be trained under the joint auspices of medical schools and universities, in close association with physicians, surgeons, and psychiatrists, to be licensed to practise psychodiagnosis and psychotherapy, but again only in clearly defined association with physicians. He views the development of such a profession as the only means of closing the gap; and urges that such a programme be launched in a series of small pilot tests, which should limit themselves at first to the training of teachers. The programme would begin to yield results for science and for the community as a whole in about twenty-five years.

In addition to these logistical arguments, the author presents philosophical and scientific reasons for believing that appropriately trained behavioural scientists who represent the various allied disciplines will make essential contributions to the science of psychiatry, as well as practical sociological and clinical contributions to the community.

responseSet.title: News, Notes and Comments
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

News, Notes and Comments

Sigmund Freud Centenary Celebrations

Reports on the Centenary Celebrations organized by the International Psycho-Analytical Association and by its Component Societies will, no doubt, figure prominently in the President's Report at the forthcoming 20th International Congress in Paris and will subsequently be published in the Bulletin. To avoid duplication no interim report will be given in these columns. The programme of the Centenary meetings and lectures has already been printed in this volume, pp. 115–116.

Publications on the Occasion of the Freud Centenary

Only a very few of the many publications dedicated to Freud and psycho-analysis have so far become known to the editor. Some of these should be recorded here.

Quite recently a volume has been published by Dr. Ernest Jones, Sigmund Freud—Four Centenary Addresses (New York: Basic Books, 1956; London: Tavistock Publications, 1956).

Two publications have reached us from the United States. The Freud Centenary Exhibit of the American Psychoanalytic Association is a descriptive catalogue for visitors to the Freud Centenary Exhibit at the Morrison Hotel, Chicago, and the New York Academy of Medicine. It contains many and rarely seen illustrations, among them facsimiles of documentary material on the life of Freud and the history of psycho-analysis. Dr. Bertram D. Lewin, chairman of the American Centenary Committee, and Dr. Naomi Katcher, editor of this absorbing production, are to be congratulated and thanked for it.

A monograph in the form of an attractive pocketsized volume by Dr. Jacob A. Arlow, The Legacy of Sigmund Freud (New York: International Universities Press, 1956), is another publication by one of the members of the American Centenary Committee. Dr. Arlow's assignment was 'to arrange a display of Freud's work directed to the general public rather than to the expert in psycho-analysis or in the history of science'. In this volume a 'representative sampling of Freud's work, organized according to the various departments of interest and correlated roughly with the different periods of his life, has been outlined'.
