responseSet.title: The Leaven of Love. A Development of the Psychoanalytic Theory and Technique of Sandor Ferenczi: By Izette de Forest.  (New York:  Harper and Brothers.  London:  Gollancz.  1954.  Pp. 206.  $3.50 or 15 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Leaven of Love. A Development of the Psychoanalytic Theory and Technique of Sandor Ferenczi

By Izette de Forest. (New York: Harper and Brothers. London: Gollancz. 1954. Pp. 206. $3.50 or 15 s.)

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones  

This book, written by one of Ferenczi's expatients, is a more than sympathetic account of his therapeutic outlook during the last ten years of his life. It is a dithyrambic, and in places religious, paean of the importance of love in the world, and particularly in the treatment of neurosis. As is well known, Ferenczi in those years went very far in his view that the analyst should display positive emotions to his patient and in that way compensate for any insufficient love in her childhood. Naturally this attitude often evoked profound gratitude on the part of the patient, and the present book appears to be a somewhat belated expression of it.

Mrs. de Forest's ardently expressed gospel of love and charity, however, has distinct limits in its application, and there is a note of strong resentment at the possibility of Freud not having treated his friend with the full meed of love he doubtless deserved. It is true that Freud did not go so far as Ferenczi along the therapeutic path mentioned, and once or twice he even warned him mildly against the practical risk of potential excess in the direction he was following. The author is not aware of the latter information, but that Freud was not wholeheartedly enthusiastic about Ferenczi's later technique is quite sufficient for her to construe this simple fact into accusations of 'Freud's increasingly harsh criticisms and disapproval', indeed his 'enmity', 'bitter criticism' and 'hostility'. Ferenczi, we are told, endured all this with a Christlike patience and devotion that saved the old friendship from a final break.

The truth of all this is quite otherwise. Freud's feelings for his friend never changed except in the one respect of regret at his withdrawal from him. When Ferenczi almost ceased writing to Freud and was evidently withdrawing into a self-absorbed isolation Freud was naturally distressed and saddened, and on one occasion uttered what Ferenczi in a letter referred to as a 'gentle reproach', adding 'to be quite frank, I was prepared for much worse'. This reproach about Ferenczi not having written to him for an unusually long time Mrs. de Forest thinks was a part of the 'harsh criticism' of his theories. So the only piece of evidence she can quote of her extraordinary view of Freud's imagined hostility is based on a misreading of a phrase and ignorance of its context.

responseSet.title: The Management of Addictions: Edited by Edward Podolsky.  (New York:  Philosophical Library, 1955.  Pp. xvii + 413.  $7.50.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Management of Addictions

Edited by Edward Podolsky. (New York: Philosophical Library, 1955. Pp. xvii + 413. $7.50.)

A. Williams

A. Williams

A. Williams  

This is rather a long book, consisting of a large number of papers dealing with widely different aspects of the management of addictions. As the word management implies, the main emphasis is on treatment, reference to aetiology and pathology being made mainly in their relationship to treatment.

By far the greater part of the book is devoted to alcoholism, drugs being relegated to about one-fifth of the whole. This is perhaps a fair emphasis in view of the fact that alcoholism is much more prevalent, and much more pressing as a social problem. Even so, the actual problem of drug addiction seems to be dealt with in less detail and less convincingly than is that of alcoholism. The individual papers are not interrelated, and though they do deal with different aspects of the subject, there is a good deal of repetition. Practical details are given of the various kinds of treatment of alcoholism and drug addiction. For this reason the book will be likely to find its most useful place as a light reference volume for psychiatrists who are confronted with the inevitable problems which arise during the treatment of alcoholics or drug addicts.

It is encouraging to find out so much that can be done in a positive way to treat alcoholics or drug addicts, even when long-term psycho-analytical treatment is not available. The opinion that alcoholics differ constitutionally from other people in their nutritional needs is put forward in a thought-provoking way. It is stated also that there is an endocrine abnormality in alcoholics, though whether this precedes or succeeds the alcoholism seems to be in some doubt.

Most of the book is of little direct interest to psycho-analysts, except those actually treating an alcoholic or an addict, though a long initial chapter by John W. Higgins gives a good summary of current psycho-analytical concepts of the psychopathology of addiction. The second paper by Katz views the problem with a psycho-biological slant which is well presented and worthy of attention. A short paper by Lolli outlines the problems of counter-transference in the treatment of addicts without actually using the term.

responseSet.title: The Technique of Psycho-Analysis: By Edward Glover, M.D.  (London:  Bailliere, Tindall and Cox, 1955.  Pp. viii + 404. 35 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Technique of Psycho-Analysis

By Edward Glover, M.D. (London: Bailliere, Tindall and Cox, 1955. Pp. viii + 404. 35 s.)

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer  

This is the best textbook-like presentation of the technique of psycho-analysis with which the present reviewer is acquainted. It is suitable for the beginner provided that he does not intend to remain such, and it is sufficiently advanced to be studied with interest and profit by the experienced practitioner of psycho-analysis. Moreover, it comes from a writer who has long been famed for his mastery of psycho-analytic theory and for his eloquence as well as his vigour of presentation. The reader who is eager to have a competent instructor or a refresher course with someone who understands how to keep his interest always at a high pitch will not be deterred by Glover's individual style, which as usual is richly spiced with critical comment and at times even passes into censoriousness.

This book is not the author's first attempt at presenting a coherent description of psycho-analytical technique: 'The first edition of The Technique of Psycho-Analysis consisted of a verbatim report of six lectures delivered as a training course at the London Institute of Psycho-Analysis in the days before systematic instruction in psycho-analytic technique was initiated'. In the new edition the chapter headings and most of the contents have remained the same, and this testifies to the fact that as far back as 1927 the author had already acquired a definite and lasting conception of the main aspects of psycho-analytical technique. Little can be found to be called outdated, and Ernest Jones' recommendation of the first edition still holds good: 'There are not many books absolutely indispensable to the practising analyst, but this is assuredly one of them.'

Much instruction will be found by the beginner and little to grumble about by the critic in the first two chapters of Part I (The Technique of Psycho-Analysis), which describe 'the analytic situation' and 'the opening phase'. The five chapters which follow describe the main phases of treatment as well as the principal variations of the standard technique. In Glover's description the 'defence-resistance' comprises such defensive mental activities as serve the maintenance of an anticathexis and thus operate as resistances. The analyst's intrapsychic reaction to the analytic situation was in the first edition examined under the heading 'Counter-transference and Resistance', and has now been dealt with still more searchingly under the heading 'Counter-resistance and Counter-transference'. Will the modern critics of 'orthodox psycho-analysis' please note that in 1927 the problems of counter-transference and the practical difficulties stemming from it were well in the mind of analysts and that counter-transference problems were being quite successfully handled even at that time.

responseSet.title: Clinical Essay Prize
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Essay Prize

Charles Rycroft

Charles Rycroft

Charles Rycroft, Honorary Scientific Secretary
Institute of Psycho-Analysis
63 New Cavendish Street
London, W.
 1.

Charles Rycroft  

Members and Associate Members of the International Psycho-Analytical Association are reminded that competitors for the Clinical Essay Prize must send in their work to the Hon. Scientific Secretary of the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, 63 New Cavendish Street, London, W.1, by 31 March of the year in which they wish to enter the competition.

The conditions governing the competition are the following:

A prize of (not exceeding) 30 guineas is offered.

Requirements for the Essay

The essay shall consist of a clinical record of a case investigated by psycho-analytical methods. It should illustrate clearly the events and changes in the mental life of the patient and their relation to external environment. In awarding the prize, the Judges will pay attention to acuity of observation and the clarity with which the facts are stated. If the writer wishes to draw theoretical conclusions, he must bear in mind the necessity of making the evidence for such conclusions carry conviction.

It is recommended that the length of the essay should not exceed 20, 000 words.

Date of Sending in Essays: Language: Format, etc.

Essays must be submitted on or before the 31st day of March in any year, in the English language. They must be typescript on quarto paper with ample left-hand margin. They must be in triplicate and be sent to the Hon. Scientific Secretary of the Institute. All copies of essays submitted become ipso facto the property of the Institute (or its successor) while it has the appointment of the Trustees for the Prize Fund.

No Award

If no essay of merit worthy of a prize is submitted in any year, no award shall be made for that year.

Joint Award

In the event of the Judges regarding the essays of two or more competitors as of equal merit, they may divide the prize money into equal parts and award it to such competitors jointly.

Eligibility

Any person of either sex, who is not a member or a past-member of the Board of the Institute, shall be eligible for the competition.

Tenure

The prize shall be given to the writer of the best essay in the opinion of the Judges submitted in any year, but the prize may be awarded to the same person twice, provided that he submits a second essay of sufficient merit in a later competition, and that the prize shall not be awarded more than twice to the same person.

responseSet.title: Beyond the Oral Stage
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Beyond the Oral Stage11

Arnaldo Rascovsky M.D.

Arnaldo Rascovsky

Arnaldo Rascovsky, M.D.
BUENOS AIRES

Arnaldo Rascovsky  

The stages that precede the oral organization may be studied through sleep and dreams, in certain psychoses such as Kanner's Infantile Autism (1), and in experiments in prenatal behaviour. In psycho-analytic research the material specially available consists in the regressions that appear upon intense frustrations or difficulties in oral working-through.

Owing to lack of time we cannot present the analysis of a patient where this material was particularly visible. The vicissitudes of her prenatal regression upon meeting with serious difficulties in working-through the oral situation led to a marked intensification of her exhibitionism and scoptophilia. She had adopted three fundamental solutions for facing her oral difficulties, to wit:

i. Regression to more primitive forms of prenatal incorporation connected with archaic visual perception of internal images.

ii. Oral acceptance dependent on the unconscious fantasy of a frustrating or toxic breast to which she remains fixated and which conditions her addiction to drugs and alcohol.

iii. Precocious passage from relationship with the breast to that with the penis with consequent enhancement of masculine identification, etc.

Besides the referred type of clinical material the study of Kanner's Infantile Autism through the analytic approach made by Margaret Mahler (2) and Rodrigué (3) and in our own cases was very useful. The fact stressed by Rodrigué, that the autistic ego was more integrated than the schizophrenic ego, depends on the regression to the prenatal ego, i.e. the autistic ego is prior to the birth trauma. The birth trauma provokes the dissociation of the ego and the consequent schizoparanoid position.

In mythology there is an interesting reference to the prenatal nucleus of the ego and the characteristics of its vision. Such is the legend of Ulysses and the giant Polyphemus, the Cyclops. In the legend of Polyphemus it is essential that Ulysses should blind the giant so as to escape from the cave. In this way the middle eye vision is superseded and Ulysses is able to evolve outwards and continue his Odyssey, which represents the essential vicissitudes inherent in human evolution.

responseSet.title: Sir Arthur George Tansley, F.R.S. 1871–1955
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sir Arthur George Tansley, F.R.S. 1871–1955

S. Payne

S. Payne

S. Payne  

Sir Arthur George Tansley, F.R.S., who died on 25 November, 1955, at Cambridge, at the age of 84, was a scientist of widely ranging interests. His main scientific interest and his original work was the study of communities of plants and animals, and he was the acknowledged founder of the science of ecology. He was Sherardian Professor of Botany from 1927 to 1937, and Chairman of the Nature Conservancy from 1949 to 1953. In 1927 he was elected a Fellow of Magdalen, Oxford, and an Hon. Fellow of Trinity College, Cambridge, in 1944.

This distinguished man, whose original work led him to recognize communities in plants and animals, a discovery which might be regarded as on the threshold of psychology, was one of the few scientists who acknowledged the significance of Freud's work as soon as it was published. In 1920 he made the experiment of interpreting Freudian theories in biological terms, and published a book called The New Psychology. Subsequently he went to Vienna and worked with Freud before becoming Sherardian Professor of Botany. On his return to England he was elected in 1924 an associate member and, in 1926, a member of the British Psycho-Analytical Society, and maintained an interest in psycho-analysis to the end of his life. He was unable to take an active part in psycho-analysis owing to the absorbing interest of his research work and his teaching at the Universities, but always gave his support when an occasion arose.

197

responseSet.title: Transference and Transference Neurosis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference and Transference Neurosis11

W. Hoffer M.D.

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer, M.D.
LONDON

W. Hoffer  

In this brief reflection on the summary report of our conscientious and impartial surveyor, Dr. Elizabeth Zetzel, I shall have to confine myself to the relation of transference and transference neurosis. Dr. Zetzel has already examined the historical development of the use of these terms and the meanings that have been given to them by the various schools or groups of psycho-analysts. The task which I have set myself will be just to bring to the fore such clinical applications of the terms as are easily neglected in a time of intensive demands on our clinical resources, on practitioners and teachers of psycho-analysis alike.

The term 'transference' refers to the generally agreed fact that people when entering into any form of object-relationship and using objects around them for instinct gratification and for protection against anxieties (as a defence) transfer upon their objects those images which they encountered in the course of previous infantile experiences, and experienced with pleasure or learned to avoid (pleasure-pain principle). The term 'transference', stressing an aspect of the influence our childhood has on our life as a whole, thus refers to those observations in which people in their contact with objects, which may be real or imaginary, objects, which may be real or imaginary, positive, negative or ambivalent, 'transfer' their memories of significant previous experiences and thus 'change the reality' of their objects, invest them with qualities from the past, judge them and try to make use of them in accordance with their own past.

The analytic situation is a variant of such human transference relationship. Who will deny the fact that there exists an object-relationship between patient and analyst and vice versa, and that it has proved to be a potent aspect of our professional reality? It comes to the fore in the 'therapeutic alliance', but it reaches far beyond this. The patient's transference, which is needed in order to begin the analytic work and to carry it through, 'exists from the beginning' (Freud, 1917) and is responded to and thus re-enacted by the analyst in many ways. The patient finds in him an object of his need for 'treatment', to be helped in an acute state of helplessness, to be cured, whatever this may mean to him at various stages of the treatment; he makes the analyst into an important object for himself; sooner or later, he turns him in many ways into a part of his psychic reality. And in return he receives from his analyst what can objectively be summarized under the heading 'humanity'. The patient will turn this into inner experiences according to patterns developed in childhood. The analyst re-enacts with the patient in the desire for a therapist—however unstable this may prove in the course of the treatment; a basic human need for security is taken very seriously and will always be respected, though this does not mean satisfied to a greater extent than is considered necessary for the day by day development of the analysis.

<artinfo lang="en" artsubtype="ART" j="IJP" artsource="A1V2" arttype="ART" id="IJP.037.0347A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1956</artyear><artvol actual="37">37</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">347-350</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">Repetition and Repetition Compulsion<ftnx r="F001" type="PG">1</ftnx></arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Toman, Walter" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Walter</nfirst><nlast>Toman</nlast></aut>

responseSet.title: Repetition and Repetition Compulsion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Repetition and Repetition Compulsion11

Walter Toman

Walter Toman

Walter Toman
VIENNA

Walter Toman  

It has been shown that ants trained to secure food at a certain place at a certain time of the day will come to that place at the exact hour. It could be demonstrated that their sense of time depended on metabolic processes. They were said to have a 'metabolism watch' (Stoff-wechseluhr). When fed a retarding drug (quinine) they came late. When fed an accelerating drug (thyroxin) they came early (1).

The higher animal and especially man shows similar phenomena. Man gets a little anxious when 'it is time' to eat or drink again, to smoke another cigarette, to stretch his legs, to have a chat for a change, or to make love. The difference is, however, that the higher animal and especially man has many more, and more complex, desires than the ant, and that getting a little anxious does not mean that something must be done about it. Man can appraise and calculate opportunity in a complex fashion.

Where does this sense of time come from? A simple assumption will help: Every desire increases steadily and continuously in intensity from zero, i.e. from the moment it has just been satisfied, to maximum intensity, i.e. to the moment when any further delay of satisfaction would bring about panic. Under ordinary conditions man manages to satisfy most of these desires long before they are as intense as that, and to keep them in mind and plan for them long before he actually cares to satisfy them. He manages to satisfy them when they are not too weak and not too strong, i.e. at the 'right' time. His sense for intensities of desires is his sense of time.

This implies that several desires are likely to be simultaneously active at any given time of a person's life, or waking life. Not only that. We may assume that, at a comparable point of development, more different desires are likely to be simultaneously active in man than in any other animal. We may finally assume that more different desires are likely to be simultaneously active at a given point of development, as compared with an earlier point of development, of a given person. In other words: the number of different desires that are likely to be simultaneously active in a person at any given time tends to increase with development.

responseSet.title: Report on my Studies in Paris and Berlin (1886)—Carried out with the Assistance of a Travelling Bursary Granted from the University Jubilee Fund (October, 1885—end of March, 1886)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Report on my Studies in Paris and Berlin (1886)—Carried out with the Assistance of a Travelling Bursary Granted from the University Jubilee Fund (October, 1885—end of March, 1886)

Sigmund Freud

Sigmund Freud

Sigmund Freud, Dr.
DOZENT IN NEUROPATHOLOGY AT THE UNIVERSITY OF VIENNA

Sigmund Freud  

Editorial Introduction

The circumstances in which Freud obtained a travelling bursary from Vienna University in 1885 are related in detail at the end of Chapter V in the first volume of Ernest Jones's biography of Freud. The grant, which was for 600 florins (worth at that time something under £50 or $250) and intended to cover a period of six months, was allotted by the College of Professors in the Faculty of Medicine; and to them he was expected to make a formal report on his return to Vienna. He is known to have spent about ten days in writing it and had finished it on 22 April, 1886. (Jones, Sigmund Freud, 1, 252.) On the initiative of the late Dr. Siegfried Bernfeld, this report was unearthed in the University Archives by Professor Josef Gicklhorn, and we are able to publish it for the first time, in a translation, through the kindness of Dr. K. R. Eissler, Secretary of the Sigmund Freud Archives in New York. The original, which remains in the Archives of the University of Vienna, consists of twelve manuscript sheets, of which the first contains only the title.

The high importance which Freud himself always attributed to his studies under Charcot is a matter of common knowledge. As Ernest Jones has said, Freud's experience at the Salpêtrière was a critical moment in his life. The following report marks it with the utmost clarity as a turning-point. When he arrived in Paris, his 'chosen concern' was with the anatomy of the nervous system; when he left, his mind was filled with the problems of hysteria and hypnotism. He had turned his back on neurology and was moving towards psychopathology. It would even be possible to assign a precise date to the change—in early December, 1885, when he ceased his work in the pathological laboratory of the Salpêtrière; but the inconvenient arrangements at that laboratory, which he himself puts forward as the explanation, were, of course, no more than a precipitating cause of the momentous shift in the direction of Freud's interests. Other and deeper factors were at work, and among them, no doubt, the great personal influence which Charcot evidently exercised on him. He expressed his sense of that influence most fully in the obituary which he wrote on his teacher's death a few years later, in 1893. Much, indeed, of what he says of Charcot in the present report found a place in his later study.

responseSet.title: Freud and the Future
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud and the Future11

Thomas Mann

Thomas Mann

Thomas Mann  

We are gathered here to do honour to a great scientist. And the question may very properly be raised: what justifies a man of letters in assuming the rôle of spokesman on such an occasion? Or, passing on the responsibility to the members of the learned society which chose him, why should they not have selected one of their own kind, a man of science, rather than an author, to celebrate in words the birthday of their master? For an author, my friends, is a man essentially not bent upon science, upon knowing, distinguishing, and analysing; he stands for simple creation, for doing and making, and thus may be the object of useful cognition, without, by his very nature, having any competence in it as subject. But is it, perhaps, that the author in his character as artist, and artist in the field of the intellect, is especially called to the celebration of feasts of the mind; that he is by nature more a man of feast-days than the scientist and man of knowledge? It is not for me to dispute such a view. It is true, the poet has understanding of the feasts of life, understanding even of life as a feast—and here I am just touching, very lightly for the moment, upon a theme that may become a main motif in the chorus of homage which we are to perform this evening. But it is more likely that the sponsors of this evening had something else in mind in their choice: that is to say, the solemn and novel confrontation of object and subject, of knowledge with the knower—a saturnalia, as it were, in which the knower and seer of dreams himself becomes, by our act of homage, the object of dreamlike penetration. And to such a position I could not object, either; particularly because it strikes a chord capable in the future of great symphonic development. It will recur, more clearly accented and fully instrumented. For, unless I am greatly mistaken, it is just this confrontation of object and subject, their mingling and identification, the resultant insight into the mysterious unity of ego and actuality, destiny and character, doing and happening, and thus into the mystery of reality as an operation of the psyche—it is just this confrontation that is the alpha and omega of all psycho-analytical knowledge.

responseSet.title: Re-Evaluation of the Process of Working Through
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Re-Evaluation of the Process of Working Through11

Phyllis Greenacre M.D.

Phyllis Greenacre

Phyllis Greenacre, M.D.
NEW YORK

Phyllis Greenacre  

The process of working through has held positions of varying importance and significance in the development of psycho-analytic therapy. Just at present one hears it referred to relatively little, and as a specific principle in technique it does not attract very much attention. Indeed, students rarely use the term, and sometimes seem uncertain as to its meaning. This may be due in part to the fact that the process of working through is now largely subsumed in other technical procedures. But there may also be some lessening in emphasis on its value. There are certain cases, however, and some situations in many cases in which a sound and thorough working through is essential for a sustained therapeutic result. A consideration of these is the topic for discussion at present.

In the early days of psycho-analytic treatment, when it was still within nodding distance of hypnotic therapy, the aims of therapy were especially the recovery of infantile traumatic memories and their abreaction through repetition, first in the hypnotic, subsequently in the psycho-analytic relationship. It was observed that the specific reliving of the disturbing experience relieved the associated conflict-bound emotional tension more than educative discussions concerning disturbed feelings could possibly do. Certainly in the re-living, the patient may more nearly admit the full emotional resonance to consciousness, whereas in discussions dealing with the disturbance in general or in incompletely specific terms, some degree of defensive distance may be maintained. In these early days, neurotic disturbances were thought to be due largely to actual experiences producing disturbing traumatic effects. In this setting, then, working through seemed to have two functions: first, it was a progressive and repetitive overcoming of resistances, which uncovered the repressed instinctual demands and showed their power to the patient; and second, it was a working to the supposed traumatic memories which were considered the nuclei of the neurosis. In his 1914 paper on Technique, Freud warned of its necessity and its arduousness (7).

responseSet.title: The Vocational Hazards of Psycho-Analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Vocational Hazards of Psycho-Analysis

Allen Wheelis M.D.

Allen Wheelis

Allen Wheelis, M.D.
SAN FRANCISCO, CAL.

Allen Wheelis  

INTRODUCTION

For some persons the choice of a career follows easily from the various inner and outer circumstances of their lives which have a bearing on the matter. For others, divided within and driven to find a vocation which will resolve an inner conflict, the decision is made with difficulty and is not elective. Often not one but many conflicts are involved. The more numerous the conflicts the more difficult the choice becomes, and the less likely is it that any one career can resolve them all. The choice proves successful when the vocation makes possible a partial sublimated discharge of the impulses and a corresponding reduction in the warding-off activities of the ego.

It comes about at times, however, that the very conflict which has led one into a certain profession is aggravated by the practice of that profession. The vocation misleads. It proves to be different in practice from what it was taken to be when viewed from the outside. The young man discovers only gradually that his vocation is not what he expected and, because of inner conflict, needed. A long time may elapse before he learns this, so long a time, in fact, that he may no longer be a young man, but at the midpoint of life and deeply committed. Indeed, he may never realize his mistake; for there are powerful forces opposed to such awareness if one has made certain crucial and irreversible decisions.

Many vocations are, in quality of experience, easily known: the nature of work in carpentry and chemistry, for example, may be correctly perceived by those whose acquaintance with these fields is relatively slight. There are a few vocations, however, which are truly knowable only after long experience. Those which mislead belong to this group. They have a quality which cannot be fully communicated in words. One has to find out for one's self. The most painful states of inner turmoil, the severest tests of integrity, arise in those professions which have these combined characteristics: of being truly knowable only from within; and of offering promise, when viewed from without, of alleviation of inner conflict—which promise is insidiously retracted by increasing proficiency in the field. Art is one such; the church is another; and, without implication that this completes the list, I suggest that psycho-analysis is a third.

responseSet.title: Barbara Low
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Barbara Low

Marjorie Franklin

Marjorie Franklin

Marjorie Franklin  

Miss Barbara Low died peacefully in her sleep on Christmas morning 1955, at the age of seventy-eight, at the home in Hampstead Garden Suburb which she had shared during her last two years of poor health and semiretirement with her sister Miss Florence Low. She was a founder-member of the London Association of Psycho-Analysis, bringing to the movement a vigorous trained mind, wide culture, clear thinking, and a notable power of exposition, and becoming well known both as a practising analyst and as a lecturer and interpreter of psycho-analytical thought. Her lectures and writings throughout her life appealed to a wide circle, particularly among teachers and students. She was a pioneer in calling attention to the influence of unconscious factors in education and in educators.

Within the British Psycho-Analytical Society she acted for some years as librarian at the Institute, and was a regular attendant at and frequent participant in discussions at the Society's meetings and at international congresses.

She made a notable contribution to theoretical formulation as the originator of the 'Nirvana Principle', to which Freud referred with approval, acknowledging the suggestion as Miss Low's.

Barbara Low was associated in close friendship and community of ideas with her sister, Mrs. Edith Eder, both of whom predeceased her by some years. It was Dr. Eder who first introduced her to the new science of psycho-analysis, of which he was a pioneer in Great Britain, and which, after training, became her central preoccupation. Her book, Psycho-Analysis: A Brief Account of the Freudian Theory, was first published in 1920 and passed through several editions. It appears to be one of the earliest descriptions in English written for the general public by a psycho-analyst.

Barbara Low had a strong and incisive personality, was a loyal and staunch friend, and a vigorous fighter for principles in which she believed. In recent years, since the British Psycho-Analytical Association has comprised within its membership adherents of two modes of analytical thought, Miss Low adhered firmly to the older concepts.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs style="AuthorName">Jeanne Lampl-De Groot, Dr.</cs></ln><ln>AMSTERDAM</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Role of Identification in Psycho-Analytic Procedure
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Identification in Psycho-Analytic Procedure11

Jeanne Lampl-De Groot

Jeanne Lampl-De Groot

Jeanne Lampl-De Groot, Dr.
AMSTERDAM

Jeanne Lampl-De Groot  

By the term 'identification' we denote a normal mental process applied by an individual with the tendency to become like another person. Identifications make use of the mechanism of 'introjection' (or 'incorporation'). The individual 'introjects' the image of the other person, he 'takes over' some or many of the latter's characteristics. In normal growth 'identifications' express themselves in different contexts during the various phases of maturation and development. In this short paper I have to limit myself to the description of only a few of them.

As identification is a form of relationship with an object, it can only manifest itself after some (primitive) differentiation between self and object has come into existence.

In Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego Freud defines identification as 'the earliest expression of an emotional bond with another person'.

In normal development the process of identification serves different aims. I will mention three of them:

i. Identification is one of the ways to secure the satisfaction of bodily and psychical needs, provided by the object.

ii. It finds a place in, and promotes, the process of learning. The little child acquires intellectual knowledge, it learns to speak, to read, to handle toys and tools, to test reality as well as to master emotional situations, and to adjust its instinctual life to the demands of the environment in large measure by identification with parents, siblings and other persons. All these learned skills may be called ego-achievements.

iii. Identifications form the base of the ego-ideal. In the pre-oedipal, magic phase of development the little child identifies itself with the images of omnipotent parents in order to feel as powerful as they are imagined to be.

In normal development the ego-ideal unfolds and widens its contents through new identifications with other persons, examples in social life, etc., a process continuing throughout the whole lifetime and constituting one of the normal outcomes of so-called narcissistic identifications.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Medical and Psychological Teamwork in the Care of the Chronically Ill

Edited by Molly Harrower. (Reprinted from Texas Reports on Biology and Medicine, Vol. 12, No. 3, Pp. 561–794. Fall, 1954.)

Margaret Little

Margaret Little

Margaret Little  

This book consists of the 'Reports and Discussions of a Conference held 28–31 March, 1954, at Galveston, Texas, under the auspices of the Josiah Macy Jr., Foundation of New York City, and the University of Texas Medical Branch'.

After some preliminary correspondence thirty men and women from the three disciplines of psychiatry, clinical psychology, and medicine (the last drawn from such varied specialities as bacteriology and pathology, pharmacology, rheumatology, neuro-surgery, and paediatrics) discussed a wide range of problems concerning chronic illness, with a view to developing to the fullest possible extent the various resources available. The 'common denominator' of the conference was defined at the outset by the Chairman, Dr. Frank Fremont Smith, as 'the needs of the patient', and the atmosphere of the discussions that of 'free-floating security'. On this basis the conference met in full session for the first day, divided into three study groups on the second, and joined up again into full session for the third and last day.

Reading the report gives an impression of repetitiveness and overlapping, but it is these very things that emphasize both the striking comprehensiveness of the discussions, the co-operativeness of the members of the conference, the extent and degree of their mutual understanding, and their unanimity.

In the first day's work the personal problems of chronically ill people were considered in their medical and psychological, social and economic aspects, emphasis being laid on the need to help the patient to live with and not by his illness, to adjust to it, and after due mourning for his lost health to become rehabilitated as far as possible.

Alongside of this the problems of those who diagnose, treat, and care for chronically ill people were also considered. It was not only the psychiatrists and psychologists who recognized that doctors, nurses, relatives, and people in the outside world may need patients to be ill for their own reasons, apart from any that the patients may have, so that any illness is far from existing in an emotional vacuum.

responseSet.title: Depression in the Schizophrenic
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Depression in the Schizophrenic11

Hanna Segal

Hanna Segal

Hanna Segal
LONDON

Hanna Segal  

The thesis of this paper is that, in the course of development, schizophrenics reach the depressive position and, finding it intolerable, deal with it by projecting their depressive anxieties. This can only be done by projecting a large part of their ego into an object, that is by projective identification. I am speaking here of the depressive position as described by Melanie Klein. Briefly, this is a phase of development in which the infant's ego is integrated enough and the object synthesized enough for the infant to experience a whole object relation involving ambivalence, dread of loss, guilt, and the urge to regain and restore the object. By projective identification I mean that process in which a part of the ego is split off and projected into an object with a consequent loss of that part to the ego, as well as an alteration in the perception of the object.

In the course of psycho-analytic treatment of the schizophrenic, it is of great importance to put him in touch with his depressive feelings and the wish to make reparation which springs from them. As the treatment progresses, and after some analysis of the paranoid anxieties, idealizing and splitting processes, the patient comes more and more frequently to experience, for a short time, depressive anxieties. He usually tries to get rid of these anxieties by projective identification. Very often it will be found that the depressive part of the patient's ego is projected into the analyst, and in order to achieve that projection the patient may resort to careful stage-managing of the analytical situation designed in a way to provoke depressive feelings in the analyst. It is of great importance, then, to find where and in what circumstances the part of his ego capable of experiencing depression has been projected and to interpret this to the patient.

I will illustrate what I mean by two examples taken from the analysis of a 16-year-old schizophrenic girl. She had suffered from hallucinations from the age of 4, or maybe earlier. She was an unusually gifted and intelligent child, and for a long time retained something of her original brilliance, but there had been a progressive withdrawal and a steady though slow deterioration of her personality, and at the time when she started treatment at the age of 16 she had a well-established, chronic hebephrenic schizophrenia.

responseSet.title: J. C. Flugel
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

J. C. Flugel

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones  

John Carl Flugel was born in London on 13 June, 1884, and died there on 17 August, 1955. His father was German, but with close connections in France, and his mother English, so Flugel had the advantage of growing up with a fluency in three languages. He was educated privately up to University level, the reason for this being that he was plagued by a congenital affliction of the feet, a hyperkeratotic variety of ichthyosis hystrix, which made walking painful and difficult and therefore excluded school life. His first contact with contemporaries was when he proceeded to Balliol College, Oxford, with an exhibition at the early age of seventeen. There his inquiringly original mind was soon evident, as was also a certain penchant for unconventionality. He deviated from the orthodox study of philosophy—he was reading Classical Moderations and Greats—by attending Gotch's lectures on psychology, took part in psychological experiments in the Museum laboratory, and deserted Plato for Darwin and Spencer. Worse than all that he studied the black art of hypnotism and conducted séances under the auspices of the Society for Psychical Research, of which he was already a member.

On leaving Oxford he went to Würzburg to study the new subject of experimental psychology under Professor Külpe, after which he returned to Oxford. There in 1908 he won the John Locke scholarship in Mental Philosophy, which at once marked him out. Soon after that he added to his Oxford degree a Doctorate of Science at the University of London. In the following year he was appointed Demonstrator in Psychology at University College, and he retained his connection with that institution till the day of his death. He became Senior Lecturer there in 1920 and Assistant Professor in 1929. After resigning the latter position in 1944 he continued to give courses as a special lecturer. For over twenty years he was in charge of the experimental work in the laboratory there, where his inspiring and helpful influence was invaluable to generations of students and research workers. He had a lucid and persuasive style in lecturing that enabled him to gain a hearing for new ideas to which at the time there was great opposition and made him an excellent teacher. His sympathy, tolerance, and wide knowledge made him popular with students drawn from all over the world.

responseSet.title: Symbolic Associations Following Subliminal Stimulation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Symbolic Associations Following Subliminal Stimulation

N. Dixon

N. Dixon

N. F. Dixon
DEPARTMENT OF PSYCHOLOGY, UNIVERSITY COLLEGE
LONDON

N. Dixon  

SUMMARY

From research into the effect upon verbal and autonomic behaviour of subliminal stimulation, evidence has come to light which suggests that verbal responses made during such stimulation tend to be associations to the material presented. The content of these associations, moreover, points to their being symbolic versions, in the Freudian sense, of the act or object connoted by the stimulus material.

It is this latter finding, from the above research, with which this paper is primarily concerned; a more detailed account of the methodology of the relevant experiments being reported elsewhere.

INTRODUCTION

In the present context and throughout what follows 'subliminal stimulation' refers to stimulation, either visual or auditory, of such low intensity that the subject remains unaware of both its nature and its presence. That such stimulation may determine behaviour, that the awareness threshold is not necessarily the physiological threshold, has been shown by several previous researches: Dunlap (1904); Bressler (1931); Baker (1937); Williams (1938); Miller (1940).

Evidence for experimentally induced symbolism in situations where the subject remains unaware of the exciting stimulus has also been forthcoming. Schroetter (1911) and Roffenstein (1924) induced their subjects to dream symbolic versions of tabu material related to them during hypnotic trance. Nachmansohn (1925), similarly, by post-hypnotic suggestion, influenced the ordinary night dreams of his subjects. In the course of experiments involving verbal responses to briefly exposed words Bruner1 and McGinnies1 have noticed that pre-recognition hypotheses frequently appeared to be associations, often of a symbolic kind, to the stimulus material being shown. Fisher (1954), also using the tachistoscopic method, has shown, as Poetzl (1917) did earlier, that that part of the display not consciously discriminated at the time of exposure often served a symbolic function in a subsequent dream.

Finally, Licklider1 has reported informally upon what he describes as 'Freudian influences', as a by-product of word articulation tests carried out at particularly low signal/noise ratios.

responseSet.title: The Communication of Primary Sensual Experience—(The Yell of Joy)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Communication of Primary Sensual Experience—(The Yell of Joy)11

Marion Milner

Marion Milner

Marion Milner
LONDON

Marion Milner  

The aim of this paper is to present some drawings from two patients of mine—a child, Ruth, and an adult, Miss A., both suffering from acute social anxieties. Ruth's drawings were made between the ages of 10 and 13, Miss A's when she was 28, Ruth's were all made during the sessions, Miss A's mostly between the sessions. Miss A. made over 4, 000 drawings during a period of about nine months, when she was in an extremely regressed state. She said she did not think while drawing what she was drawing.

In discussing the drawings I shall not be talking about the reparative aspect of them, but about the light they throw on the specific problem of how love and joy is to be expressed, communicated. I shall be talking about the interplay between the wish to communicate, to share feelings, and the strivings after primary narcissistic states; and how this interplay is shown in the drawings, in the specific fields, both of anal erotism and that part of oral erotism which has to do with the use of the voice and facial expression.

Both these patients made drawings showing something special happening to the body boundary; it is either blurred or multiple or radiant or luminous: for instance, a duck (Fig. 1) drawn by Miss A. and representing herself. Miss A. told me that she had, at the age of 20, experienced very intense sensations spreading over her body, which made her feel luminous and transparent. She said that other people noticed something happening in her and said she looked beautiful; she insisted that whatever it was that happened, it had nothing to do with sexuality. She also drew very many flower-like shapes radiating from a centre, the centre being occasionally a mouth, as in Fig. 2, or an eye. Although she described the luminous states as having to do with 'beauty', she seemed uncertain whether they sprang from love or hate. In fact she remembered saying at the time that love and hate were the same thing, but no-one had understood her. When I said that perhaps she had meant that the love and the hate oscillated so quickly that she could not distinguish them, she said 'Yes, of course I meant that.

responseSet.title: The Release of Internal Images
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Release of Internal Images11

Gustav Bychowski M.D.

Gustav Bychowski

Gustav Bychowski, M.D.
NEW YORK CITY

Gustav Bychowski  

In previous studies I have followed the vicissitudes of internal images within the ego, that is of images of original objects to which the child had been related in a diffuse hate-love relationship.

In particular I was concerned with parental images of certain individuals whose attitude toward their parents had, at that early stage of development, been characterized by deep ambivalence or rather, as I have called it, bipolarity. In these persons the incorporation of such images of original hate-love objects had resulted in a split within the ego along the lines of the bipolar cathexis.

In following the vicissitudes of internalized images I encountered on many occasions, within the mental system, situations calling for their externalization. I shall call the process in question the 'release' of the introjects.

I described this process at some length in the discussion of male homosexuality and of schizophrenia. I shall complete this description by a few illustrations from psycho-analytic observations of latent schizophrenics.

Patient A, a college student in his early twenties. Starting with his tenth year and long into his late adolescence, he used to beat the pillows in his bed furiously and then embrace them, throw spitballs at the wall, and fight imaginary armies. It appears that all these imaginary objects were substitutes for parental images, externalized out of his wish to fight it out with them and to grab them lovingly at the same time.

Yet A externalized his introjects also without any active participation of his ego: then they came to haunt him at night, he was tormented by the feeling of their invisible and frightening presence, and looked for them under the bed and behind the closed door of his bedroom.

Now in the course of his analysis A repeats the process of externalization in everyday life and in transference. On a dance floor, in a classroom, or in a restaurant he is constantly harassed and at the same time attracted by characters consisting of real individuals fused with internal images. Their position reflects deep ambivalence which he had experienced in his relationship with his parents, his older sister, and the playmates of his childhood: they are coveted, painfully longed for, and seemingly unattainable friends, as well as hated persecutors. A would like to attack some of the individuals he meets, smash their skulls and tear them apart. Yet, at the same time, he feels drawn toward them and would like to make love to them if they are women or to submit to their sexual embrace if they are men.

responseSet.title: The Closed Circle—An Early Image of Sexual Intercourse
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Closed Circle—An Early Image of Sexual Intercourse11

Hilde Lewinsky

Hilde Lewinsky

Hilde Lewinsky
NEW YORK

Hilde Lewinsky  

Psycho-analytic literature has always assigned an important place to infantile sexual theories occupied with birth and with the hidden and suspected activities of the parents. The infantile sexual phantasies are built in accordance with the currently predominant level of erotogenicity; or, earlier fixated impressions are used retroactively in the so-called 'understanding and explanation' of newly acquired factual knowledge.

Although the importance of the earliest, the oral, phase is universally accepted, there appears to be a scarcity of oral sexual 'theories' dealing with an exclusive mouth-on-mouth activity. Theories exist that father and mother kiss each other and thus make a child These theories must have been formed at a much later level and are expurgated and socialized. True early mouth-to-mouth phantasies must be scarce, as this type of activity is entirely unknown to the infant.

Combinations of oral and phallic (or genital) phantasies have been reported far more frequently in the analytic literature. The acting out of these phantasies in cunnilingus and fellatio occurs from a relatively early age onwards and continues through physiological maturity (35).

The use of the mouth (in foreplay and perversion) in connection with the genitals of the homo- or heterosexual partner is exceedingly widespread. (It belongs (e.g. in the U.S.A.) to the crimes against nature, punishable by law, though rarely prosecuted. Apart from the rational attitude with regard to procreation, this legal tabu may be due to a connection with incest.) What is the reason for this preference? Apart from rationalized motives, e.g. avoidance of pregnancy, it seems that the preference is due to a fixation to an early phantasy, an ideal image of sexual intercourse. I think that this image is founded on the first dim recognition that the breast does not belong to the infant. (This phase seems to correspond to one described by Eidelberg (8) in which the child would 'try to save his megalomania by attempting to play the role of the lover and the beloved …' Eidelberg, however, here speaks of a time when 'the wish is turned against the self.') The disappointed desire for unity and omnipotence, when the breast is found to be separate from the infant, is then soothed by another image, slightly nearer to reality. Yes, mother and child are separate, but each sucks the other and each gives to the other. Mother suckles the child and also sucks the child. The child does the same. The two are in a closed circle where the infantile penis or clitoris and the maternal nipple have the same functions, where milk becomes urine, urine becomes milk again, or where milk remains milk and flows in an 'unending stream' (2) through mother and child; a perpetuum mobile.

responseSet.title: Freud's Repression Concept—A Survey and Attempted Clarification
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Repression Concept—A Survey and Attempted Clarification11

Peter Madison

Peter Madison

Peter Madison
THE INSTITUTE OF THE PENNSYLVANIA HOSPITAL AND SWARTHMORE COLLEGE

Peter Madison  

SUMMARY

'Repression' as a term was applied by Freud to a variety of distinctive conceptions whose separate recognition in our thinking can add a needed clarity to contemporary usage. One can see these in the clearest way by seeing these different underlying repression conceptions in relation to the broader defence concept, which, in Freud's usage, was often synonymous with 'repression'.

'Defence' in its most general sense refers to all forms of ego-protection against the inner impulses of the person when these have a dangerous character to the individual because of his past experiences with the impulse. The defence concept is broader than Freud's own

80

development of it, since he limited himself to the study of only two more particular forms of defence.

In his early work, Freud restricted himself principally to the study of one general type of defence, those in which the ego-protection is achieved through an unawareness of some aspect of the impulse, or of its relation to the person.

In the later part of his career, he formulated a second general type of defence in which ego protection was achieved by emotional inhibition. Here the development of the emotion is prevented, whereas, in the first case, the development takes place—actual feelings exist—but these are misinterpreted through protective unawareness of some aspect of the impulse.

Thus, we have the broad defence idea and two general kinds of defence under this which Freud studied, one resting on misinterpretation through unawareness; the other on emotional inhibition, in which there is also a resulting unawareness, but unawareness is secondary to the inhibition.

'Repression' as a term was regularly used by Freud in four different senses. First it was a loose synonym for the general defence idea in its meaning of all forms of ego-protection against impulse, including those he himself did not study. Second, 'repression' was also used to refer to those two types of defence he did study. As applied to the misinterpretive or unawareness defences, 'repression' meant the rejection and keeping of some aspect of the impulse from consciousness. In this sense, isolation, undoing, reaction-formation, projection, substitution, reversal, conversion, and amnesia, etc., were particular means by which unawareness was achieved. They were, in this sense, particular forms of repression.

Thirdly, 'repression' was used to refer to defensive ego-protection through emotional inhibition.

Finally, 'repression' also had the very specific meaning of hysterical amnesia—of ego-protective unawareness achieved through forgetting.

responseSet.title: Notes on Symbolism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Notes on Symbolism

Emilio Rodrigué

Emilio Rodrigué

Emilio Rodrigué
BUENOS AIRES

Emilio Rodrigué  

INTRODUCTION

Susanne Langer (23), in her well-known book in which symbols are considered as the 'new key' to philosophy, wrote: 'The great contribution of Freud to the philosophy of mind has been the realization that human behaviour is not only a food-getting strategy, but is also a language; that every move is at the same time a gesture' (p. 51).

By this it is to be understood that human activity was found by Freud to have meaning. He discovered that the language of our needs is made up of unconscious phantasies. He provided the tools for discovering this semantic of the unconscious. In so doing he had quite forcibly struck on the 'new key' of contemporary thought. Langer adds: 'Certainly he has carried his theories far enough to make a philosophical study of "impractical" actions—rites, formalities, dramatizations, and above all, the unapplied arts—relevant and promising in the light of them. Yet few epistemologists have taken advantage of the new ideas that fairly cry to be explored' (23, p. 51).

This last criticism, to some extent, includes analysts as well. Symbolism has had a strange and disappointing fate in the development of psycho-analytical thought. At a time in which the social sciences were becoming 'symbol-conscious', analysts lost interest in the subject. After a promising start, about 1909–11, when the subject was tackled by Freud and the best analytical minds, the initial impetus faded away. After the far-reaching discovery that the same symbolic motifs which appeared in dreams and insanity were also present in art, religion, and folklore, later work mainly consisted in finding new symbols or in venturing alternative meanings for old ones. Practically no theoretical work has been done since then on the nature and function of symbols. In fact, the theory took its final shape in the surprisingly short period of less than ten years. Little has been added since Jones (16) wrote his comprehensive essay on 'The Theory of Symbolism'. This paper is now forty years old.

responseSet.title: Editorial: The Sigmund Freud Centenary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial: The Sigmund Freud Centenary

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer  

Celebrations will take place this year in honour of the hundredth anniversary of Sigmund Freud's birth. Delegates of the International Psycho-Analytical Association, led by the Officers of the Association, will meet at Freud's last residence in London on Sunday, 6 May, to witness the unveiling of the London County Council's Commemorative Plaque. This ceremony will be preceded and followed by scientific and social events arranged by the British Psycho-Analytical Society. On Saturday, 5 May, Dr. Heinz Hartmann, President of the International Psycho-Analytical Association, will deliver an address entitled 'The Development of Freud's Ego Psychology', and on Sunday, 6 May, Dr. Ernest Jones, Honorary President of the International Psycho-Analytical Association, will deliver the Centenary Address of the Association.

The London Celebrations will be preceded by functions in the United States, more particularly at the Convention of the American Psychoanalytic Association in Chicago, 26-29 April. Dr. Ernest Jones will attend this convention as the guest of the Association and will speak on 'Our Attitude to Greatness', preceded by Dr. Robert Waelder, who will speak on 'Freud and the History of Thought', and by Dr. Karl Menninger.

In compiling this Centenary Number of the Journal the Editor has not aimed at a series of contributions written for the occasion, but has made use of various opportunities to secure appropriate papers. The Number includes four brief but historically significant papers by Freud himself, not previously translated, which, to all intents and purposes, can be considered unknown. These were offered for pre-publication by Mr. James Strachey, General Editor of The Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud, with the consent of Miss Anna Freud, who also gave advice on several occasions. Mr. Alan Tyson and Mr. James Strachey offered for publication A Chronological Hand-List of Freud's Works. This supersedes the Bibliography, List of English Translations of Freud's Works, by James Strachey (published in this Journal in 1945), and is the fullest list of Freud's published writings that has yet appeared in print. Dr. Ernest Jones' paper, The Inception of 'Totem and Taboo', read at the last International Congress, has been included in the Centenary Number, and will therefore not be found in the forthcoming numbers containing the Congress Papers. Several other authors consented to delay the publication of their manuscripts to allow of their inclusion in this Number.

responseSet.title: The Transference in Symbolic Realization
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Transference in Symbolic Realization11

M. Sechehaye

M. Sechehaye

M. A. Sechehaye
GENEVA

M. Sechehaye  

I. Introduction

Although for a long time it was suspected that schizophrenics were incapable of transference, to-day analysts who treat these patients are unanimous in recognizing the possibility of a transference relationship in them. Federn was one of the first to show this.

Does this mean that the transference in the schizophrenic is identical with that in the neurotic? That it should be so is difficult to accept when we know the difficulty schizophrenics experience in maintaining real object relationships, relationships indispensable to the analytical situation. But before investigating what it is that differentiates these transferences, I should like to stress that in the treatment of schizophrenics, as in classical analysis, the transference is the analyst's most important instrument. And this remains the case no matter what the category of the patient being treated. There is no doubt, however, that there are individuals diagnosed as schizophrenics who, as Nacht has shown, are better regarded as schizoids with neurotic disturbances and in whom a transference similar to that in neurotics is found.

The patients to whom I shall refer in this paper are on the contrary individuals who are extremely severely regressed and whose ego has much deteriorated. Not only are interpersonal relationships in these patients non-existent, but intrapersonal relationships are deeply disturbed, that is to say they have lost the feeling of their existence, of their identity, and of their situation in the world. In their infancy repeated frustrations are almost always found; an early and severe maternal neglect. Important works of English and American writers on this subject, from Anna Freud to Greenacre, Fromm-Reichmann, Rosen and Spitz, to mention only a few, have been unanimous in underlining the connections which exist between these first experiences and the serious disturbances observed in the personality of schizophrenics: experiences which leave an almost indelible imprint on the psychic make-up of these individuals and which make their ultimate contact with reality fragile and inconstant. These patients have broken every tie with the frustrating primary object and have wished to destroy it in destroying themselves. In its place they have built up a wall of defences and compensations as solid as they are rigid. How, in such circumstances, could it be expected that such patients would immediately make a transference on to the analyst? On the contrary, deeply buried in their autistic dreams they do not notice the doctor's presence; they do not even distinguish him from the inanimate objects which surround them. It will therefore be for the psychotherapist to take the first steps, to make himself noticed by the patient by a resolved and sustained attitude, in short, to impose his presence on him.
